title: Androgen receptor amplification in mesonephric remnants
summary: Mesonephric remnants (MRs) are embryonic vestiges commonly found along the lateral cervical wall that can potentially give rise to rare mesonephric neoplasms. This case report employed next-generation sequencing to investigate the molecular basis of mesonephric remnants and identified amplification of the androgen receptor gene as a key genetic abnormality. The mesonephric duct development is governed by a highly regulated genetic program that has been extensively characterized in animal models. The androgen receptor amplification represents a significant molecular finding that may explain the pathophysiology of these remnant tissues and their potential to develop into neoplastic lesions. This discovery of androgen receptor involvement in mesonephric remnant biology provides new mechanistic insights into these rare embryologic structures and their neoplastic potential.

title: Embryological Insights into the Origin of Epithelial Cancers of the Female Reproductive Tract
journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Medicine
summary: This article clarifies the embryological origins of female reproductive tract epithelium, distinguishing between ovarian and Müllerian-derived tissues. The ovarian surface epithelium lacks embryological connection to the coelomic epithelium and does not originate from pluripotent cells capable of ready differentiation, establishing it as a distinct developmental lineage. In contrast, the Müllerian ducts give rise to multiple internal reproductive structures including the endometrium, endocervix, and fallopian tubes, which share a common embryological origin. Notably, the Müllerian ducts also share an embryological link with the renal collecting system, which may explain certain anatomical variations and pathologies affecting these structures. These embryological distinctions have important implications for understanding the differential susceptibility of reproductive tissues to malignant transformation and developmental dysfunction, including endometrial pathology.

title: Extraction and identification of exosomes from three different sources of human ovarian granulosa cells and analysis of their differential miRNA expression profiles
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study identified and characterized exosomes secreted by three distinct sources of human ovarian granulosa cells—immortalized SVOG and KGN cell lines, and primary granulosa cells (PGC)—finding that all three sources actively secrete exosomes with differential miRNA cargo. The exosomes contained distinct miRNA profiles, with PGC-derived exosomes carrying the highest number of miRNAs (471), compared to 327 in KGN and 218 in SVOG exosomes. The miRNAs identified in these exosomes are functionally enriched in pathways regulating cell cycle, cell division, and cell differentiation, suggesting granulosa cell-derived exosomes play regulatory roles in reproductive processes. Since ovarian granulosa cells are critical regulators of oocyte growth and development as well as female fertility preservation, their differential exosome-mediated miRNA signaling may represent an important mechanism influencing reproductive outcomes. The source-dependent variation in exosomal miRNA profiles indicates that granulosa cell heterogeneity produces functionally distinct extracellular vesicles that could differentially impact endometrial function and embryo implantation during reproductive failure.

title: Histomorphological Evidence of Complex Cannibalism—An Uncharted Territory in Oral Squamous Cell Carcinoma
journal: Indian Journal of Otolaryngology and Head & Neck Surgery
summary: This study investigates complex cannibalism (CxC), a newly recognized histomorphological phenomenon in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC), and its relationship to tumor aggressiveness and immunological markers. Complex cannibalism was found to be significantly elevated in OSCC cases and showed strong associations with both histological grades and aggressive tumor potential. The researchers examined CD68 expression, a marker of macrophage infiltration, in tumor cells and discovered it was highly expressed and significantly correlated with histopathological grades (p < 0.001). CD68 expression demonstrated a medium-strength association with complex cannibalism, suggesting that macrophage-mediated cellular engulfment may contribute to tumor biological behavior. Both complex cannibalism and CD68 expression showed statistically significant correlations with histological grading, indicating these features may serve as markers for assessing OSCC aggressiveness. These findings establish complex cannibalism as an important histopathological indicator of oral squamous cell carcinoma progression and suggest that macrophage activity, reflected by CD68 expression, plays a role in this cannibalistic process.

title: Regenerative Medicine in Gynecology
summary:
# Regenerative Medicine Approaches for Endometrial Dysfunction

This review examines regenerative medicine strategies for treating endometrial and gynecologic pathology, focusing on cell-based therapies and biomaterial scaffolds. Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) promotes angiogenesis, cell proliferation, and wound healing through its rich content of chemo- and mitogenic factors, with intrauterine PRP infusion demonstrated to increase endometrial thickness by approximately 20% and treat conditions including thinned endometrium and Asherman's syndrome. Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) and endometrial stem cells, sourced from bone marrow, adipose tissue, endometrium, umbilical cord, and placenta, exert therapeutic effects through anti-inflammatory signaling and promotion of extracellular matrix remodeling via increased elastin and collagen deposition. Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), generated through reprogramming with Oct3/4, Sox2, Klf4, and c-myc, can differentiate into tissue-specific cells including smooth muscle cells that further enhance matrix quality. Acellular extracellular matrix bioscaffolds derived from decellularized porcine tissues promote tissue regeneration while favoring a beneficial M2 macrophage response, contrasting with inflammatory reactions to synthetic meshes, and when formulated as hydrogels containing growth factors like IGF-1, support myogenesis and functional restoration of damaged pelvic and reproductive tissues.

title: Long-term engraftment and maturation of autologous iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes in two rhesus macaques
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates successful long-term engraftment and maturation of autologous induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs) in two rhesus macaques with myocardial infarction, using the sodium/iodide symporter (NIS) as a reporter gene for non-invasive positron emission tomography tracking. Autologous iPSC-CMs engrafted stably for up to 14 months without immune rejection, teratoma formation, or tumor development, with engrafted cells demonstrating progressive maturation evidenced by sarcomere organization, connexin 43 expression at cell junctions, and a shift from immature (slow-skeletal troponin I) to mature (cardiac troponin I) isoforms. In contrast, MHC-mismatched allogeneic iPSC-CMs were rapidly rejected within 8 weeks through CD3+ T lymphocyte and CD20+ B lymphocyte-mediated immune responses, highlighting the critical importance of autologous approaches. The engrafted cardiomyocytes showed hypertrophic growth with rod-shaped mature morphology, vascular integration through endogenous CD31+ endothelial cells, and stable cardiac function as measured by left ventricular ejection fraction over the 6+ month follow-up period. These findings establish the feasibility and safety of autologous iPSC-CM therapy as a potential treatment for chronic ischemic heart disease in large animal models.

title: Role of Epigenetic Modification in the Intergeneration Transmission of War Trauma
summary: War trauma induces profound neuroendocrine and immunological system dysfunction that can persist across generations through epigenetic mechanisms. Traumatic stress exposure causes both immediate long-term psychological and biological alterations in survivors, with epigenetic changes identified in genes regulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and immune system function. These epigenetic modifications represent a key adaptive mechanism through which environmental stressors reprogram gene expression without altering DNA sequences, thereby explaining the transgenerational consequences of trauma. The genetic modifications resulting from war exposure drive sustained dysregulation of stress response pathways and increase vulnerability to physical health disorders. By linking neuroendocrine dysfunction, immunological changes, and epigenetic reprogramming, this work elucidates how traumatic events imprint biological changes that extend beyond individual survivors to affect offspring, with implications for understanding endometrial dysfunction and reproductive complications in trauma-affected populations.

title: Endometrial decidualization status modulates endometrial microvascular complexity and trophoblast outgrowth in gelatin methacryloyl hydrogels
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how endometrial decidualization—the hormonally-driven differentiation process essential for pregnancy—regulates microvascular complexity and trophoblast behavior using engineered gelatin methacryloyl hydrogels. Decidualization induced by medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) or progesterone increased total network length and vessel number while reducing branch length, with these architectural changes dependent on optimal endothelial-to-stromal cell ratios (2:1) for network stability. Decidualized microvascular networks secreted 14 altered proteins including prolactin, IGFBP-1, and angiogenic factors (angiopoietin-1, FGF-1, PDGF-AA) that regulate vessel development and endothelial proliferation. Critically, decidualized endometrial microvascular networks significantly reduced trophoblast outgrowth compared to non-decidualized controls, with MPA-induced decidualization producing the most pronounced suppression of trophoblast expansion. These findings establish that decidualization-driven remodeling of endometrial vasculature and its secreted biomolecules create a microenvironment that constrains trophoblast invasion, providing mechanistic insight into how proper endometrial preparation coordinates vascular changes with controlled trophoblast infiltration during implantation.

title: Satellite Glial Cells Bridge Sensory Neuron Crosstalk in Visceral Pain and Cross-Organ Sensitization
journal: The Journal of Pharmacology and Experimental Therapeutics
summary: Satellite glial cells (SGCs) in dorsal root ganglia serve as critical intermediaries coordinating communication between sensory neurons from different visceral organs, particularly those innervating the colon and bladder. Through bidirectional neuron-SGC coupling mediated by neurotrophins and purinergic signaling pathways, SGCs facilitate cross-organ sensitization whereby inflammatory signals from one organ activate afferent neurons innervating adjacent organs. When colonic inflammation occurs, SGCs relay signals that activate previously quiescent bladder afferent neurons located in the same spinal segments, establishing a mechanism for colon-bladder cross-sensitization and chronic visceral pain. This SGC-mediated sensory neuron crosstalk represents a novel therapeutic target for treating conditions involving multi-organ pain syndromes and cross-sensitization phenomena. The identification of unique molecular markers distinguishing SGC populations could enable selective manipulation of these glial cells to prevent or reverse pathological cross-organ communication in visceral pain disorders.

title: Altered Extracellular Matrix-Related Pathways Accelerate the Transition from Normal to Pre-Fibroid Myometrium in Black Women
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary:
# Summary: Extracellular Matrix Pathways and Uterine Fibroid Susceptibility in Black Women

This study identifies enhanced extracellular matrix (ECM)-related pathways and mechanotransduction signaling in normal myometrium from Black women compared to White women, suggesting a predisposition to fibroid development. In normal myometrium from Black women, elevated expression of structural proteins (Tenascin C, actin family proteins, myosins) and mechanosensing molecules (Integrin α5β1, vinculin, serum response factor) activates the MRTF-SRF transcriptional complex, which regulates profibrotic and cytoskeletal genes. The transition to at-risk myometrium in Black women is characterized by further ECM accumulation (fibronectin, collagen type I), upregulation of fibrosis-associated factors (activating transcription factor 3, indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase 2), and increased p38 MAPK phosphorylation, indicating heightened mechanotransduction signaling. ECM accumulation increases tissue stiffness, which perpetuates mechanotransduction through integrin-mediated pathways, creating a feed-forward mechanism promoting fibrogenesis. These molecular alterations may explain racial disparities in uterine fibroid incidence, as Black women exhibit baseline elevation of ECM-related gene networks that accelerate progression from normal to pre-fibroid myometrial states.

title: Role of Microgliosis and NLRP3 Inflammasome in Parkinson’s Disease Pathogenesis and Therapy
summary:
# Summary

Parkinson's disease is characterized by dopaminergic neuronal loss in the substantia nigra pars compacta, leading to dopamine deficits that manifest as motor symptoms including rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability, and resting tremor, often preceded by pre-motor symptoms. Emerging evidence indicates that inflammation mediated by microglial activation and NLRP3 inflammasome signaling plays a critical role in the pathogenesis and progression of the disease. These immune mechanisms represent key therapeutic targets for developing new treatment strategies to slow or halt neurodegeneration in Parkinson's disease. Understanding the interplay between microglial activation and inflammasome function may enable earlier intervention based on pre-motor symptom recognition before substantial dopaminergic neuronal loss occurs.

title: Muscular hyperplasia in Crohn’s disease strictures: through thick and thin
journal: American Journal of Physiology - Cell Physiology
summary:
# Summary

Smooth muscle cell hyperplasia emerges as a critical pathological mechanism in fibrostenosing Crohn's disease, driving luminal narrowing and associated clinical symptoms through uncontrolled proliferation. The progressive thickening of the muscular layer directly contributes to the development of strictures and fibrostenotic complications that characterize this disease phenotype. This review synthesizes evidence demonstrating that smooth muscle proliferation, rather than fibrosis alone, represents a significant therapeutic target for addressing the mechanical obstruction and symptomatology in Crohn's disease strictures. By identifying muscle thickening as a novel intervention point, the research suggests that modulating smooth muscle cell dynamics could provide therapeutic benefit in managing fibrostenosing disease complications. These findings shift focus toward the muscular component of stricture pathogenesis, complementing traditional anti-fibrotic approaches with strategies targeting smooth muscle biology.

title: Screening Genes Related to Development and Injury of the Mouse Optic Nerve by cDNA Microarrays
summary: This cDNA microarray study identified 17 functional gene groups involved in mouse optic nerve development and injury responses. The genes encompass diverse biological processes including transcription regulation, signal transduction, protein synthesis and degradation, RNA processing, cytoskeletal organization, and axon growth guidance. These functional categories were found to play key roles not only in normal optic nerve development but also in the pathological responses to optic nerve injury and the subsequent repair mechanisms. The comprehensive classification reveals that optic nerve development, injury response, and repair processes share overlapping molecular machinery, suggesting common underlying regulatory networks. This systematic gene profiling provides a foundation for understanding the molecular basis of optic nerve biology and identifying potential therapeutic targets for nerve injuries.

title: Clinical protocol phase II study of tumor infiltrating lymphocytes in advanced tumors with alterations in the SWI/SNF complex: the TILTS study
summary: The TILTS study investigates tumor infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) as a therapeutic strategy for advanced cancers harboring alterations in the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, which comprises proteins such as SMARCA4 and SMARCB1. Mutations in SWI/SNF complex components drive development of aggressive malignancies including epithelioid sarcoma, malignant rhabdoid tumors, and small cell carcinoma of the ovary hypercalcemic type. These SWI/SNF-mutant tumors are characteristically highly infiltrated with TILs, indicating that tumor-associated lymphocytes are recognizing tumor-specific antigens presented by the malignant cells. The clinical trial explores TILs as a promising immunotherapeutic approach, leveraging the inherent lymphocytic infiltration and antigen recognition present in these tumors. This strategy represents a novel treatment avenue for otherwise aggressive malignancies defined by chromatin remodeling defects, potentially offering clinical benefit by harnessing endogenous anti-tumor immune responses.

title: Cellular heterogeneity and dynamics of the human uterus in healthy premenopausal women
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
summary: This comprehensive single-cell analysis reveals the dynamic cellular composition and functional specialization of the human endometrium across the menstrual cycle in healthy premenopausal women. The endometrium undergoes cyclical regeneration and remodeling involving distinct epithelial and stromal populations with specialized roles: ciliated epithelial cells (Epi-1) support transport, secretory populations (Epi-2, Epi-5, Epi-6) facilitate glandular functions and embryo implantation signaling through markers like LPAR3 and WNT7A, while proliferative epithelial cells (Epi-8) drive tissue renewal. The stromal compartment shows remarkable heterogeneity, with Stro-1 cells producing extracellular matrix, Stro-2 promoting angiogenesis, Stro-3 orchestrating immune interactions via STEAP4, and Stro-4 functioning as vascular progenitors bridging to endothelial cells. Critically, a senescent stromal population (Stro-8) expresses elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (CXCL1, CXCL8, IL6), while the transcription factor FOXO1 controls the decidualization program by activating markers like IGFBP1, PRL, and WNT4 in response to progesterone signaling. These findings establish a detailed cellular framework for understanding endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure and endometriosis-related pathology.

title: Material properties of nonpregnant and pregnant human uterine layers
journal: Journal of the mechanical behavior of biomedical materials
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes the mechanical properties of the three uterine layers—endometrium-decidua, myometrium, and perimetrium—in nonpregnant and pregnant human tissues. The endometrium-decidua exhibits distinct material properties, being the least stiff and most viscous layer in nonpregnant uteri but undergoing significant stiffening, increased viscoelastic ratio, and enhanced diffusivity during pregnancy, changes attributed to shifts in collagen composition and organization. These pregnancy-induced mechanical alterations in the endometrium-decidua layer, which forms the critical maternal-fetal interface, are proposed to result from increased mechanical loads generated by fetal growth and are mediated through mechanotransduction pathways. The myometrium, composed of smooth muscle fascicles supported by collagen and elastic fibers, expands dramatically during pregnancy through hyperplasia and hypertrophy to accommodate fetal growth and facilitate labor contractions. These findings underscore how structural and mechanical remodeling of specific uterine layers enables successful implantation, placentation, and pregnancy progression, with implications for understanding reproductive complications such as placenta accreta spectrum disorder that arise from aberrant uterine wound healing after cesarean delivery.

title: Increase of the T-reg-recruiting chemokine CCL22 expression in a progressive course of cervical dysplasia
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study identifies CCL22 as a key mediator in cervical dysplasia progression through its role in recruiting immunosuppressive regulatory T cells. CCL22 expression, primarily from CD68-positive macrophages, significantly increases in cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) lesions that progress compared to those that regress, and this expression correlates with FoxP3-positive T-regulatory cell infiltration. The progressive accumulation of FoxP3-positive T-regs is associated with higher grades of cervical dysplasia, suggesting an immunosuppressive microenvironment that promotes malignant potential. A substantial proportion of CCL22-producing macrophages also express PPARγ, linking metabolic regulation to immune tolerance in these lesions. The strong correlation between epithelial CCL22 expression and CIN progression indicates that CCL22 could serve as a biomarker for predicting lesion outcomes and potentially guide clinical management. These findings propose CCL22-producing macrophages as therapeutic targets to enhance immune responses against cervical dysplasia.

title: Loss of PRICKLE1 leads to abnormal endometrial epithelial architecture, decreased embryo implantation, and reduced fertility in mice
summary: PRICKLE1 is essential for maintaining proper endometrial epithelial architecture through its role in the planar cell polarity (PCP) pathway, and its loss in mice results in severely compromised fertility. Ablation of Prickle1 in the endometrial epithelium causes abnormal luminal folding, impaired luminal closure, and disrupted cellular polarity, leading to incomplete cytokinesis and increased multinucleated cells. Loss of PRICKLE1 triggers epithelial-mesenchymal transition and alters expression of key adhesion molecules including decreased E-cadherin localization and aquaporin-2, while simultaneously increasing fibroblast growth factors and upregulating SOX9, a marker associated with endometrial hyperplasia and endometriosis. Single-cell transcriptomics reveals a shift toward stromal dominance with increased collagen deposition and EMT markers, accompanied by a fourfold increase in natural killer cells and decreased epithelial cell numbers. These structural and molecular changes severely impair embryo implantation by gestational day 4.5 and reduce litter size from 9.36 to 5.70 pups per litter, highlighting PRICKLE1's critical role in coordinating the epithelial-stromal dialogue required for successful reproduction.

title: Spatial transcriptomic analysis identifies epithelium-macrophage crosstalk in endometriotic lesions
summary:
# Summary

This spatial transcriptomic study reveals a critical epithelium-macrophage crosstalk mechanism in superficial peritoneal endometriotic lesions. Endometriotic lesions, which develop from endometrial tissue fragments refluxed into the peritoneal cavity, are composed of epithelium-like tissue, stromal cells, and fibroblasts, and are associated with pain, infertility, and estrogen-dependence. The lesion epithelium orchestrates heightened inflammatory signaling through upregulation of immune-related genes including complement 3 (C3), HLA molecules (HLA-DRB1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQB1), and CD74, while the stromal compartment shows elevated TNFα signaling via NF-κB and interferon gamma responses. Most notably, the lesion epithelium initiates a 3.70-fold increase in epithelium-to-macrophage signaling compared to normal endometrium, primarily through complement and macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF) pathways, which promote a pro-repair macrophage phenotype. The heterogeneous macrophage population within lesions—including peritoneal and monocyte-derived macrophages—likely reflects altered peritoneal cytokine environments, alongside contributions from dysregulated miRNAs, Notch signaling, aromatase, and prostaglandin metabolism. These findings establish the lesion epithelium as a key driver of local immune dysregulation and provide mechanistic insights into how endometriotic lesions perpetuate inflammation and impair fertility.

title: Mapping Human Uterine Disorders Through Single-Cell Transcriptomics
summary:
# Summary: Mapping Human Uterine Disorders Through Single-Cell Transcriptomics

Single-cell RNA sequencing has revealed cellular heterogeneity and dysfunction across multiple uterine pathologies, particularly highlighting stromal and immune cell abnormalities in endometriosis. In endometrial tissues affected by endometriosis, fibroblasts are elevated and exhibit enhanced expression of pro-inflammatory and pro-tumorigenic genes (C3, C7, S100A10), while fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transitions driven by TGF-β/Wnt signaling promote fibrosis and extracellular matrix remodeling. Endometrial mesenchymal stem cells can differentiate into myofibroblasts via the activin A/STAT3 pathway, contributing to pathological tissue remodeling and impaired decidualization. Critical immune dysfunction includes reduced macrophage activity, impaired antigen presentation and phagocytosis by myeloid cells, and decreased natural killer cell and T-cell cytotoxicity, allowing lesion persistence and creating a pro-inflammatory microenvironment. Additionally, stem cell populations expressing SOX9 and LGR5, along with estradiol accumulation through SULT1E1 downregulation, drive lesion growth and MAPK signaling activation. These transcriptomic insights reveal that endometriosis pathogenesis involves coordinated stromal fibroblast dysfunction, immune dysregulation, and aberrant signaling pathways that collectively promote chronic inflammation and increase the risk of endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer.

title: A positive ReceptivaDx result for BCL6 does not correlate with abnormal ERA results or decreased expression of receptivity-associated markers: two sides of the endometrial receptivity coin in fertility evaluation and treatment
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a critical discordance between two diagnostic approaches for assessing endometrial receptivity in infertile patients. While BCL6, proposed as a marker of progesterone resistance and endometrial inflammation, has been associated with impaired uterine receptivity, a positive ReceptivaDx BCL6 result does not correlate with abnormal ERA (Endometrial Receptivity Array) results or decreased expression of receptivity-associated markers. Using single-nucleus transcriptomics to achieve cell type-specific resolution, the researchers found that BCL6-positive endometrial epithelial cells actually maintained or even upregulated many receptivity-associated genes in both natural and programmed cycles, contrary to the expected downregulation. Notably, BCL6 positivity was associated with *reduced* odds of pre-receptive ERA results, indicating these endometria may be transcriptomically receptive despite elevated BCL6. The findings demonstrate that BCL6 elevation and ERA transcriptomic profiles measure distinct biological aspects of endometrial function—BCL6 reflecting inflammatory status while ERA genes encompass immune response, cell adhesion, and cell-cell signaling pathways. These results suggest that BCL6 positivity alone may not reliably predict implantation failure and highlight the importance of distinguishing between endometrial inflammation and receptivity status in fertility evaluation.

title: Attenuation of Ampullary Anoctamin 1 by the peritoneal fluid in rhesus macaques with spontaneous endometriosis†
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is associated with altered peristaltic and ciliary dysfunction in the reproductive tract, mechanisms that may contribute to subfertility. Anoctamin 1 (ANO1), a calcium-activated chloride channel expressed in the ampulla of the oviduct, orchestrates smooth muscle contractions, ciliary beating, and epithelial secretion—all critical for normal reproductive function. This study demonstrates that ANO1 expression is significantly downregulated in the ampulla of rhesus macaques with spontaneous endometriosis, and this reduction can be replicated in vitro by exposing ampullary tissue to peritoneal fluid from endometriosis cases. The peritoneal fluid environment in endometriosis thus directly suppresses ANO1 function, potentially explaining the oviductal dysfunction observed in affected females. By inhibiting ANO1-mediated smooth muscle contractions and ciliary beating, the altered peritoneal milieu in endometriosis may impair egg transport and contribute to the subfertility phenotype associated with this condition.

title: Endometrial zinc transporter Slc39a10/Zip10 is indispensable for progesterone responsiveness and successful pregnancy in mice
summary:
# Endometrial ZIP10 and Zinc Signaling in Pregnancy

The endometrial zinc transporter ZIP10 (encoded by *Slc39a10*) is essential for successful pregnancy by regulating intracellular zinc levels required for progesterone responsiveness. ZIP10 is highly expressed in decidualizing stromal cells and uterine epithelium during early pregnancy, and its deletion in mice causes female infertility through impaired embryo implantation and subsequent embryonic loss. Mechanistically, ZIP10-mediated zinc uptake is critical for proper progesterone-progesterone receptor signaling, which controls zinc-dependent GLI1 nuclear localization and subsequent expression of decidualization genes including *Hoxa10*, *Bmp2*, *Wnt4*, and *Hand2*. Loss of ZIP10 function leads to intracellular zinc depletion, disrupting the epithelial-to-stromal shift in progesterone receptor expression and preventing luminal epithelial cell removal—a prerequisite for embryo invasion and implantation. Additionally, zinc deficiency impairs hedgehog signaling by increasing Indian hedgehog expression while decreasing GLI1 and downstream transcription factors, creating a dysregulated molecular environment incompatible with decidualization. These findings establish zinc homeostasis as a fundamental mechanism linking progesterone signaling to successful pregnancy, with potential implications for understanding human reproductive failure associated with altered endometrial zinc metabolism.

title: Multi-omics-based mapping of decidualization resistance in patients with a history of severe preeclampsia
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study identified decidualization resistance as a key mechanism underlying severe preeclampsia and other pregnancy complications by mapping dysregulated stromal cell populations in affected endometria. In women with histories of severe preeclampsia, the endometrium exhibits an aberrant stromal landscape characterized by increased proliferative stromal cells expressing MMP11 and SFRP4 alongside abnormal decidualized subpopulations, reflecting impaired epithelial-stromal transition and disrupted progesterone and cAMP signaling. This decidualization resistance is further driven by aberrant cell-cell communication networks involving endoglin, WNT, and SPP1, combined with elevated inflammatory activation in immune cells including heightened IL-1B and TNF expression in macrophages and increased IL32 in NK cells, which together promote endothelial dysfunction and vascular resistance. The study establishes decidualization resistance as a shared pathogenic feature linking severe preeclampsia with endometriosis, miscarriage, and recurrent pregnancy loss, suggesting that restoration of normal decidualization signaling and immune tolerance may be therapeutic targets for preventing these pregnancy complications.

title: Single‐cell transcriptomic atlas of different endometriosis indicating that an interaction between endometriosis‐associated mesothelial cells (EAMCs) and ectopic stromal cells may influence progesterone resistance
summary:
# Summary

This single-cell transcriptomic study identifies endometriosis-associated mesothelial cells (EAMCs) as key mediators of progesterone resistance through intercellular communication with ectopic stromal cells. The researchers found that mesothelial cells in deep-infiltrating endometriosis undergo epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and upregulate fibronectin (FN1) and related genes including TAGLN2, CHI3L1, IGF1, TGFBI, and ANXA4. These EAMCs interact with stromal cells via FN1-integrin complexes that activate the FN1-PI3K-AKT signaling pathway, thereby inducing progesterone resistance in stromal cells and explaining why 25-33% of endometriosis patients fail to respond to progesterone therapy. The study also reveals distinct cellular landscapes across endometriosis subtypes, with ovarian endometriosis showing increased neovascularization and immunosuppressive cell populations, while peritoneal endometriosis exhibits elevated perivascular cells. These findings mechanistically link mesothelial cell dysfunction to therapeutic resistance and provide potential targets for improving progesterone therapy efficacy in endometriosis patients.

title: Molecular and genetic insights into human ovarian aging from single-nuclei multi-omics analyses
summary: This single-nuclei multi-omics study reveals that ovarian aging involves coordinated transcriptomic and chromatin remodeling across multiple cell types, with mTOR signaling and the transcription factor CEBPD emerging as central regulators of age-related decline. Aging dramatically alters ovarian cellular composition, characterized by reduced abundance of granulosa cells, theca cells, and vascular endothelial cells—critical for follicle function—alongside paradoxical expansion of epithelial cells that exhibit elevated cell-cycle gene expression. Key aging-associated pathways include enhanced mTOR signaling, cellular senescence, and histone modification, while protective pathways including oxidative phosphorylation, extracellular matrix signaling, and base excision repair are significantly diminished. Age-related vascular reduction drives hypoxia-mediated cellular senescence through HIF-1 pathway activation, particularly in CDKN1A+ senescent cells that upregulate senescence-associated secretory phenotypes. Intercellular communication networks progressively deteriorate with aging, with oocytes receiving profoundly fewer signals from granulosa and theca cells while losing critical FSH and GDF signaling required for follicle development and steroidogenic function. These findings identify mTOR inhibition as a potential therapeutic strategy to delay ovarian aging, particularly relevant given evidence that mTOR suppression extends female lifespan more robustly than in males.

title: Integral Roles of the TGFβ Signaling Pathway in Uterine Function and Disease
summary: The TGFβ superfamily serves as a master regulator of uterine physiology, controlling cell proliferation, differentiation, and developmental processes essential for reproductive function. TGFβ signaling specifically governs critical uterine functions including endometrial receptivity, implantation, decidualization, placentation, and tissue remodeling and regeneration. Dysregulation of TGFβ signaling pathways is associated with multiple reproductive pathologies, including endometriosis, adenomyosis, recurrent pregnancy loss, and recurrent implantation failure. Understanding the molecular mechanisms by which TGFβ signaling controls these uterine processes is fundamental for identifying diagnostic markers and developing therapeutic strategies for endometrial dysfunction-related reproductive disorders. The high-confidence associations between altered TGFβ signaling and these disease states suggest that targeting this pathway could address the underlying biological defects in reproductive failure conditions.

title: Construction organoid model of ovarian endometriosis and the function of estrogen and progesterone in the model
summary: This study developed an organoid model of ovarian endometriosis using endometrial epithelial and stromal cells from affected patients, demonstrating that eutopic and ectopic endometrial organoids (EUT-O and ECT-O) both express estrogen and progesterone receptors, epithelial markers, and maintain genetic stability through serial passages. Estradiol demonstrated biphasic effects on organoid proliferation, with moderate concentrations (5-10 μM) stimulating growth while higher concentrations inhibited it, whereas progesterone consistently inhibited proliferation and induced apoptosis at elevated doses. Notably, EUT-O exhibited greater sensitivity to estrogen, higher proliferation capacity, superior culture success rates, and cystic growth patterns, while ECT-O showed increased autophagy and lipid accumulation, suggesting greater cellular stress. Ultrastructural analysis revealed that EUT-O maintained relatively intact cellular architecture with expanded protein synthesis machinery, whereas ECT-O displayed more severe organellar damage and cytoplasmic dissolution. These findings underscore the critical roles of estrogen and progesterone in modulating endometriotic organoid behavior and provide a valuable in vitro platform for studying the hormone-dependent mechanisms underlying endometriosis-associated infertility and pelvic pain.

title: CD56-Positive NK Cells and CD138-Positive Plasma Cells in Basal Decidua of Term Placentas in Singleton Pregnancies After Assisted Reproductive Technology Treatment of Endometriosis-Related Infertility
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by profound immune dysfunction in the eutopic endometrium that extends into pregnancy and affects decidualization. CD56-positive natural killer cells, which are essential for embryo implantation, decidualization, and placentation, are significantly reduced in the basal decidua of pregnancies following endometriosis-related infertility, with median counts of only 11.5/mm² compared to controls. This reduction in protective NK cells is associated with multiple placental pathologies including immature villous ramification, villitis of unknown etiology, intervillous thrombosis, and perivillous fibrin deposition. The underlying mechanism involves progesterone resistance in endometriosis, which impairs IL-15-driven NK cell proliferation and creates a pro-inflammatory decidual microenvironment that disrupts embryo development and placentation. Endometriosis also increases the prevalence of chronic endometritis and elevates cytotoxic CD16-positive NK cells while reducing the resting CD56+CD16− population, further compromising immune tolerance and endometrial receptivity. These sustained immune alterations likely contribute to the increased pregnancy complications observed in endometriosis patients, including miscarriage, preterm birth, and placental dysfunction.

title: Revolutionizing the treatment of intervertebral disc degeneration: an approach based on molecular typing
summary: This study presents a molecular typing approach for intervertebral disc degeneration (IVDD) based on chondrocyte differentiation status, addressing a condition that affects approximately 80% of adults with lower back pain. The researchers developed a stratified classification system dividing patients into high and low chondrocyte scoring groups, with the high-scoring classification associated with favorable prognosis and the low-scoring group exhibiting the worst clinical outcomes. The study identified key molecular markers including ADCYAP1, PTHLH, MIA, TUBA1C, LGALS1, and AGR2 that are differentially expressed in degenerating discs, and IL-1β was demonstrated to induce chondrocyte degeneration in vitro. Notably, gefitinib, a therapeutic candidate designed for low-scoring cases, showed protective effects on chondrocytes and potential to reduce IVDD symptoms. This molecular typing framework enables clinicians to perform risk stratification and implement personalized treatment strategies tailored to individual IVDD subtypes, representing a significant advancement beyond conventional morphological assessment using the Thompson grade.

title: An aerobe exercise intervention for optimizing metabolic, cardiovascular and immune status: protocol of an intervention study with a multi-systemic approach for women with unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss
summary:
# Summary

Women with unexplained recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) display abnormal endometrial immune profiles characterized by reduced CD56bright NK cells—which normally facilitate embryo implantation through anti-inflammatory signaling and trophoblast invasion—and elevated pro-inflammatory CD56dim NK cells with increased CD16 expression and IFN-γ/TNF-α secretion. Beyond immune dysfunction, RPL is associated with metabolic disorders including hyperhomocysteinemia and altered amino acid metabolism, as well as cardiovascular aberrations such as reduced uteroplacental perfusion and hypertension. This protocol proposes moderate aerobic exercise as a multi-systemic intervention to simultaneously optimize metabolic, cardiovascular, and immune function, capitalizing on exercise's established ability to increase peripheral NK cells, improve cardiovascular capacity, and modulate immune responses while reducing pregnancy complication risks.

title: Lung Cancer Organoid System to Evaluate the Cytotoxicity of Natural Killer Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study developed a lung cancer organoid system to evaluate natural killer (NK) cell cytotoxicity as a platform for cancer immunotherapy research. NK cells, key mediators of anti-tumor immunity, effectively infiltrated and killed lung cancer organoids derived from genetically modified models with Trp53 and Keap1 deletions. The research revealed that transforming growth factor-beta (TGFβ) signaling suppresses NK cell function, as inhibition of this pathway through either A83-01 treatment or Smad3 deletion significantly enhanced NK cell-mediated cytotoxicity by increasing granzyme B and interferon-gamma secretion. These findings identify TGFβ pathway inhibition as a promising strategy to enhance chimeric antigen receptor NK (CAR-NK) cell immunotherapy, which requires interleukins IL-2 and IL-15 for optimal expansion and activation. The organoid-based co-culture system provides a physiologically relevant three-dimensional model that better recapitulates tumor microarchitecture and enables comprehensive evaluation of immunotherapeutic approaches against lung cancer.

title: 3D bioprinting technology innovation in female reproductive system
summary:
# Summary

3D bioprinting technology represents an emerging approach for addressing endometrial dysfunction and reproductive tissue engineering by enabling construction of biomimetic tissue constructs and disease-specific models. The technology involves depositing bioinks composed of natural polymers—particularly gelatin, alginate, and fibrin—into precise three-dimensional scaffolds that support cell attachment, migration, proliferation, and differentiation. Extrusion-based and inkjet bioprinting methods have been successfully applied to construct artificial ovaries and cervical cancer models, with gelatin-based biomaterials providing optimal biocompatibility and biodegradability for reproductive tissue applications. Alginate hydrogels loaded with induced mesenchymal stem cells have demonstrated efficacy in repairing damaged endometrium, while estrogen therapy complements these approaches by promoting endometrial repair and treating intrauterine adhesions. These integrated strategies enable both regenerative medicine applications and creation of physiologically relevant in vitro models for investigating tumor-stromal interactions and drug screening in reproductive diseases.

title: Global research on NK cells in miscarriage: a bibliometric study
summary:
# Summary

Natural killer cells are critical regulators of successful pregnancy, functioning in both uterine (uNK) and decidual (dNK) compartments to promote angiogenesis, trophoblast invasion, and spiral artery remodeling through chemokine and cytokine signaling. Dysregulation of NK cell populations—particularly decreased CD56bright uNK cells and elevated activated peripheral NK cells—is strongly associated with recurrent spontaneous abortion and implantation failure. The bibliometric analysis reveals that NK cell immunology, along with related immune factors including T cells, dendritic cells, and cytokines, has emerged as a major research focus in understanding miscarriage pathogenesis, complementing established risk factors such as genetic, endocrine, and anatomical abnormalities. Therapeutic interventions targeting NK cell dysfunction, including prednisolone administration to reduce uNK cell populations, show promise in preventing recurrent pregnancy loss. The research landscape also highlights the interconnection between NK cell dysfunction and other pregnancy complications including preeclampsia, endometriosis, and implantation failure, with peripheral blood NK cell profiles potentially reflecting endometrial NK cell status in affected women.

title: Establishment and characterization of endometrial organoids from different placental types
summary: This study established endometrial organoids from epithelial stem cells across five mammalian species with different placental types and characterized their molecular signatures through integrative transcriptome analysis. The organoids maintained epithelial characteristics (E-cadherin expression) and proliferative capacity (Ki-67 expression) while exhibiting upregulated hormone metabolism genes, particularly those involved in steroid biosynthesis such as SQLE, DHCR7, CYP51A1, and CYP24A1. Key signaling pathways including PI3K-Akt, extracellular matrix-receptor interaction, and focal adhesion were significantly enriched in organoids, whereas vascular smooth muscle contraction genes were downregulated, reflecting the epithelial rather than smooth muscle cell composition. Notably, species-specific genes emerged, including SULT1E1 in bovine organoids for estrogen inactivation, CYP1A2 in canine organoids for carcinogen metabolism, and HSD3B2 in rat organoids for steroid hormone synthesis. These endometrial organoids represent a valuable in vitro model system for studying hormone metabolism, reproductive dysfunction including endometriosis-related pathologies, and developing therapeuties relevant to menstrual cycle regulation and infertility treatment.

title: Thermo-sensitive ε-polylysine-heparin-poloxamer hydrogel-encapsulated BMSCs promote endometrial regeneration
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial injury from intrauterine surgeries, infections, or ovulation-stimulating drugs leads to complications including thin endometrium and intrauterine adhesions that compromise reproductive function. This study developed a thermo-sensitive ε-polylysine-heparin-poloxamer (EPL-HP) hydrogel scaffold to deliver bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs) for endometrial regeneration. The hydrogel improved BMSC survival and cytocompatibility while promoting controlled cell release and adhesion. Treatment with EPL-HP hydrogel-encapsulated BMSCs significantly enhanced endometrial thickness, increased gland numbers, reduced apoptosis, promoted immune cell infiltration and angiogenesis, and improved endometrial receptivity compared to untreated controls. Mechanistically, the hydrogel activated the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway, which upregulated the SDF-1/CXCR4 axis to enhance BMSC homing and survival while also upregulating pro-angiogenic factors including SDF-1α and VEGF. These findings demonstrate that this bioengineered scaffold effectively restores endometrial structure and function through coordinated activation of stem cell survival pathways and tissue regeneration mechanisms.

title: Endostatin-expressing endometrial mesenchymal stem cells inhibit angiogenesis in endometriosis through the miRNA-21-5p/TIMP3/PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometriosis-derived endometrial mesenchymal stem cells engineered to express endostatin (EMSCs-Endo) effectively inhibit pathological angiogenesis through a miRNA-21-5p/TIMP3/PI3K/Akt/mTOR regulatory axis. EMSCs-Endo suppress endothelial cell (HUVEC) migration, tube formation, and proliferation while promoting apoptosis by downregulating miRNA-21-5p, which consequently elevates TIMP3 expression and inhibits downstream PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling. In murine models of endometriosis, EMSCs-Endo treatment significantly reduced lesion growth, microvessel density, and VEGF levels while increasing TIMP3 expression and suppressing phosphorylation of PI3K, Akt, and mTOR. These findings establish a mechanistic link between miRNA-21-5p dysregulation in endometriosis and angiogenic progression, while validating engineered stem cells expressing endostatin as a promising cell-based therapeutic strategy that targets the pro-angiogenic microenvironment characteristic of this chronic inflammatory disease.

title: Artificial intelligence-based tissue segmentation and cell identification in multiplex-stained histological endometriosis sections
summary: This study demonstrates the application of artificial intelligence-based image analysis to characterize tissue architecture in endometriotic lesions, which are pathologically characterized by excessive fibrosis driven by α-SMA+ myofibroblasts and collagen deposition that contributes to chronic pain associated with endometriosis. The researchers used multiplex immunohistochemistry with markers including PanCK (epithelium), CD10 (endometrial stroma), α-SMA (myofibroblasts), CD45 (immune cells), and Ki67 (proliferation) to analyze tissue composition across different endometriosis subtypes. Machine learning-based tissue segmentation and deep learning algorithms were superior for automated cell identification compared to traditional methods, revealing that fibrotic tissue comprised over 60% of most lesions and that proliferating immune cells were notably enriched in ovarian endometrioma epithelium and stroma. These AI-driven analytical approaches enable objective, quantitative assessment of the complex stromal and epithelial remodeling underlying endometriosis pathology, which presents clinically with dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and infertility. The findings establish computational pathology as a powerful tool for understanding endometrial dysfunction in endometriotic disease and may facilitate identification of therapeutic targets.

title: GRB2 regulation of essential signaling pathways in the endometrium is critical for implantation and decidualization
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that GRB2, an adapter protein regulating cell survival and proliferation, is critical for endometrial receptivity and implantation. GRB2 expression is significantly reduced in the endometrium of infertile women with endometriosis and in endometrial stromal cells from endometriosis-affected mice, and this loss of GRB2 leads to progesterone resistance and impaired decidualization. Mice with conditional GRB2 deletion in the endometrium (Grb2d/d) are sterile due to implantation failure, defective embryo decidualization, and reduced expression of key decidualization markers including Bmp2, Fst, Fkbp5, and Wnt4. GRB2 mediates its effects through activation of the EGFR/ERK1/2 and PI3K/AKT signaling pathways, which regulate transcription factors FOXO1 and PGR essential for epithelial integrity and decidualization. Notably, GRB2-deficient endometrial tissue prevents endometriotic lesion formation, suggesting that GRB2 loss may contribute to both implantation failure and endometriosis pathogenesis through distinct mechanisms. These findings establish GRB2 as a critical molecular link between endometrial dysfunction and reproductive failure in endometriosis.

title: Immune-privileged cord blood-derived endothelial colony-forming cells: advancing immunomodulation and vascular regeneration
summary:
# Summary

Cord blood-derived endothelial colony-forming cells (CB-ECFCs) represent a promising cell therapy platform combining superior vasculogenic and immunomodulatory properties with minimal immunogenicity. These cells express tolerance-inducing molecules such as HLA-G and demonstrate markedly higher proliferative capacity and resistance to apoptosis compared to adult-derived counterparts, while maintaining lower risk of graft-versus-host disease. CB-ECFCs actively participate in vascular repair and organ revascularization through multiple mechanisms: direct integration into host tissues, secretion of pro-angiogenic factors (PDGF-BB, chemokines, exosomes), and promotion of perivascular cell recruitment via Notch/Jagged-1 signaling and endoglin-dependent adhesion. The cells express key stem cell-related markers including ALDH, which protects against oxidative stress and maintains stemness, as well as NRG1, which protects cardiomyocytes from apoptosis through PI3K/Akt pathway activation. Collectively, these findings position CB-ECFCs as a uniquely effective cell therapy candidate for addressing vascular dysfunction in reproductive and other tissue-regeneration applications.

title: LINE-1 ORF1p expression occurs in clear cell ovarian carcinoma precursors and is a candidate blood biomarker
summary:
# Summary

Clear cell ovarian carcinoma (CCOC) arises from endometriosis through a stepwise progression involving typical endometriosis, atypical endometriosis, and finally malignant transformation. LINE-1 retrotransposon de-repression, marked by elevated expression of its encoded protein ORF1p, is an early event in this pathological sequence, with ORF1p detected in 82% of CCOC cases. In benign cells, DNA methylation and histone modifications suppress LINE-1 expression, but loss of DNA methylation during neoplastic progression allows ORF1p reactivation, a mechanism that can be experimentally induced by DNMT1 inhibitors like decitabine and GSK3685032. Notably, ORF1p is secreted by CCOC cells into extracellular vesicles and can be detected in patient blood plasma and serum with high specificity, demonstrating its potential as a minimally invasive biomarker for early detection of CCOC. The strong and persistent ORF1p expression in pre- and post-chemotherapy samples suggests it may also serve as a marker for disease monitoring and recurrence surveillance.

title: Spheroids from Epithelial and Mesenchymal Cell Phenotypes as Building Blocks in Bioprinting (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Spheroid formation from epithelial and mesenchymal cells represents a promising approach for tissue engineering and regenerative medicine applications relevant to endometrial dysfunction. Epithelial cells contribute protective and absorptive functions, while mesenchymal cells, particularly multipotent mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs), provide structural support and tissue restoration through matrix remodeling and their inherent anti-inflammatory and homing properties. Three-dimensional spheroid culture significantly enhances cellular phenotype maintenance and functionality compared to traditional monolayer cultures; for instance, MSC spheroids upregulate pluripotency markers (Oct4A, Nanog, SOX2) and increase secretion of pro-regenerative factors including VEGF, basic fibroblast growth factor, and interleukin-11, thereby enhancing angiogenic and osteogenic differentiation. The spheroid microenvironment promotes extracellular matrix accumulation and growth factor gradients that activate mechanotransduction pathways, particularly α2β1-integrin signaling required for osteogenesis, and involves key structural proteins including E-cadherin, actin, and tubulin that regulate spheroid compaction and reactivation. These findings establish cell spheroids as superior bioengineered building blocks for regenerative medicine, offering advantages in maintaining differentiation potential, genetic stability, and physiological responsiveness compared to conventional culture methods.

title: Identification of potential drug targets for pelvic organ prolapse using a proteome-wide Mendelian randomization approach
summary: Using proteome-wide Mendelian randomization, this study identified EFEMP1 and MFAP4 as promising drug targets for pelvic organ prolapse (POP), proteins that are enriched in fibroblasts and smooth muscle cells where they regulate extracellular matrix organization, collagen fibril formation, and Wnt signaling. The research revealed significant positive genetic correlations between POP and reproductive conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and ovarian cysts, as well as metabolic factors such as waist-hip ratio and body fat percentage. Mendelian randomization analysis identified FVC and body fat percentage as causally associated with POP, implicating lipid metabolism, metabolic disorders, hormonal changes, and inflammatory responses in disease pathogenesis. Tissue and cellular analyses demonstrated that POP-affected individuals show elevated fibroblasts but reduced B cells and endothelial cells, supporting a dysregulation of connective tissue homeostasis. Three candidate drugs—dasatinib, progesterone, and retinoic acid—showed binding potential to both EFEMP1 and MFAP4, offering therapeutic avenues for this condition characterized by pelvic discomfort, vaginal bleeding, and urinary and sexual dysfunction.

title: Molecular Basis of Impaired Decidualization in the Eutopic Endometrium of Endometriosis Patients
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes impaired decidualization of the eutopic endometrium, a critical defect that compromises embryo implantation and contributes to the 50% infertility rate associated with this chronic gynecological disorder. This decidualization impairment is mechanistically linked to progesterone resistance, wherein endometrial stromal cells fail to properly differentiate into specialized decidual cells that produce essential implantation factors such as prolactin and IGFBP1. Multiple molecular pathways are dysregulated in endometriosis, including elevated estrogen receptor expression and local estrogen synthesis (through increased CYP19A1 and StAR), elevated microRNA-194-3p that directly inhibits progesterone receptor expression, and reduced expression of membrane progesterone receptors (PAQR5, PAQR7, PAQR8). Dysregulation of the BMP2-FOXO1 signaling axis is particularly significant, as reduced FOXO1 expression impairs the transcriptional regulation of decidualization markers; this reduction occurs through multiple mechanisms including Notch pathway downregulation and phosphorylation-mediated nuclear exclusion via AKT1 and NEK2 kinases. These interconnected molecular abnormalities collectively explain how endometriosis disrupts endometrial receptivity and perpetuates reproductive failure even in the anatomically normal eutopic endometrium.

title: Endometrium-derived organoids from cystic fibrosis patients and mice as new models to study disease-associated endometrial pathobiology
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary:
# Summary

Cystic fibrosis, caused by mutations in the CFTR gene, impairs ion and fluid transport and is associated with significantly decreased fertility in affected women. CFTR is expressed cyclically in the endometrial epithelium, with estrogen upregulating its expression and progesterone downregulating it, and plays a critical role in regulating uterine luminal fluid secretion and absorption alongside the ENaC channel. The study developed endometrial organoid models from both CF patients and CF mice that faithfully recapitulate disease characteristics, revealing that CFTR is predominantly expressed in endometrial stem cells marked by LGR5 and shows higher expression during the proliferative phase compared to the secretory phase. Single-cell transcriptomic analysis of CF mouse uterus identified molecular, pathway, and cellular differences associated with CFTR dysfunction, including altered bicarbonate balance that may impair sperm capacitation. Treatment with CFTR modulators successfully restored specific functional aberrations in CF endometrial organoids, suggesting that CFTR modulator therapies could address endometrial pathobiology underlying the subfertility observed in cystic fibrosis patients.

title: Multi-omics analysis of uterine fluid extracellular vesicles reveals a resemblance with endometrial tissue across the menstrual cycle: biological and translational insights
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study demonstrates that uterine fluid extracellular vesicles (UF-EVs) closely mirror endometrial tissue composition across the menstrual cycle at both the transcriptomic and proteomic levels, providing a non-invasive window into endometrial function. UF-EVs originate from multiple endometrial cell populations including epithelial cells, immune cells, and endothelial cells, with distinctive molecular signatures during the mid-secretory phase characterized by elevated tetraspanins (CD9, CD63, CD81), immune markers (CD56, CD45, CD3), and tissue factor (CD142). The mid-secretory phase UF-EVs contain pregnancy-associated microRNAs—particularly members of the miR-200 family (miR-200a-3p, miR-200b-3p, miR-141-3p) and miR-30d-5p—that regulate critical transcription factors (GATA3, GATA6, SOX2, NR5A2, TEAD1) essential for trophectoderm lineage development and embryo implantation. Strong concordance in histone and metallothionein gene expression between UF-EVs and endometrial biopsies across cycle phases, along with shared expression of receptivity biomarkers (PAEP, GPX3, MAOA, DKK1), supports UF-EVs as reliable biomarkers for assessing endometrial receptivity. These findings establish UF-EVs as functional mediators of maternal-embryonic communication and propose them as a non-invasive diagnostic tool for evaluating endometrial competency and implantation potential.

title: The effect of norethisterone acetate on the uterine telocytes, immune cells and progesterone receptors in albino rats
summary:
# Summary

Norethisterone acetate (NETA), a synthetic progestogen used clinically to treat endometriosis and abnormal uterine bleeding, exerts multifaceted effects on uterine structure and immunity. NETA stimulates infiltration and activation of multiple immune cell types including eosinophils, neutrophils, macrophages, lymphocytes, and mast cells while simultaneously enhancing telocyte vesicular activity and their communicative interactions with immune cells. The drug downregulates progesterone receptor expression in the uterine surface epithelium but upregulates it in infiltrating leucocytes, and importantly prevents conception through multiple mechanisms including inhibition of ovulation, alteration of cervical mucus, disruption of normal decidualization, and impairment of fertilization and implantation. These cellular and immunological modifications, mediated largely through progesterone and glucocorticoid receptor signaling, fundamentally alter endometrial morphology and function in ways that appear mechanistically linked to both its contraceptive efficacy and potential long-term effects on endometrial physiology.

title: Potential Role of Menstrual Fluid‐Derived Small Extracellular Vesicle Proteins in Endometriosis Pathogenesiss
summary: This study identifies menstrual fluid-derived small extracellular vesicles (MF-sEVs) as key mediators in endometriosis pathogenesis, a chronic debilitating condition affecting one in seven to ten women and characterized by severe chronic pain and subfertility. Proteomic analysis revealed that MF-sEVs from women with endometriosis display a distinctive protein signature, including elevated levels of immune activation markers (CD86), adhesion molecules (A2M, SELL), and immunoglobulins (particularly IgM), alongside significant reductions in proteins involved in nitrogen metabolism, immune regulation, and programmed cell death. When endometriosis-derived MF-sEVs were exposed to mesothelial cells in vitro, they induced loss of cellular barrier integrity by reducing trans-epithelial electrical resistance and junctional protein expression, while also promoting immune activation. The downregulation of classical small extracellular vesicle biogenesis proteins (TSG101, ALIX, FLOT1) and antimicrobial response components suggests compromised vesicle production and impaired immune surveillance in the peritoneal microenvironment. These findings establish MF-sEVs as potential biomarkers for early endometriosis detection and highlight their role in facilitating the establishment of ectopic endometrial lesions through disruption of mesothelial barrier function and local immune dysregulation.

title: Limiting Premenstrual Endometrial Hypoxia Inducible Factor 2 Alpha May Fine-Tune Endometrial Function at Menstruation
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that elevated endometrial HIF2A (hypoxia-inducible factor 2 alpha) during the premenstrual secretory phase impairs menstrual endometrial repair and contributes to heavy menstrual bleeding. Women with heavy menstrual bleeding showed elevated HIF2A in the glandular epithelium during the mid-secretory phase, and experimental HIF2A stabilization delayed endometrial repair in mice, while HIF2A partial deficiency enhanced repair efficiency and reduced menstrual blood loss. The detrimental effects of HIF2A stabilization involved downregulation of critical repair genes including glucose transporter Slc2a1 and hypoxia-regulated genes Cxcr4 and Ccnd1, which normally support cell proliferation and homing. In contrast, HIF1A supports adequate menstrual repair, suggesting a functional balance between these hypoxia-inducible factors. These findings indicate that limiting premenstrual HIF2A elevation may optimize endometrial hemostasis and repair mechanisms at menstruation, offering a potential therapeutic target for managing heavy menstrual bleeding through fine-tuning of endometrial hypoxic signaling.

title: Stem cells in human normal endometrium and endometrial cancer cells: Characterization of side population cells
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: The human endometrium contains stem cell populations responsible for the tissue's remarkable cyclic regeneration in response to sex steroid hormones. Side population (SP) cells, a subset with stem cell characteristics, were isolated from both normal endometrium and endometrial cancer (EC) cell lines. In normal endometrium, SP cells function as progenitor cells supporting tissue regeneration. In contrast, endometrial cancer SP cells display cancer stem-like properties including reduced differentiation markers, enhanced self-renewal and long-term repopulating capacity, and increased migration and podia formation. These EC SP cells also demonstrate elevated tumorigenicity and bipotent developmental potential, capable of generating both tumor and stromal-like cells. These findings suggest that dysregulation of endometrial SP cells toward a stem-like phenotype with increased proliferative and invasive capacity may contribute to endometrial cancer development and progression.

title: The autophagy protein ATG14 safeguards against unscheduled pyroptosis activation to enable embryo transport during early pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

The autophagy protein ATG14 is essential for successful early pregnancy by preventing unscheduled pyroptosis in the oviduct, thereby enabling normal embryo transport to the uterus. ATG14, a component of the Beclin 1–VPS15–VPS34 autophagy initiation complex, maintains oviductal structural integrity and function through mitochondrial homeostasis; loss of ATG14 triggers mitochondrial dysfunction and aberrant pyroptosis activation via the caspase-1–GSDMD pathway, accompanied by elevated inflammatory cytokines including TNFα and IL-1β. ATG14-deficient mice exhibit marked oviductal structural abnormalities with luminal obliteration and fail to transport embryos to the uterus, resulting in complete infertility, while genetic or pharmacological induction of pyroptosis similarly causes embryo retention in the oviduct. Notably, ATG14 loss specifically impairs progesterone-mediated uterine receptivity but not estrogen signaling, and defective ATG14 function contributes to recurrent pregnancy loss. These findings establish pyroptosis as a critical mechanism underlying oviductal dysfunction and suggest that targeting autophagy modulators may offer therapeutic strategies for early pregnancy loss and infertility.

title: Insights into the single‐cell transcriptome characteristics of porcine endometrium with embryo loss
summary:
# Summary

This single-cell transcriptome study identifies critical endometrial dysfunction mechanisms underlying embryo loss in pigs by characterizing epithelial and stromal cell populations and their signaling networks. The research reveals that embryo loss tissue exhibits pathological elevation of ciliated epithelial cells driven by excessive estrogen activity, associated with increased progesterone receptor (PGR) and estrogen receptor (ESR1) expression, which disrupts normal endometrial receptivity. Key signaling pathways including BMP, IGF, and TGF-β are dysregulated in embryo loss tissue, with abnormal ciliated cell involvement in these networks contrasting sharply with successful implantation tissue where specific epithelial populations prominently express implantation-associated genes (FBLN1, ID1, KLF4, EGR1) and collagen molecules (COL1A1, COL3A1, COL1A2). The immune microenvironment differs markedly, with embryo loss tissue dominated by pro-inflammatory lymphocytes expressing IL-1β and IL-18, whereas successful implantation tissue maintains protective NK cell and macrophage populations expressing anti-inflammatory TGF-β1. These findings suggest that BMP signaling dysfunction may directly cause embryo loss and highlight the importance of balanced inflammatory signaling and proper epithelial-stromal cell communication for successful pregnancy establishment.

title: Rediscovering the rete ovarii, a secreting auxiliary structure to the ovary
summary:
# Summary

This study rediscovered the rete ovarii (RO), an overlooked ovarian appendage comprising three distinct regions—the intraovarian rete, connecting rete, and extraovarian rete—that persist throughout mammalian life, including in humans. Using PAX8 immunostaining and molecular characterization, the researchers revealed that the extraovarian rete is a highly organized secretory structure with ciliated epithelial cells, dense vascularization, associated macrophages, and neuronal contacts, enabling active protein secretion including insulin-like growth factor binding protein 2 (IGFBP2). The extraovarian rete exhibits functional characteristics of a transport conduit, with cilia and smooth muscle contractility facilitating fluid movement toward the ovary that likely carries secreted factors critical for ovarian homeostasis. Proteomic analysis and single-cell RNA sequencing identified components of the SNARE complex machinery—including STX3, STXBP2, clusterin, and RAB11—confirming active vesicle-mediated protein secretion into the lumen. These findings suggest the RO functions as an auxiliary secreting structure that regulates female reproductive function through delivery of paracrine factors, positioning this previously overlooked tissue as an important component of the ovarian regulatory microenvironment.

title: A seed or soil problem in early endometriosis: stromal cell origin drives cellular invasion and coupling over mesothelial cell origin
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates whether endometrial stromal cells ("seed") or peritoneal mesothelial cells ("soil") are the primary drivers of early endometriosis lesion formation. The researchers found that endometrial stromal cells from endometriosis patients exhibit significantly greater invasive capacity across mesothelial cell barriers compared to control stromal cells, demonstrating a twofold increase in transmesothelial invasion. Notably, mesothelial cells from endometriosis patients showed reduced capacity to limit stromal cell invasion, particularly when isolated from established lesion areas, suggesting the mesothelium becomes compromised in disease. Gap junction coupling between stromal and mesothelial cells was identified as a critical mechanism enabling invasion, with endometriosis-derived stromal cells showing heightened responsiveness to coupling induction by mesothelial cells. Overall, the data indicate that the endometrium—specifically the inherent invasive properties of endometrial stromal cells from affected patients—is the predominant pathological driver of early lesion formation, while mesothelial dysfunction plays a permissive supporting role.

title: Single-cell insights into HNSCC tumor heterogeneity and programmed cell death pathways
summary:
# Summary

Single-cell RNA sequencing revealed substantial heterogeneity in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC), identifying six distinct tumor subpopulations with unique biological signatures. The C2 MALAT1+ tumor subpopulation exhibited significantly reduced expression of multiple programmed cell death pathways—including apoptosis, pyroptosis, ferroptosis, and entotic cell death—along with suppressed metabolic activity in riboflavin metabolism, glycolysis, and oxidative phosphorylation. A prognostic model based on the MALAT1 Tumors Risk Score (MTRS), incorporating risk genes ADM, RPL31, EIF5B, and TAF7, identified patients with high-risk disease who exhibited worse survival outcomes, elevated tumor purity, and enrichment of activated CD4 memory T cells. High-risk MTRS patients demonstrated greater sensitivity to conventional chemotherapies including cisplatin, docetaxel, and paclitaxel, suggesting potential therapeutic vulnerability despite immune checkpoint upregulation. These findings establish MALAT1-driven tumor cells as a distinct subpopulation with impaired cell death capacity and altered metabolism, presenting both prognostic value and opportunities for treatment stratification in HNSCC.

title: The tissue distribution of nucleobindin-2/nesfatin-1 in the reproductive organs of bitches with regard to the animal’s age and body weight
summary:
# Summary

This study examined nesfatin-1 (nucleobindin-2 derivative) tissue distribution in canine reproductive organs, revealing significant associations with age, body weight, and reproductive dysfunction. Nesfatin-1, a neuropeptide involved in food intake regulation, energy metabolism, and body weight control, also interacts with the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis to regulate reproductive function. The researchers found elevated nesfatin-1 expression and immunoreactivity in ovarian and uterine tissues of overweight and older bitches, with expression levels correlating positively to body condition score. Notably, nesfatin-1 expression was altered in dogs with endometrial pathologies including cystic endometrial hyperplasia and pyometra, suggesting a role in endometrial dysfunction. These findings indicate that obesity and aging drive metabolic alterations reflected in elevated nesfatin-1 levels, potentially linking metabolic dysfunction to reproductive failure through neuroendocrine dysregulation of the reproductive axis.

title: In vitro co-culture of Fasciola hepatica newly excysted juveniles (NEJs) with 3D HepG2 spheroids permits novel investigation of host–parasite interactions
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that three-dimensional HepG2 spheroid co-culture provides a superior in vitro system for studying Fasciola hepatica infection biology compared to traditional two-dimensional culture. Newly excysted juveniles (NEJs) of this liver fluke parasite, which causes fasciolosis affecting over 17 million people globally, exhibited significantly enhanced survival, growth, and developmental maturation when cultured with HepG2 spheroids, including expansion of gut caeca, body musculature, protective spines, and sensory papillae essential for host interaction. The parasites actively ingested spheroid-derived hepatocytes and oriented toward cell clumps using chemosensory structures, while simultaneously altering their cysteine protease expression profile—decreasing FhCL3 and increasing FhCL1 expression—patterns consistent with in vivo infection. In contrast, NEJs cultured without spheroids became progressively dormant with atrophied musculature, highlighting the critical role of hepatocyte-derived growth factors in parasite development. This 3D spheroid methodology provides a physiologically relevant platform for investigating parasite biology and testing anti-parasitic interventions against this major veterinary and human pathogen.

title: Transcriptomics Reveals the Differences in mRNA Expression Patterns in Yak Uterus of Follicular, Luteal, and Pregnant Phases
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptomic study characterized messenger RNA expression patterns in yak uterus across follicular, luteal, and pregnant phases to identify molecular mechanisms underlying endometrial function and reproductive success. Comprehensive differential gene expression analysis revealed distinct transcriptional signatures associated with reproductive phase transitions, with 495 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between follicular and luteal phases, 353 DEGs between follicular and pregnant phases, and notably 1,303 DEGs between luteal and pregnant phases. Gene enrichment analysis demonstrated that DEGs between follicular and luteal phases primarily participate in adhesion and cell differentiation processes, with significant involvement in arachidonic acid metabolism, retinol metabolism, and cAMP signaling pathways—collectively important for establishing uterine receptivity. During the transition to pregnancy, DEGs were enriched in extracellular matrix-receptor interactions, relaxin signaling, and focal adhesion pathways, processes critical for embryo implantation and placental formation. Between luteal and pregnant phases, DEGs clustered in signal transduction, metabolism, and autophagy pathways, reflecting the substantial molecular remodeling required to support fetal development and maintain pregnancy.

title: Revolutionizing Implantation Studies: Uterine-Specific Models and Advanced Technologies
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the molecular mechanisms governing endometrial receptivity and successful embryo implantation through advances in uterine-specific genetic models. The study reveals a critical hormonal balance where estrogen promotes endometrial proliferation during the preovulatory phase, yet excessive estrogen impairs receptivity, while progesterone counteracts this by downregulating estrogen receptor alpha (ESR1) and suppressing epithelial proliferation to prepare the uterus for implantation. Progesterone orchestrates multiple essential processes including decidualization and epithelium-stroma communication, mediated through key transcription factors and signaling pathways such as LIF-STAT3, IHH-COUP-TFII, and BMP2 signaling.
