CDO knockout mice exhibit dramatic taurine deficiency (89-93% reduction), leading to severely impaired uterine gland formation with a 60% reduction in gland number, reduced uterine size, and disrupted epithelial development. The underlying mechanism involves decreased epithelial cell proliferation and enhanced apoptosis in CDO-deficient uteri, with reduced expression of key developmental regulators including Foxa2. Notably, taurine supplementation in CDO knockout mice substantially reversed these defects by restoring uterine taurine levels, increasing gland formation, restoring PCNA proliferation markers, and reducing the apoptotic BAX/BCL2 ratio by 40%. These findings establish CDO-mediated taurine synthesis as a novel pathway controlling uterine adenogenesis and suggest taurine deficiency may contribute to endometrial dysfunction and reproductive failure.

title: Identification and Characterization of Static Craniofacial Defects in Pre-Metamorphic Xenopus laevis Tadpoles
summary:
# Article Summary

This study demonstrates differential teratogenic effects of two chemical exposures on craniofacial development in *Xenopus laevis* tadpoles. Thioridazine-hydrochloride induces craniofacial defects that spontaneously remodel during pre-metamorphic stages, with normalization of eye morphology, jaw angle, and cartilage structures by stage 49, whereas ivermectin induces permanent, progressively worsening malformations including jaw narrowing and cartilage condensation. Gene expression analysis revealed that thioridazine-induced defects upregulate matrix metalloproteinases 1 and 13 (MMP1 and MMP13), which are regulated by prolactin.2 and drive craniofacial tissue remodeling and repair. The nervous system plays a previously underappreciated role in coordinating this remodeling process alongside the plasticity of musculature, which remodels in response to changes in underlying cartilage structure and mechanical stimuli. These findings reveal a thyroid hormone-independent remodeling pathway essential for correcting developmental craniofacial abnormalities and highlight prolactin and matrix metalloproteinases as critical regulators of embryonic tissue plasticity and recovery.

title: The Interface of Gene Editing with Regenerative Medicine
summary:
# Summary

CRISPR/Cas9 gene editing technology serves as a powerful platform for regenerative medicine by enabling precise genomic modifications through knock-ins, knockouts, and transcriptional control, thereby treating genetic and degenerative diseases while regulating cell fate for tissue repair. When combined with induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and tissue engineering scaffolds, CRISPR/Cas9 facilitates controlled regeneration of damaged tissues by governing gene expression patterns that dictate both tissue deterioration and restoration. The technology encompasses diverse applications including functional genomics and disease modeling, with multiple CRISPR variants—such as CRISPRa/CRISPRi for transcriptional modulation, base editors for single-nucleotide changes, and Cas13 for RNA targeting—each optimized for specific therapeutic outcomes. Advanced biomaterial scaffolds including decellularized extracellular matrix and hydrogels, combined with stem cell transplantation and cutting-edge 3D/4D bioprinting technologies, create biomimetic environments that enhance tissue regeneration and vascularization. While CRISPR/Cas9 applications show remarkable promise, they present potential off-target effects such as chromosomal translocations and p53 activation, which engineered variants like nickases and modified PAM-recognition systems aim to mitigate. Together, these integrated technologies represent a transformative approach to regenerative medicine, enabling precise cellular reprogramming and functional tissue restoration for previously difficult-to-treat conditions.

title: Healing Kinetics of Sinus Lift Augmentation Using Biphasic Calcium Phosphate Granules: A Case Series in Humans
summary:
# Summary

This case series evaluated the healing kinetics of biphasic calcium phosphate (BCP) granules composed of 30% hydroxyapatite and 70% β-tricalcium phosphate during maxillary sinus augmentation in six patients over 6-12 months. The BCP biomaterial successfully facilitated bone regeneration by recruiting mesenchymal cells through host-derived growth factors while providing a three-dimensional scaffold that supported vascular ingrowth and osteoprogenitor cell migration. Regenerated bone at 6 months displayed significantly higher mineralized volume fraction (58±5%) with reduced trabecular spacing and lower degree of anisotropy compared to native bone, while β-TCP progressively resorbed and was completely absent in regenerated tissue by study completion. Histomorphometric analysis revealed increasing proportions of newly formed bone at 9 months with active osteoblasts and dense vascularization, while residual biomaterial particles were colonized by new bone spicules. These findings demonstrate that the HA/β-TCP 30/70 ratio is suitable for successful sinus augmentation, with the biphasic composition allowing fine-tuning of degradation rates and bioactivity to support bone regeneration and remodeling.

title: Changes in Epithelial Cell Polarity and Adhesion Guide Human Endometrial Receptivity: How In Vitro Systems Help to Untangle Mechanistic Details
summary:
# Summary

Successful embryo implantation depends critically on dynamic changes in endometrial epithelial cell polarity and adhesion properties controlled by ovarian steroid hormones. The study demonstrates that progesterone and 17β-estradiol regulate distinct phases of the menstrual cycle and trigger molecular reorganizations in the endometrial epithelium, including downregulation of polarity proteins like Crumbs and Scribble, and redistribution of adhesion molecules such as E-cadherin, desmoplakin, and tight junction components. Loss of epithelial cell polarity during the secretory phase is a prerequisite for trophoblast adhesion and implantation, a process mediated by RhoA-dependent actin cytoskeleton reorganization triggered by trophoblast binding. In vitro models using human endometrial cell lines—ranging from highly polarized HEC-1-A cells with strong tight junctions to poorly polarized RL95-2 cells with weak E-cadherin and reduced desmosomes—reveal that reduced epithelial organization enhances adhesive capacity. The findings reveal how epithelial–mesenchymal transitions in the endometrium, including alterations in basement membrane composition and tight junction integrity, are essential for embryo receptivity, with disruptions in these processes contributing to implantation failure and infertility.

title: Canine Endometrial Mesenchymal Stem Cells: Characterization and Functional Assessment for Cartilage Repair
summary:
# Summary

Canine endometrial mesenchymal stem cells (eMSCs) are multipotent cells expressing high levels of mesenchymal markers CD29 and CD44, with variable CD90 expression, demonstrating robust capacity for osteogenic and chondrogenic differentiation while exhibiting limited adipogenic potential. The conditioned medium from eMSCs (eMSCCM) provides significant cytoprotective effects, enhancing the viability and metabolic activity of oxidatively stressed chondrocytes in a dose-dependent manner through secreted bioactive factors including hepatocyte growth factor, insulin-like growth factor-1, transforming growth factor-β, and extracellular vesicles enriched with antioxidant enzymes and regulatory microRNAs. eMSCCM counteracts oxidative stress-induced cellular damage by promoting antioxidant gene expression (SOD2, GPX1, HO-1) and inhibiting pro-apoptotic signaling pathways, effectively reducing the reactive oxygen species-mediated extracellular matrix breakdown that characterizes cartilage degeneration. These findings establish eMSC-derived conditioned medium as a cell-free therapeutic approach for cartilage repair and osteoarthritis, with broader applicability to other degenerative and inflammatory conditions including colitis and neurological disorders. The paracrine activity of endometrial stem cells positions them as a valuable regenerative resource for treating various pathologies involving oxidative stress and tissue damage.

title: Integrating Graphene Oxide and Mesenchymal Stem Cells in 3D-Printed Systems for Drug Delivery and Tissue Regeneration
summary: This study demonstrates that graphene oxide (GO) integrated with mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in 3D-printed systems creates a powerful platform for tissue regeneration and drug delivery. Graphene oxide enhances MSC function by promoting cell adhesion, stimulating proliferation, and driving osteogenic differentiation through activation of intracellular signaling pathways, while its oxygen-containing functional groups facilitate protein and growth factor adsorption essential for bioactivity. MSCs, which reside in diverse tissues including bone marrow, adipose tissue, and endometrium, demonstrate remarkable multipotency—differentiating into osteogenic, chondrogenic, adipogenic, and neural lineages—and possess potent immunomodulatory properties that treat inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. The extracellular matrix plays a critical regulatory role in controlling MSC behavior and promoting key regenerative processes including proliferation, differentiation, and angiogenesis, while the MSC secretome modulates the tissue microenvironment to support healing. When conjugated with polymers such as poly(L-lactic acid), GO significantly enhances these regenerative properties through its large surface area, electrical conductivity, and chemical interactions mediated by carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxide groups. This integrated approach represents a promising therapeutic strategy for endometrial dysfunction and broader tissue regeneration applications in reproductive failure management.

title: MSC-derived exosomes improve endometrial fibrosis via the lncRNA IGF2R/ miR-143-5p/AQP8 axis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that human umbilical mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes (hUMSC-Exo) prevent endometrial fibrosis through a novel lncRNA IGF2R/miR-143-5p/AQP8 regulatory axis. Endometrial injury triggers aberrant stromal cell metabolism and transformation into myofibroblasts, leading to excessive extracellular matrix deposition and fibrosis that compromises fertility and causes complications including intrauterine adhesions and endometrial thinning. hUMSC-Exo treatment suppressed fibrosis markers (Collagen I/III, Fibronectin, α-SMA, CTGF) while restoring endometrial thickness, gland count, and estrogen/progesterone receptor expression, ultimately improving reproductive outcomes in a mechanically injured uterus model. The therapeutic mechanism involves hUMSC-Exo delivery of miR-143-5p and lncRNA IGF2R, which coordinately suppress AQP8 expression to inhibit fibrosis-related molecule expression in endometrial stromal cells. These findings establish mesenchymal stem cell exosomes as a promising regenerative therapy for endometrial fibrosis-associated reproductive failure.

title: 3D map-guided modeling of functional endometrial tissue using multi-compartment assembloids
summary: The human endometrium undergoes constant remodeling and is particularly susceptible to diseases including endometriosis and endometrial cancer, making improved models of endometrial function essential. Researchers developed a 3D multi-compartment assembloid that successfully recapitulates the complete architecture of functional endometrial tissue, including epithelial cells within a basement membrane and stromal cells embedded in a collagen-rich layer that interact realistically with each other and their extracellular matrices. This engineered tissue model reproduces all three phases of the menstrual cycle—proliferative, secretory, and menstrual regression—within a single platform, supporting controlled growth and organization of multiple cell types. The assembloid enables compartment-specific hormonal responses, particularly stromal cell decidualization, and provides the first opportunity to study dynamic, phase-resolved, and cell-type-specific paracrine signaling within human endometrial tissue. This advanced 3D model addresses longstanding limitations of traditional 2D cultures and provides a powerful tool for investigating endometrial dysfunction underlying reproductive failure and disease.

title: Extracellular matrix reorganization during endometrial decidualization
summary:
# Extracellular Matrix Reorganization During Endometrial Decidualization

Endometrial decidualization, the transformation of the endometrium following embryo implantation, involves extensive reorganization of the extracellular matrix (ECM), with fibrillar collagens, elastin, and lysyl oxidase family enzymes playing central roles in this process. Upon embryo implantation, fibrillar collagens align parallel to the direction of trophoblast invasion while exhibiting region-specific structural reorganization, with type I collagen abundant throughout the decidua and type III collagen dramatically reduced from day 5 onwards. Stromal cells serve as the primary source of ECM components during decidualization, upregulating genes encoding collagen, decorin, asporin, thrombospondin 2, fibulin proteins, and the lysyl oxidase family, which catalyze critical cross-linking of collagen and elastic fibers to enhance tissue integrity and resilience. Elastin expression increases progressively around the implanting embryo and becomes heavily localized in blood vessels, suggesting roles in both placental development and vascular remodeling. Aberrant ECM alterations have been associated with reproductive pathologies including recurrent miscarriage, endometriosis, and adenomyosis, highlighting the functional importance of proper matrix organization for successful pregnancy. These findings establish ECM reorganization as a fundamental process underlying successful decidualization and implantation, with potential implications for understanding endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure.

title: Exploring the Emerging Role of Stem Cell Therapy in Neurodegenerative Diseases and Spinal Cord Injury: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Stem cell therapy represents a promising regenerative approach for treating neurodegenerative diseases and spinal cord injuries through multiple biological mechanisms. Stem cells exert neuroprotective effects by reducing oxidative stress, producing antioxidants, and secreting neurotrophic factors including brain-derived neurotrophic factor, nerve growth factor, and glial cell-derived neurotrophic factor. Additionally, stem cells modulate immune responses and trigger anti-inflammatory signals by producing interleukin-10 and prostaglandin, which help slow disease progression in conditions like Alzheimer's disease by reducing amyloid-beta deposits and neuronal apoptosis. Various stem cell types—including mesenchymal stem cells, neural stem cells, and adipose-derived stem cells—have demonstrated therapeutic efficacy in preclinical and clinical settings, with bone marrow and umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells showing particular promise in reducing neuroinflammation and promoting neuronal survival. The combination of direct neural replacement, paracrine signaling, and immunomodulation positions stem cell therapy as a multifaceted treatment strategy capable of addressing the complex pathophysiology of these debilitating nervous system disorders.

title: Decoding functional and developmental trajectories of tissue-resident uterine dendritic cells through integrative omics
summary: This integrative omics study reveals that uterine dendritic cells (uDCs) are critical for endometrial function through distinct developmental and functional trajectories. Resident progenitor uDCs within the endometrium generate multiple uDC subtypes that perform specialized roles in immune regulation, antigen presentation, and embryo implantation. Dendritic cells uniquely balance maternal tolerance to the semi-allogeneic fetus with pathogen defense by creating an inflammatory gradient with macrophages that facilitates blastocyst implantation and decidualization. Using transcriptomic markers including LYZ and IRF8, the researchers identified a progenitor cluster (cluster 0) expressing CX3CR1, CLEC10A, and CD1C that differentiates into functionally distinct populations, including a plasmacytoid-like subset (cluster 1) marked by GZMB and JCHAIN expression. Cluster 3 displays proliferative capacity with expression of proliferation-associated genes like MKI67 and TOP2A, while immune regulatory genes including IL-1b, FCGR2b, CD1D, and IL-18 are strategically expressed across clusters to modulate inflammation and antigen presentation. These findings establish uDCs as essential orchestrators of reproductive success through coordinated control of immune tolerance, tissue remodeling, and implantation competence.

title: Postpartum Uterine Involution in Cows: Quantitative Assessment of Structural Remodeling and Immune Cell Infiltration
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes the temporal dynamics of structural remodeling and immune cell infiltration during postpartum uterine involution in dairy cows, a process essential for restoring fertility. Following calving-induced mechanical injury to the endometrium, uterine involution proceeds through distinct immunological phases: an early postpartum period characterized by neutrophil and macrophage infiltration, transitioning to peak lymphocyte, natural killer cell, and T and B lymphocyte infiltration by day 21, and continued endometrial gland proliferation by day 35. Concurrently, stromal edema and subepithelial collagen deposition peak at day 21, indicating active extracellular matrix remodeling. The authors propose that dysregulation of this normally coordinated immune response—such as persistent neutrophilic or dysregulated inflammatory responses—may underlie subclinical endometritis and chronic fibrosis, both of which compromise ovarian and endometrial function and fertility. These findings suggest that immune cell dynamics serve as potential biomarkers for early detection of postpartum uterine diseases and highlight how metabolic stress, tissue trauma severity, and individual immune competence during the peripartum period influence involution success and reproductive outcomes.

title: Bilateral Osseous Metaplasia of the Ovary in Association with Endometrioma: A Rare Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of bilateral osseous metaplasia of the ovary occurring in conjunction with benign ovarian endometrioma in a 43-year-old woman presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding and dysmenorrhea. While osseous metaplasia is typically associated with malignant ovarian conditions such as low-grade serous carcinoma and psammocarcinoma, it has also been documented in benign lesions including endometrioma, serous cystadenoma, fibroma, and thecoma. The pathogenesis of osseous metaplasia appears multifactorial, involving chronic inflammation, tissue damage, and tissue repair mechanisms, with bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs) playing a key role by upregulating adhesion molecules that promote leukocyte infiltration and perpetuate inflammation. Pluripotent endometrial stromal cells and postabortal fetal tissue implantation have been proposed as potential sources of ectopic osteogenesis. The patient was managed with hormonal therapy including dienogest combined with ethinylestradiol, as well as tranexamic acid and mefenamic acid for symptomatic relief of heavy menstrual bleeding.

title: Microfluidic chip-integrated vascularized endometrial complexes: Mitochondrial function and paracrine crosstalk enhance regenerative potential
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that a microfluidic-integrated vascularized endometrial complex, termed HEO (comprised of endothelial cells, epithelial cells, and stromal cells), exhibits superior regenerative capacity through coordinated paracrine signaling and mitochondrial enhancement. Endothelial cells secrete BMP6 and Galectin-9, which enhance mitochondrial function and promote epithelial cell proliferation, while epithelial and stromal cells secrete WNT7A and WNT5A respectively to stimulate angiogenesis and vascular network formation. Dynamic culture conditions further amplify these benefits by increasing epithelial proliferation and reducing apoptosis while maintaining proper mitochondrial membrane potential. When implanted in mice, the HEO complex demonstrated superior endometrial repair and the highest pregnancy rates compared to control groups, suggesting potential therapeutic utility for treating endometrial damage caused by curettage, infection, or trauma that leads to pathologies including intrauterine adhesions, endometriosis, adenomyosis, and implantation failure. Hormonal stimulation with estrogen and progesterone induced appropriate secretory-phase markers (PRL, PAEP, SPP1) and downregulated proliferation markers, confirming the complex's ability to recapitulate normal endometrial function.

title: Ectopic recruitment of neuroblasts in striatal myelin bundles and nucleus accumbens following AraC chemical lesion
summary:
# Summary

Cytarabine (AraC) chemotherapy induces profound disruption of the subventricular zone neurogenic niche through multiple interconnected mechanisms, ultimately causing ectopic neuroblast migration to striatal myelin bundles and nucleus accumbens. AraC administration damages the structural integrity of this niche by destroying the ependymal layer, reducing neural stem cell proliferation, and triggering robust acute neuroinflammation characterized by microglial activation and peripheral immune cell infiltration. The ensuing damage to oligodendrocytes and myelin structures is particularly significant, with marked reductions in mature oligodendrocytes, thinned myelin sheaths, and increased axonal degeneration evident by six weeks post-injection. Rather than following their normal rostral migratory stream pathway to the olfactory bulb, diverted neuroblasts preferentially accumulate within striatal myelin tracts where they remain in an immature, non-proliferative state, whereas those in gray matter differentiate into medium spiny neurons expressing mature markers. This study reveals how chemotherapy-induced neuroinflammation initiates a cascade of oligodendrocyte dysfunction and myelin pathology that fundamentally redirects neuronal progenitor cell migration and fate, resulting in widespread tissue loss and structural degeneration in affected brain regions.

title: Human Decidual RUNX1 Promotes Angiogenesis and Trophoblast Differentiation by Regulating Extracellular Vesicle Signaling
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the transcription factor RUNX1 in human decidual cells orchestrates critical early pregnancy events through extracellular vesicle (EV) signaling. RUNX1 promotes decidualization and activates the HIF2α-RAB27B pathway, which enhances EV secretion from decidual cells. These EVs contain key cargo proteins including ANGPTL2 and IGF2, which are directly regulated by RUNX1 and mediate essential placentation processes. ANGPTL2-containing EVs promote maternal angiogenesis by stimulating vascular network formation in endothelial cells, while IGF2-containing EVs facilitate trophoblast differentiation and extravillous trophoblast invasion. RUNX1 depletion significantly reduces EV secretion and impairs both vascular development and trophoblast differentiation, highlighting decidual RUNX1 as a central regulator of the paracrine signaling mechanisms necessary for successful implantation and early placental development.

title: Role of hyaluronan in endometrial receptivity: key insights for implantation and reproductive success
summary:
# Summary

Hyaluronan (HA) metabolism plays a critical role in endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation, with distinct expression patterns of key regulatory genes during the menstrual cycle. The HA synthases HAS2 and HAS3 are upregulated in the mid-secretory phase to promote HA accumulation, while the degradation enzymes HYAL2, CEMIP, and CEMIP2 are coordinately regulated to fine-tune HA levels during this window of implantation. HA receptors, particularly CD44, are elevated in the mid-secretory phase and facilitate cell adhesion and migration necessary for implantation through interactions with HA and the extracellular matrix. In patients with repeated IVF failure, there is significant downregulation of these HA-synthetic and receptor genes (HAS2, HAS3, CD44, versican, and syndecans), resulting in impaired HA-mediated signaling and defective endometrial receptivity. Additional HA-binding proteins including hyalectans (versican, aggrecan) and heparan sulfate proteoglycans (syndecans, glypicans) stabilize the HA-rich extracellular matrix to support implantation. These findings establish hyaluronan metabolism and HA receptor signaling as critical determinants of reproductive success and potential therapeutic targets for improving outcomes in implantation failure.

title: Mesenchymal stem cells: opening a new chapter in the treatment of gynecological diseases
summary: Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs), which possess self-renewal and multidirectional differentiation capabilities, are emerging as a promising therapeutic approach for multiple gynecological conditions including endometriosis, intrauterine adhesions, premature ovarian insufficiency, and pelvic floor dysfunction. MSCs derived from various sources—bone marrow, adipose tissue, umbilical cord, placenta, endometrium, and menstrual blood—exhibit distinct advantages, with umbilical cord-derived MSCs demonstrating superior proliferative and migratory capacity compared to adult-derived counterparts. The therapeutic mechanisms involve promotion of endometrial epithelial cell differentiation and regeneration, inhibition of fibrosis through suppression of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, and modulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β, tumor necrosis factor-α, and interleukin-6 that drive pathological adhesion formation. Specialized delivery strategies, such as poly(glycerol sebacate) scaffolds, enhance MSC retention in damaged uterine tissue, while acupuncture stimulation promotes MSC migration to injured sites through the stromal cell-derived factor-1/CXC motif chemokine receptor 4 axis. Additionally, MSC-derived apoptotic bodies and adipose-derived MSCs upregulate vascular endothelial growth factor to enhance endometrial angiogenesis and tissue repair, offering a multi-faceted approach to restoring endometrial function in reproductive failure.

title: Single-cell RNA sequencing of endometrium uncovers dynamic characteristics and dysregulation of perivascular CD9+SUSD2+ cells in thin endometrium
summary: Single-cell RNA sequencing identified perivascular CD9+SUSD2+ cells as a distinct population of endometrial stem/progenitor cells expressing canonical mesenchymal markers (CD73, CD90, CD105) while lacking hematopoietic and immune markers. These cells localize to spiral arteries and demonstrate higher abundance during the proliferative phase, playing crucial roles in endometrial regeneration, repair, wound healing, and extracellular matrix remodeling. In thin endometrium—a reproductive disorder associated with infertility and reduced implantation success—perivascular CD9+SUSD2+ cells exhibit dysregulation characterized by collagen over-deposition, impaired cell cycle progression, and attenuated adipogenic differentiation. The pathology involves aberrant cell-cell communication networks and increased fibrosis, potentially driven by defective stem cell function and perivascular adaptation. The study provides molecular insights into how dysregulated CD9+SUSD2+ cell populations contribute to thin endometrium pathogenesis, informing therapeutic development alongside existing treatments including estrogen, aspirin, pentoxifylline, sildenafil, and platelet-rich plasma administration.

title: Role of CD138+ Plasma Cells and Natural Killer Cells in Couple Infertility: A Review
summary:
# Summary

CD138+ plasma cells and uterine natural killer (NK) cells represent key immune populations whose dysregulation contributes to implantation failure and recurrent pregnancy loss in infertile couples. Chronic endometritis, characterized by elevated CD138+ plasma cells, impairs the endometrial microenvironment by downregulating implantation-associated genes (LIF, HOXA10, IGFBP1) while simultaneously upregulating pro-inflammatory pathways including NF-κB signaling and chemokines (CXCL13, CCL20), ultimately compromising embryo receptivity. Uterine NK cells normally support implantation through angiogenesis and decidualization via secretion of VEGF, but dysregulation leads to excessive cytotoxic mediator production (perforin and granzyme B), creating a hostile endometrial environment that inhibits successful pregnancy establishment. Clinical evidence demonstrates that targeting these immune abnormalities—through antibiotic treatment of chronic endometritis or corticosteroid-mediated normalization of NK cytotoxic markers—can restore implantation rates and reproductive outcomes. The presence of ≥3 CD138+ lesions is now recognized as a diagnostic criterion strongly associated with reduced clinical pregnancy and live birth rates, highlighting the diagnostic and therapeutic relevance of immune profiling in managing couple infertility.

title: Metabolomic Analysis of Key Metabolites and Regulatory Mechanisms in the Transition of Uterine Receptivity in Water Buffalo (Bubalus bubalis)
summary:
# Summary

This metabolomic study in water buffalo reveals that successful embryonic implantation requires precisely timed endometrial receptivity supported by coordinated metabolic and immunological changes. The research identified stage-specific metabolic reprogramming across the implantation window: steroid hormone biosynthesis establishes receptivity by day 15, retinol and folate metabolism on day 18 support organogenesis, and aromatic amino acid pathways by day 21 prepare for nutrient allocation to the developing conceptus. Circulating metabolites regulate endometrial receptivity through multiple mechanisms including amino acid and lipid metabolism, with unsaturated fatty acids, retinol, and phenylalanine metabolism showing direct correlation with successful implantation. The immune microenvironment also undergoes dynamic remodeling during pregnancy, characterized by reduced white blood cell counts, lymphocytes, and neutrophils alongside altered monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratios, indicating immunometabolic integration essential for embryo attachment. These findings demonstrate that dysregulation of these interconnected metabolic pathways and immune responses contributes to early pregnancy loss, positioning serum metabolite profiling as a potential biomarker for predicting implantation success.

title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Analysis Highlights the Impact of NFKB2-Mediated MIF-CD44 Signaling Axis in Endometrioid Endometrial Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This single-cell transcriptomic study reveals that endometrioid endometrial cancer progression is driven by a critical signaling axis involving NFKB2-mediated MIF-CD44 communication between tumor-associated macrophages and malignant epithelial cells. The SOX9+LGR5- epithelial cell subtype, which is significantly enriched in cancer tissues and displays elevated copy number variations, exhibits heightened malignancy through NFKB pathway activation and serves as the primary target of M2-like macrophages expressing MIF. The transcription factor NFKB2 mediates MIF's pro-tumorigenic effects on the CD44 receptor in malignant epithelia, with MIF co-expressed alongside the epithelial marker E-cadherin, suggesting preservation of epithelial characteristics despite malignant transformation. Additionally, the tumor microenvironment supports cancer progression through pericyte-to-fibroblast transition in stromal cells and increased CD8 exhausted/regulatory T cells, facilitating immune evasion and reduced cytotoxic T cell activity. These findings establish the NFKB2-MIF-CD44 signaling axis as a key therapeutic target in endometrioid endometrial cancer and highlight the complex interplay between malignant epithelia and immunosuppressive stromal components.

title: Primary human ovarian interstitial cells contribute to murine follicle growth through follicle-interstitial paracrine crosstalk
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that primary human ovarian interstitial cells enhance murine follicle growth through direct cell-cell paracrine crosstalk rather than soluble factor signaling. Both prepubertal and postpubertal human interstitial cell populations co-cultured with isolated secondary follicles significantly improved follicle diameter by day eight, with postpubertal interstitial cells additionally increasing estradiol production without affecting oocyte maturation rates. Notably, conditioned media alone from these interstitial cells failed to promote follicle growth, establishing that physical cell-cell contact is essential for the growth-promoting effects. The interstitial compartment, enriched in theca and stromal cells expressing extracellular matrix and growth factor-related genes, functionally supports follicle expansion through mechanisms involving blood vessel recruitment and ECM remodeling. These findings underscore the critical role of paracrine signaling from ovarian stromal populations in early folliculogenesis and provide insights into optimizing in vitro follicle culture systems for reproductive applications.

title: The dominant follicle: the final frontier in bovine oocyte development
summary:
# Summary: The Dominant Follicle and Oocyte Competence in Cattle

The dominant follicle creates a specialized hormonal and immunological environment that supports progressive oocyte capacitation, a process essential for acquiring full developmental competence through both cytoplasmic maturation (involving cytoskeleton and organelle reorganization) and nuclear maturation (meiosis I completion). The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, initiated by GnRH and FSH, drives follicle selection and estradiol production by theca and granulosa cells, while the pre-ovulatory LH surge triggers the critical switch from estradiol to progesterone production and initiates meiosis resumption through EGF-signaling cascades. Oocytes matured in vivo from dominant follicles achieve substantially higher blastocyst development rates (58-78%) compared to in vitro matured oocytes (35%), highlighting the critical importance of the natural follicular microenvironment; this superiority depends on proper expression of competence-associated genes including CYP19A1, IGF1, and their signaling pathways. Pre-ovulatory follicular fluid contains a complex immunological milieu—including chemokines (CCL2, CCL3, CCL4, CXCL10), growth factors (VEGF-A, IL10), and lipid mediators (ω-3 PUFA)—that facilitates leukocyte infiltration and angiogenesis while suppressing meiotic resumption via hypoxanthine until ovulation. Nutritional and environmental stressors such as postpartum body condition loss and heat stress impair dominant follicle competence by reducing IGF-1, estradiol, and follicular glucose concentrations,

title: Single-cell atlas of cervical organoids uncovers epithelial immune heterogeneity and intercellular cross-talk during Chlamydia infection
summary:
# Summary

This study used single-cell RNA sequencing of cervical organoids to characterize epithelial heterogeneity and immune responses during Chlamydia trachomatis infection. The cervical epithelium functions as both a structural and immunological barrier, with ectocervical squamous cells reinforcing barrier integrity upon infection while endocervical columnar cells undergo extensive transcriptional reprogramming, particularly in uninfected bystander cells activated by interferon signaling. Uninfected bystander cells emerged as major contributors to immune response coordination and tissue regeneration, revealing a previously underappreciated role in managing infection-induced epithelial dysfunction. The analysis identified distinct epithelial cell populations with specialized functions: basal stem cells expressing COL17A1 and TP63, proliferative cells marked by MKI67 and CDK1, mucin-producing cells expressing MUC5B and MUC5AC for pathogen defense, and terminally differentiated ciliated cells expressing TUBA1A and FOXJ1. Chronic Chlamydia infection compromises epithelial cell fate determination and genomic surveillance, contributing to pathological sequelae including infertility, pelvic inflammatory disease, and ectopic pregnancy. These findings establish the cervical epithelium as a dynamic immunological interface where intercellular communication coordinates protective responses to infection.

title: Imaging Mass Cytometry‐Based Immune Profiling of Human Peyer's Patches in Patients with Crohn's Disease
summary:
# Summary

Peyer's patches are crucial lymphoid structures that mediate mucosal immunity through antigen sampling by M cells and subsequent T and B lymphocyte activation, but undergo significant immune dysregulation in Crohn's disease patients. Using imaging mass cytometry to profile tissue architecture and immune composition, this study revealed that colonic Crohn's disease exhibits marked lymphoid remodeling characterized by increased activated CD38+ CD8+ T cells, reduced naive T cell populations, and expanded B cell-enriched neighborhoods compared to healthy controls. In contrast, ileal Crohn's disease samples maintained immunological profiles resembling healthy tissue with preserved naive T cell frequencies and minimal structural perturbations, indicating distinct regional immune dysregulation patterns. The spatial analysis demonstrated that lamina propria regions show increased interactions between mature CD8+ T cells and monocytes in disease, while lymphoid follicles display altered effector T cell–B cell interactions at the expense of naive T cell involvement. These findings suggest that colonic inflammation triggers substantial reorganization of lymphoid architecture, with impaired antigen presentation and dysregulated T cell responses perpetuating the inflammatory state, highlighting divergent immunopathogenic mechanisms between intestinal disease locations.

title: Steroid hormone-treated endometrial organoids enhance implantation of in vitro-produced porcine embryos
summary:
# Summary

Steroid hormone treatment of porcine endometrial epithelial organoids enhances embryo implantation through coordinated upregulation of growth-promoting factors. Estradiol activates fibroblast growth factor 7 and insulin-like growth factor 1 while increasing estrogen receptor expression, promoting endometrial proliferation and thickness. Progesterone upregulates transforming growth factor β1 and vascular endothelial growth factor via progesterone receptor signaling, stabilizing the endometrium and promoting angiogenesis. When co-cultured with in vitro-produced blastocysts, hormone-treated organoids significantly enhanced embryo growth rate, surface area, and outgrowth compared to untreated controls. These findings demonstrate that endometrial organoid systems can effectively model the hormonal regulation of embryonic-maternal interactions critical for successful implantation, offering a valuable tool for studying reproductive dysfunction and screening therapeutic interventions for infertility and endometrial disorders.

title: Transplantation of UC-MSCs in ovary improves ovarian function and intermediate outcomes in IVF/ICSI cycles of POI patients: a clinical cohort study
summary: This clinical cohort study demonstrates that direct ovarian injection of umbilical cord-derived mesenchymal stem cells (UC-MSCs) significantly improves reproductive outcomes in patients with premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) undergoing IVF/ICSI cycles. UC-MSC treatment substantially increased antral follicle counts, oocyte retrieval numbers, and frozen embryo production compared to controls, with 55.81% of treated cycles yielding frozen embryos versus only 11.63% in untreated controls. Mechanistically, UC-MSCs activate the FOXO signaling pathway and phosphorylated FOXO3a in ovarian cells while regulating oxidative stress and autophagy through AMPK/mTOR pathways, thereby reducing follicular apoptosis and promoting mature follicle formation. Extracellular vesicles derived from UC-MSCs integrate into granulosa cells and enhance their resistance to damage, contributing to improved ovarian function. These findings establish UC-MSC ovarian injection as a promising regenerative strategy for restoring ovarian reserve and improving fertility outcomes in POI patients, with direct local delivery facilitating more rapid functional recovery than systemic administration.

title: Injury-induced intestinal stem cell renewal requires capillary morphogenesis gene 2
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that capillary morphogenesis gene 2 (CMG2) is essential for intestinal stem cell renewal following tissue injury, despite being dispensable during homeostasis. CMG2, a membrane protein that binds extracellular matrix components including laminin and collagen IV and VI, functions as a context-specific modulator of Wnt signaling to promote the transition from fetal-like stem cells to adult Lgr5+ intestinal stem cells. In CMG2-knockout mice challenged with dextran sodium sulfate (DSS)-induced colitis, regeneration fails due to impaired β-catenin nuclear translocation and defective Lgr5+ stem cell pool replenishment, resulting in persistent epithelial atrophy, low proliferation, increased neutrophil infiltration, and elevated inflammation markers despite normal initial colitis severity. These findings explain the chronic diarrhea observed in patients with Hyaline Fibromatosis Syndrome, a rare genetic disorder caused by CMG2 mutations, and underscore CMG2's critical role in Wnt-driven intestinal regeneration and barrier restoration following injury.

title: Endometrial receptivity profiled through transcriptomic analysis of uterine fluid extracellular vesicles using systems biology and bayesian modeling for pregnancy prediction
summary: This study profiled endometrial receptivity by analyzing the transcriptomic signatures of extracellular vesicles (EVs) isolated from uterine fluid in women undergoing assisted reproductive technology. RNA-sequencing of uterine fluid EVs from 82 women identified 966 differentially expressed genes, with weighted gene co-expression network analysis clustering these into four functionally distinct modules associated with implantation-related processes including immune regulation, hemostasis, extracellular matrix remodeling, and MAP kinase signaling. Notably, women who achieved pregnancy demonstrated globally higher gene expression, with specific genes such as RPL10P9, LINC00621, and MTND6P4 significantly upregulated compared to non-pregnant controls. A Bayesian logistic regression model integrating gene expression modules with clinical variables revealed that elevated expression of the grey module (OR=8.16) and combined turquoise/brown modules (OR=4.95) strongly predicted pregnancy success, while the blue module expression and larger EV sizes were associated with reduced pregnancy odds. The findings establish that uterine fluid EVs effectively reflect endometrial molecular status and provide a non-invasive biomarker approach for predicting implantation potential in fertility treatments.

title: VSIG4 Is Dispensable for Tumor Growth and Metastasis in Murine Colorectal and Breast Cancer Models
summary:
# Summary

VSIG4 is a protein expressed on tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) in human colorectal and triple-negative breast cancers, where high expression correlates with worse patient prognosis. The receptor functions as an immune checkpoint molecule that suppresses T cell proliferation and activation while promoting phagocytosis and dampening inflammatory macrophage responses. However, this study reveals a critical species-dependent difference: VSIG4 expression is absent in murine TAMs from standard colorectal and breast cancer models, and genetic deletion of VSIG4 in mice does not affect primary tumor growth or systemic metastasis formation, with only a minor reduction in omental metastasis observed. These findings indicate that while VSIG4 may represent an important immunosuppressive mechanism in human cancers, it is not required for tumor progression in murine models, suggesting that VSIG4-targeted therapies may have limited efficacy unless human-specific mechanisms are preserved in preclinical studies.

title: Pathogenicity patterns in cytochrome P450 family
summary:
# Summary

The Cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzyme family comprises 57 genes with diverse metabolic roles including hormone production, drug metabolism, and vitamin D3/mineral homeostasis, with pathogenic variants clustering in functionally critical regions. Specific CYP variants exhibit disease associations: CYP2C19 poor metabolizer alleles increase endometriosis susceptibility through altered hormone metabolism, while CYP1B1 loss-of-function mutations cause primary congenital glaucoma through disrupted anterior segment development. Pathogenicity increases progressively from general protein areas toward substrate transport tunnels and cofactor-binding sites, with tunnel-lining residues and cofactor-binding residues showing the highest predicted pathogenicity scores. Key structural regions—particularly helices H, I, K, and L—accumulate pathogenic variants, with specific amino acids (leucine, phenylalanine, isoleucine, arginine) enriched in tunnel regions and cofactor regions showing enrichment for glycine, cysteine, phenylalanine, arginine, and tryptophan. Single amino acid alterations disrupting these tunnels can impair substrate transport, electron transfer, and oxygen activation, explaining the functional consequences of CYP variants in reproductive and ocular diseases.

title: Fibroblast heterogeneity and FN1-mediated signaling in endometriosis revealed by single-cell and spatial transcriptomics
summary: This single-cell and spatial transcriptomics study reveals the cellular heterogeneity underlying endometriosis-associated fibrosis and reproductive dysfunction. The research identified five distinct fibroblast subpopulations with specialized roles: the C2 CXCR4+ subset exhibits high proliferative capacity and stemness while orchestrating both immune and fibrotic responses through FN1-mediated signaling, positioning it as a key driver of pathology in ectopic endometrial lesions. Meanwhile, the C0 FHL2+, C1 SFRP2+, C3 RAMP1+, and C4 TFF3+ fibroblast subpopulations engage in diverse processes including metabolic regulation, tissue remodeling, and leukocyte interactions, collectively driving extracellular matrix remodeling and immune dysregulation. Fibroblasts expressed high levels of collagen genes (COL1A1, COL1A2, COL3A1) and related extracellular matrix molecules, establishing their central role in the pathological fibrosis characteristic of endometriosis progression. These findings provide a molecular basis for understanding how fibroblast heterogeneity contributes to the clinical manifestations of endometriosis—including infertility, dysmenorrhea, and pelvic pain—and suggest FN1 and C2 CXCR4+ fibroblasts as potential therapeutic targets.

title: Unlocking the Secrets of the Endometrium: Stem Cells, Niches and Modern Methodologies
summary:
# Summary: Endometrial Stem Cells, Niches, and Reproductive Dysfunction

Endometrial stem and progenitor cells (ESCs) residing in the basalis layer drive cyclic endometrial regeneration and repair, a process tightly controlled by fluctuating estrogen and progesterone levels throughout the menstrual cycle. Dysregulation of these stem cell populations—including SSEA-1+ epithelial progenitors and stromal mesenchymal stem cells—is increasingly linked to endometrial pathologies including endometriosis, implantation failure, and endometrial carcinoma. The Wnt/β-catenin and Notch signaling pathways maintain stem cell function within specialized niches, while aberrant stem cell activity in ectopic lesions promotes glandular disorganization, stromal fibrosis, and chronic inflammation through activation of T cells, B cells, and M2 macrophages. Single-cell transcriptomics has identified stem-like subpopulations (LGR5+, SOX9+, N-cadherin+) with heightened plasticity capable of epithelial-mesenchymal transition, which may contribute to the pathogenic progression from physiological regeneration to pathological conditions. SSEA-1+ epithelial progenitors demonstrate therapeutic potential, outperforming standard mesenchymal stem cells in reducing fibrosis and enhancing regeneration in intrauterine adhesion models, highlighting the clinical relevance of understanding endometrial stem cell biology in treating reproductive dysfunction.

title: Diversity of Menstrual Cycle, Formation of Decidual Cells, and Lack of Endometrial Glands in Spiny Mouse
summary:
# Summary

The Egyptian spiny mouse (Acomys cahirinus) exhibits a menstrual-like uterine cycle distinct from typical laboratory mice, with progesterone-driven decidualization of stromal cells during the late secretory phase that correlates with endometrial preparation for potential embryo implantation. Despite forming well-defined decidual cells in response to elevated progesterone levels, spiny mice notably lack visible menstrual bleeding and erythrocytes in vaginal specimens, distinguishing them from humans despite similar hormonal regulation. The spiny mouse endometrium differs structurally from conventional laboratory strains by lacking endometrial glands but featuring epithelial depressions, significantly higher density of nerve fibers, and spiral-like arteries penetrating into the myometrial layer. These unique anatomical features, combined with spontaneous decidualization and scar-free endometrial healing, position Acomys cahirinus as a valuable model organism for investigating endometrial stromal renewal mechanisms and understanding how menstrual cycle regulation relates to conditions like endometriosis and reproductive dysfunction. The heightened nerve fiber density in spiny mice is particularly relevant to endometriosis pathology, as nerve density serves as a diagnostic marker for this condition. This model organism bridges the gap between non-menstruating laboratory rodents and humans, offering insights into the molecular patterns governing normal menstrual cycle function and potential therapeutic targets for female reproductive disorders.

title: The Role of the Cell Surface Heparan Sulfate Proteoglycan Syndecan-3 in Breast Cancer Pathophysiology
summary: Syndecan-3 (SDC3), a heparan sulfate proteoglycan, is significantly upregulated in breast cancer tissue and serves as a positive prognostic marker, as high SDC3 expression correlates with improved relapse-free survival. SDC3 depletion impairs critical cancer cell behaviors including viability, cell-cycle progression, migration, and three-dimensional spheroid formation in breast cancer models. The protein regulates expression of matrix metalloproteinases (MMP1, MMP2, MMP9) and controls E-cadherin and VEGFA levels, with SDC3-dependent signaling involving Src kinase activation. Beyond breast cancer, SDC3 dysregulation is associated with multiple malignancies including ovarian cancer, pancreatic cancer, renal cell carcinoma, glioblastoma, and cutaneous melanoma. These findings suggest that SDC3 functions as a key regulator of cancer cell behavior and could represent a therapeutic target, though its role appears context-dependent across different cancer types.

title: Differential Expression of Complement Pathway Components in Unexplained Infertility Versus Male Factor Infertility: Insights from an Exploratory Pilot Study
summary: This pilot study reveals distinct alterations in complement pathway components between unexplained infertility (UI) and male factor infertility (MFI) cohorts, highlighting potential endometrial and ovarian dysfunction mechanisms. Women with UI demonstrated significantly reduced levels of key complement proteins including properdin, C3a anaphylatoxin des arginine (C3adesArg), C4, and C8 compared to the MFI group, with these differences showing statistical significance. These lower complement levels may compromise critical reproductive functions: reduced properdin and C3adesArg impair endometrial receptivity and vascularity respectively, while deficient C8 affects endometrial integrity. Additionally, the study identified novel metabolic correlations in UI patients, with properdin negatively correlating with HDL-c and C8 positively correlating with thyroid markers, suggesting complement dysregulation may be linked to systemic metabolic and endocrine dysfunction. The complement system's established roles in endometrial receptivity, oocyte maturation, angiogenesis, and tissue remodeling, combined with these observed deficiencies, propose that impaired alternative pathway activation may constitute an important pathophysiologic mechanism in unexplained infertility distinct from male factor causes.

title: The Contribution of CD26-Negative Fibroblasts to Endometrial Scarring
summary:
# Summary

CD26 (dipeptidylpeptidase 4) plays a critical role in endometrial scarring and fibrosis, yet the endometrial stroma naturally maintains low CD26 expression, which may contribute to its characteristic scarless healing. Interleukin-1 alpha (IL1α) induces CD26 expression in endometrial stromal cells, leading to increased collagen type 1 alpha 1 (COL1A1) secretion, enhanced cell migration, and elevated stromal cell viability—all pro-fibrotic responses. In contrast, CD26 activity reduces transforming growth factor-beta 3 (TGF-β3), a factor essential for scarless fetal wound healing, suggesting that CD26 promotes fibrotic outcomes while suppressing regenerative mechanisms. Pharmacological inhibition of CD26 using diprotin A effectively blocks the IL1α-induced fibrotic cascade, suppressing collagen production, cell migration, and wound closure in vitro. These findings suggest that the absence of CD26 in the endometrial stromal compartment is a protective mechanism against scarring, and that CD26 inhibition represents a potential therapeutic strategy for preventing endometrial fibrosis and conditions like Asherman's syndrome associated with intrauterine scarring.

title: Mast Cells, Pancreatic Stellate Cells, and Telocytes in Chronic Pancreatitis: Ultrastructural Study
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pancreatitis leads to progressive pancreatic fibrosis and dysfunction through coordinated actions of mast cells, pancreatic stellate cells, and telocytes. Inflammatory reactions activate pancreatic stellate cells to differentiate into myofibroblasts, which actively produce extracellular matrix components including collagen, fibronectin, and laminin. Mast cells play a central role by degranulating and directly activating stellate cells, fibroblasts, and myofibroblasts while also interacting with nerve fibers and endothelial cells to drive fibrosis and pain pathogenesis. Telocytes, which normally form a three-dimensional supportive network maintaining tissue homeostasis, undergo structural destruction during chronic pancreatitis, including plasmalemmal ruptures, telopod shortening, and nuclear fragmentation, with their processes becoming trapped by excessive collagen deposition. The study identifies dysregulation of collagen synthesis and degradation, elevated mast cell numbers correlating with fibrosis severity and pain syndrome development, and disruption of the protective telocyte network as key mechanisms in chronic pancreatitis progression. These findings suggest that telocyte abnormalities are associated with multiple fibrosis-related pathologies, potentially relevant to endometrial dysfunction in reproductive disorders.

title: Subtype- and Site-Specific Innervation of Melanocytic Nevi as Revealed by PGP 9.5 and CGRP Expression
summary: This study investigates the innervation patterns of melanocytic nevi, benign skin tumors arising from neural crest-derived melanocytes, using neuronal markers PGP 9.5 and CGRP. The authors demonstrate that innervation density varies significantly by nevus histological type and anatomical location, with junctional nevi (located at the dermal-epidermal junction) exhibiting substantially higher nerve fiber density than compound or intradermal nevi, which reside in deeper dermal layers with naturally sparser innervation. Head and neck nevi show markedly elevated PGP 9.5-positive nerve fiber density compared to trunk and limb lesions, reflecting the known rich innervation of facial skin, while CGRP-positive peptidergic sensory fibers remain uniformly distributed regardless of nevus type or location. These peptidergic fibers, which promote vasodilation, mast cell activation, and neurogenic inflammation, appear to maintain consistent involvement across all nevi. The findings suggest dynamic interactions between melanocytic nevi and the peripheral nervous system through neurotrophic factors and neuropeptides, with innervation density correlating weakly but significantly with patient age, supporting the hypothesis that nerves play an important functional role in the nevus microenvironment.

title: A spatial transcriptomics dataset of the endometrium from repeated implantation failure patients
summary: This spatial transcriptomics study identifies key endometrial dysfunctions contributing to repeated implantation failure (RIF) by mapping gene expression patterns across distinct endometrial niches and cell types. Abnormal gene expression and impaired endometrial receptivity are strongly associated with RIF, with immune dysregulation emerging as a critical mechanism through abnormal immune responses, pathological immune infiltration, and imbalances in immune cell populations that collectively disrupt normal endometrial function. The analysis identified seven spatially distinct endometrial niches characterized by specific molecular signatures: Niche1 exhibits complement and extracellular matrix genes (CFD, C1R, DCN); Niche2 shows receptivity markers (PAEP, CXCL14, CLDN4); Niche3 is defined by stress-response and antimicrobial factors (SPP1, CLU, GPX3); while additional niches express genes related to tissue remodeling (MMP26 in Niche5), immune regulation (VTCN1, LGR5 in Niche6), and smooth muscle function (ACTA2, RGS5 in Niche7). Comprehensive cellular characterization reveals multiple endometrial cell populations including epithelial subsets (ciliated and unciliated), fibroblasts, immune cells (T cells, B cells, NK cells, macrophages), and endothelial cells, each with distinct molecular signatures that collectively shape the receptive endometrial environment. These spatial transcriptomic findings establish a foundational resource for understanding how dysregulation of specific endometrial niches and cell types drives RIF pathology, potentially enabling development of diagnostic biomarkers and targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: Importance of B cells (Review)
summary: B cells play critical roles in immune regulation through multiple mechanisms including humoral immunity via antibody production, antigen presentation, and cytokine secretion, all of which contribute to adaptive immune responses. The interactions between B cells and T cells are particularly important, and disrupting B cell-mediated antigen presentation represents a potential therapeutic target for immune dysregulation. B cell-targeted therapies have demonstrated clinical efficacy in treating autoimmune diseases, including lupus nephritis, suggesting that modulating B cell function can effectively suppress pathologic immune responses. Given that endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure often involves autoimmune or immune dysregulation mechanisms, inhibiting B cell-mediated immune activation through targeted therapies may represent a novel approach to restoring reproductive competence. These findings highlight the therapeutic potential of B cell-directed interventions in conditions where aberrant humoral and cellular immunity contribute to tissue dysfunction and reproductive pathology.

title: Single-particle analysis of small extracellular vesicles from human follicular fluid unveils immunomodulatory PD-L1+ subpopulations and potentially fertility biomarkers
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes immunomodulatory small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) in human follicular fluid, revealing that PD-L1+ sEVs represent a distinct subpopulation preferentially associated with the tetraspanin CD9. These PD-L1+ sEVs suppress T-cell immunity by inducing T-cell exhaustion and inhibiting proliferation, suggesting an important immune-regulatory role within the ovarian microenvironment. The findings demonstrate that PD-L1/CD9 co-expression is highly consistent across patients with low heterogeneity, positioning it as a potential fertility biomarker. Additionally, genetically engineered EVs overexpressing PD-L1 and Galectin-9 have shown therapeutic potential in preserving ovarian function and suppressing T-cell infiltration in models of primary ovarian insufficiency. These results establish follicular fluid sEVs as key mediators of immune tolerance and reproductive success, with implications for understanding endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure and developing new fertility diagnostics and treatments.

title: The chicken chorioallantoic membrane model for human surgeries and implants
summary:
# Summary

The chicken chorioallantoic membrane (CAM) has emerged as a versatile in vivo model system for diverse biomedical applications, leveraging its extensive vascular network, accessibility, and physiological relevance to human tissues. The CAM's well-developed three-layered structure facilitates investigations of vascular development, angiogenesis, and drug delivery, making it particularly valuable for screening angiogenesis-regulating compounds such as methylated resveratrol derivatives and curcumin-capped nanoparticles, as well as testing vascular-disrupting agents for cancer therapy. Beyond oncology, the CAM model has proven instrumental in surgical technique evaluation, xenograft implantation studies (including idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis models), neuroblastoma research involving angiogenic mediators like IGFBP-2, and gynecological cancer investigations encompassing ovarian, kidney, prostate, and bladder cancer engraftment. The model successfully evaluates drug delivery systems, biomaterial biocompatibility, photodynamic therapy applications, and toxicity assessments while supporting mechanistic studies of key signaling pathways, particularly VEGF-mediated lymphatic and blood vessel formation regulated by molecules such as Everolimus. Despite its broad applicability across tissue engineering, regenerative medicine, and developmental biology, the CAM model carries limitations related to potential species-specific immunotoxicity concerns when evaluating human-targeted therapeutics.

title: Lysophosphatidic acid promotes endometrial decidualization in recurrent implantation failure patients by regulating LPAR6
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Lysophosphatidic acid (LPA) is identified as a pivotal metabolite that promotes endometrial decidualization through the LPAR6 receptor, a pathway critically impaired in recurrent implantation failure (RIF) patients. In RIF endometria, LPAR6 expression is significantly downregulated while LPA-degrading enzymes (PPAP2B and PPAP2C) are upregulated, creating a permissive environment for impaired decidualization. LPA treatment of human endometrial stromal cells stimulates decidualization by upregulating IGFBP1 and prolactin through LPAR6 signaling, promoting cytoskeletal reorganization and the transcriptional changes necessary for decidual cell transformation. Notably, blocking LPAR6—but not the related LPAR1 receptor—abolishes LPA-mediated decidualization and suppresses expression of these decidualization markers, demonstrating LPAR6's selective role in this process. The receptor's expression increases in the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle and is regulated by estrogen and progesterone, suggesting hormonal coordination of this pathway. Restoring LPAR6 function or LPA availability may therefore represent a therapeutic strategy to improve endometrial receptivity in RIF patients.

title: Applications and challenges of 3D printing in female reproductive system research
summary:
# Summary

This review demonstrates the expanding applications of 3D printing and bioprinting technologies in modeling and treating female reproductive disorders, particularly endometrial dysfunction. Three-dimensional bioprinting techniques enable tissue engineering, drug delivery, and disease modeling across multiple reproductive tissues, including ovarian, uterine, endometrial, and placental systems. In ovarian tissue engineering, 3D-printed scaffolds with optimized geometric angles promote follicle survival and growth, with GelMA-based bioinks and adipose-derived stem cells significantly enhancing ovarian function through improved neoangiogenesis and granulosa cell proliferation. For endometrial regeneration, 3D-printed hydrogel scaffolds loaded with human-induced pluripotent stem cell-derived mesenchymal stem cells and growth factors like G-CSF restore endometrial histomorphology, improve receptivity, and facilitate embryo implantation while suppressing fibrosis. These technological advances show particular promise in addressing endometriosis-related complications and reproductive failure by regenerating damaged tissues and restoring critical reproductive functions, though clinical translation and optimization of scaffold design remain ongoing challenges.

title: Morphological features of serosa‐associated lymphoid clusters of the rat parietal pleura: Exploring a relatively unexplored system
summary: This study characterizes serosa-associated lymphoid clusters (SALCs) in the rat parietal pleura, identifying a previously underexplored immune surveillance system that mirrors better-known structures like the omental milky spots. SALCs are secondary lymphoid structures located throughout serosal membranes—particularly in the retropericardial pleural folds, adipose streaks, pulmonary ligaments, and ventral mediastinal pleura—where they monitor body cavity fluid and initiate immune responses. These clusters contain a consistent composition of T cells, B cells, and macrophages, though their morphological arrangement varies across four distinct subtypes: solitary (immune cells only), vascular (lining blood vessels), adipose (surrounding adipose tissue), and protruding (extending into the pleural cavity). SALCs function as bidirectional immune conduits, absorbing and monitoring serosal fluid while recruiting circulating immune cells to body cavities during immune challenges, and notably serve as sites where B1 cells develop and contribute to baseline IgM production. The study reveals marked heterogeneity in SALC distribution, with highest abundances in right and left retropericardial pleural folds and minimal presence in diaphragmatic and costal pleura. These findings establish pleural SALCs as functionally important immune structures with potential implications for understanding both protective immunity and pathological conditions such as serosa-associated metastatic disease.

title: Serum response factor is essential for endometrial function and prevention of inflammatory fibrosis
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
summary: Serum response factor (SRF) is essential for maintaining endometrial function and preventing pathological inflammation and fibrosis relevant to endometriosis-related infertility. SRF regulates critical endometrial processes including stromal cytoskeletal remodeling and decidual growth response while suppressing epithelium-linked innate inflammation and endometrial fibrogenesis. SRF expression is significantly reduced in the endometrium of infertile endometriosis patients compared to controls. Conditional knockout of SRF in mice (Srfd/d) results in complete infertility due to implantation failure and severe endometrial pathology characterized by massive immune cell infiltration, stromal fibrosis, aberrant collagen deposition, and dysregulation of decidualization genes. SRF-deficient stromal fibroblasts display increased expression of fibrogenic genes (collagen types I and VI, TGFβ pathway components) alongside suppression of decidualization-critical genes, while SRF-deficient epithelial cells overexpress estrogen-inducible innate inflammatory mediators. These findings establish SRF as a critical protective factor whose loss in endometriosis leads to a cascade of cytoskeletal dysfunction, aberrant immune activation, and fibrogenic remodeling that culminates in reproductive failure.

title: Modeling the window of implantation: insights from endometrial biopsy and menstrual blood-derived organoids and endometrial stromal cells
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that menstrual blood-derived organoids and endometrial stromal cells (MB-ESCs) effectively model the window of implantation and endometrial receptivity, offering a non-invasive alternative to endometrial biopsy sampling. When exposed to hormonal stimulation mimicking the menstrual cycle, MB-organoids develop key structural and functional features of receptive endometrium, including glycogen accumulation, pinopode formation, and expression of decidualization markers such as IGFBP-1, ZBTB16, and glycodelin A. Progesterone signaling emerges as central to this process, as evidenced by the suppression of decidualization markers and glycogen secretion when the progesterone antagonist mifepristone blocks progesterone receptor binding. The findings highlight the physiological relevance of these ex vivo models for investigating implantation failure and endometrial dysfunction, with potential applications in personalized medicine, disease modeling, and drug screening for conditions affecting the window of implantation.

title: Comparative analysis of paraffin and JB-4 embedding techniques in light microscopy
summary:
# Summary

This study compared JB-4 (glycol methacrylate) and paraffin embedding techniques for light microscopy visualization of liver and bone tissues. JB-4 embedding demonstrated superior preservation of cellular ultrastructure across both tissue types, including enhanced visualization of nuclei, nucleoli, Kupffer cells, chondrocyte lacunae, and osteocyte nuclei compared to standard paraffin methods. A key advantage of JB-4 is its water-soluble nature, which effectively preserves hydrophilic molecules such as glycogen and prevents the need for harsh chemical clearing agents like xylene, thereby minimizing heat-related tissue damage during polymerization at low temperatures. The study challenges the conventional assumption that JB-4 is incompatible with standard histological stains, demonstrating robust compatibility with acid fuchsin and toluidine blue staining protocols. Furthermore, JB-4's capacity to produce ultra-thin and semi-thin sections enables superior cytological detail in specialized tissues, particularly bone marrow cells and structures requiring undecalcified bone specimens. These findings expand the utility of JB-4 embedding as a versatile alternative to paraffin for tissues where preservation of cellular detail and water-soluble components is critical.

title: HOXA10-TWIST2 antagonism drives partial epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition for embryo implantation
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that HOXA10, a transcription factor essential for uterine receptivity, maintains epithelial identity in the endometrium by directly activating epithelial gene networks while repressing the EMT regulator TWIST2. Loss of HOXA10 derepresses TWIST2 and induces a partial epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (pEMT) state characterized by increased cell motility, elevated N-cadherin expression, and reduced E-cadherin levels—phenotypes necessary for successful embryo implantation. Integrated CUT&RUN and transcriptomic analyses demonstrate that HOXA10 directly regulates EMT-related genes and binds to regions enriched in motifs for implantation-related transcription factors including estrogen receptors, STAT proteins, and SMAD factors, with target genes associated with VEGF, AMPK, Hippo, and TGF-β signaling pathways. Importantly, TWIST2 knockdown restores epithelial identity but impairs implantation, establishing the HOXA10-TWIST2 antagonistic circuit as a critical regulator of pEMT and epithelial remodeling. Aberrant HOXA10 expression is associated with multiple reproductive pathologies including endometriosis, endometrial cancer, and recurrent implantation failure. These findings mechanistically link endometrial dysfunction to dysregulation of a fundamental transcriptional circuit governing controlled epithelial plasticity during implantation.

title: Uterine Stroma-Derived Tumors and the Extracellular Matrix: A Comparative Review of Benign and Malignant Pathologies
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial stromal tumors arise from hormone-responsive connective tissue and display a spectrum of malignancy with clinical outcomes ranging from benign endometrial stromal nodules (near-zero mortality) to undifferentiated uterine sarcomas (75% mortality), with intermediate low-grade and high-grade endometrial stromal sarcomas showing mortality rates of less than 10% and up to 67.2%, respectively. The extracellular matrix plays a central regulatory role in tumor progression, controlling cell proliferation, invasion, and angiogenesis through modulation of collagens, proteoglycans, and glycosaminoglycans, with matrix metalloproteinases facilitating stromal permeation and myometrial invasion. Lower-grade tumors retain hormone responsiveness and focal collagen deposition with near-normal stromal architecture, whereas higher-grade tumors lose estrogen and progesterone receptor expression and acquire aggressive chromosomal rearrangements such as YWHAE-NUTM2. The tumor microenvironment, particularly tumor-associated macrophages, carcinoma-associated fibroblasts, and endothelial cells, actively promotes malignancy through secretion of growth factors including TGF-β, hepatocyte growth factor, and SDF-1, which drive epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and metastasis via integrin-mediated signaling pathways including Rho/ROCK, MAPK, and PI3K/Akt cascades.

title: Spatial Distribution of Senescent Cells and Their Proximity to Immune Subsets in the Human Endometrium During the Implantation Window
summary:
# Summary

Senescent cells display heterogeneous spatial distribution throughout the endometrium during the implantation window and establish selective proximity patterns with immune subsets that may govern endometrial receptivity. Senescent cells are characterized by irreversible growth arrest and a senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) that modulates local inflammation, immunity, and extracellular matrix organization—processes critical for endometrial remodeling and decidualization. Macrophages and monocytes position closest to senescent cells (45 ± 20 μm), while natural killer cells and T cells maintain intermediate distances, and CD4+ T helper cells and B cells show exclusionary separation, suggesting senescent cells actively organize the endometrial immune landscape. Excessive or dysregulated senescence, coupled with inefficient clearance by immune cells, impairs endometrial receptivity and is implicated in recurrent implantation failure and pregnancy loss. The spatial positioning of immune subsets relative to senescent niches—rather than cell counts alone—correlates with implantation outcomes and may represent a novel positioning-based biomarker for assessing endometrial readiness. These findings suggest that dysregulated senescence-immune crosstalk contributes to endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure, with potential clinical applications for improving implantation prediction and pregnancy outcomes.

title: Intravital calcium imaging of meningeal macrophages reveals niche-specific dynamics and aberrant responses to brain hyperexcitability
summary:
# Summary

Meningeal macrophages, resident immune cells in the brain's protective membranes, exhibit distinct calcium signaling dynamics that are essential for maintaining brain homeostasis and mounting appropriate neuroinflammatory responses. Using intravital calcium imaging in awake mice, the study reveals that meningeal macrophages are organized into functionally distinct subsets: perivascular macrophages show prolonged calcium events and are mechanically coupled to dural blood vessel dynamics, while most macrophages display intracellular calcium signal propagation and synchronized activity driven by external signals rather than direct cell-to-cell communication. In response to cortical spreading depolarization—a pathological brain hyperexcitability event underlying migraine, stroke, and traumatic brain injury—the majority of meningeal macrophages show a persistent decrease in calcium activity, whereas a smaller subset exhibits acute or sustained increases mediated by CGRP/RAMP1 signaling from sensory neurons. Blocking RAMP1 specifically inhibits the persistent calcium elevation but not acute responses, indicating that CGRP-expressing sensory neurons selectively drive prolonged macrophage activation during brain hyperexcitability through the CGRP/RAMP1 axis. These findings establish meningeal macrophages as niche-specific regulators of neuroimmune homeostasis whose aberrant calcium signaling may contribute to inflammation-related neurological disorders.

title: The influence of menstrual cycle and endometriosis on endometrial expression of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT)-related genes
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) gene expression across the menstrual cycle in women with and without endometriosis. The researchers found that endometriosis is associated with altered expression of key EMT regulators, including increased levels of EMT-inducing transcription factors (SNAIL and ZEB families) and decreased expression of E-cadherin, which normally maintains epithelial cell adhesion. During the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle, SNAI2 expression increases substantially in both control and endometriotic tissue, while miR200 family members that typically suppress EMT-promoting factors decrease. Notably, women with endometriosis exhibited significantly elevated N-cadherin expression and reduced TGFB1 mRNA compared to controls, suggesting an EMT-permissive cellular environment that facilitates the invasiveness, adhesiveness, and reduced apoptosis characteristic of ectopic endometrial lesion formation. These molecular changes in endometrial epithelial and stromal cells appear to contribute to endometriosis-associated infertility, dysmenorrhea, and pelvic pain.

title: Mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition of perivascular cells contributes to endometrial re-epithelialization
summary:
# Summary

Nestin+ perivascular cells function as multipotent progenitors that actively contribute to endometrial re-epithelialization through mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition.
