Notch signaling maintains these cells in a quiescent state adjacent to blood vessels, but estrogen—acting through estrogen receptor alpha (ESR1)—suppresses Notch signaling to activate perivascular cells and promote their differentiation into epithelial cells. During endometrial regeneration following menstruation and pregnancy, a subset of Nestin+ perivascular cells migrate toward the epithelial layer while gradually losing perivascular markers, with approximately eight percent of newly regenerated epithelial cells originating from this perivascular population. Disorders of endometrial regeneration are closely associated with severe reproductive pathologies including infertility, endometriosis, adenomyosis, endometrial hyperplasia, and endometrial cancer, highlighting the clinical significance of understanding perivascular cell contributions to uterine epithelial homeostasis. This work identifies a critical estrogen-regulated mechanism through which perivascular stem cell activation drives endometrial repair, providing insights into potential therapeutic targets for reproductive dysfunction.

title: NPS neurons receive extensive input from auditory brainstem nuclei
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals an unexpected anatomical organization of neuropeptide S (NPS)-producing neurons located in the rostral-lateral parabrachial nucleus and semilunar nucleus, which receive extensive direct input from auditory brainstem structures including the inferior colliculus, cochlear nucleus, superior olivary complex, and nucleus of the lateral lemniscus. The Nps gene encodes neuropeptide S, which signals through its receptor NPSR1 to modulate multiple physiological processes including locomotion, anxiety, arousal, and food intake. NPS neurons project widely to brain regions controlling circadian function, arousal states, and threat responses, including the paraventricular nucleus of the thalamus, bed nucleus of the stria terminalis, and dorsomedial hypothalamus, with parabrachial NPS neurons promoting wakefulness while those in the central pontine gray promote sleep. Notably, NPSR1 has been implicated in anxiety, asthma, and endometriosis, with a rare gain-of-function variant reducing sleep, suggesting the NPS system may represent a novel nexus between sensory processing and homeostatic regulation. The substantial convergence of auditory brainstem input onto NPS neurons indicates an unexpected role for auditory information in controlling fundamental physiological functions beyond hearing.

title: Development of a Synthetic Hydrogel to Foster Microvascularization of an Endometriosis Microphysiological System
summary: Researchers developed a vascularizable completely synthetic extracellular matrix (v-CS-ECM) designed to overcome limitations of existing biomaterials like fibrin and Matrigel for endometriosis modeling. The v-CS-ECM supports the morphogenesis of perfusable microvasculature and enables co-culture of endometrial epithelial organoids with fibroblasts, critical cell types involved in endometriosis pathology. When all relevant cell types were co-encapsulated within the material in a microfluidic system, the v-CS-ECM fostered the formation of microvascularized endometriosis lesion-like structures, demonstrating its capacity to recapitulate key features of the disease. Vessel formation within this system is influenced by the ECM's biochemical and biophysical properties, the source of vascular cells, and the microphysiological system operating conditions. This synthetic hydrogel platform represents a significant advance for developing more reproducible and functionally robust in vitro models of endometriosis that incorporate the vascular component essential to disease progression and treatment testing.

title: Mechanical Cues Regulate Estrogen and Progesterone-Induced Nascent ECM Deposition by Human Endometrial Stromal Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how mechanical and biochemical cues regulate extracellular matrix (ECM) deposition by human endometrial stromal cells during decidualization, a process essential for endometrial remodeling and embryo implantation. The researchers used metabolic labeling with azide-modified sugars to track nascent ECM deposition, including collagen, laminin, and fibronectin, while culturing endometrial stromal cells on hydrogels of varying stiffness. Both estrogen and progesterone induced decidualization, characterized by F-actin stress fiber formation, prolactin secretion, and increased ECM deposition. Notably, softer hydrogels (5 kPa, mimicking healthy tissue) enhanced hormone-induced ECM and fibronectin deposition more effectively than stiffer gels (15 kPa), suggesting that mechanical properties critically regulate hormonal responsiveness. The findings demonstrate that increased tissue stiffness—characteristic of endometrial pathologies such as adenomyosis and endometrial cancer—may impair the proper decidualization response needed for successful pregnancy. These results establish that both mechanical and biochemical cues work synergistically to govern endometrial stromal cell function and ECM remodeling during the reproductive cycle.

title: Little women: the relevance and reliance on mouse models for mammary gland research and next steps for translation
journal: Journal of Mammary Gland Biology and Neoplasia
summary:
# Summary

Mouse models provide essential insights into mammary gland development and function because the mouse mammary gland shares fundamental structural similarities with the human breast, including a bilayered epithelium composed of luminal and basal cells, along with immune, endothelial, and fibroblast populations within the adipose-rich stroma. Both species exhibit cyclic reproductive phases that influence breast biology, though humans possess specialized terminal ductal lobular units with distinct collagen-rich stroma that are absent in mice. Key developmental processes such as mammary bud induction and lineage specification are regulated by transcription factors like TBX3 and BMP4, with mutations in TBX3 associated with ulnar-mammary syndrome affecting multiple tissues including the breast. Mouse models have been instrumental in elucidating how mammary epithelial stem and progenitor cells undergo lineage restriction and how hormones orchestrate alveologenesis in preparation for lactation. However, human-derived organoid systems and 3D culture methods have emerged as complementary approaches to overcome limitations of traditional cell lines, which lose luminal cell populations and hormone receptor expression in standard 2D culture, enabling better investigation of cellular organization and differentiation states relevant to breast development and disease.

title: Defects in the DNA Damage Response of Patient-derived Endometriosis Stromal Cells
summary:
# Summary

The endometrium undergoes rapid proliferation and decidualization during each menstrual cycle, a process that generates replication stress and DNA damage requiring proper cellular responses to maintain tissue integrity. This study reveals that menstrual stromal cells (MenSCs) from endometriosis patients harbor a defective DNA damage response (DDR), including reduced senescence despite accumulation of 53BP1-nuclear bodies—markers of unrepaired DNA lesions—compared to healthy controls. When exposed to replication stress induced by hydroxyurea treatment, endometriosis MenSCs abnormally activate their DDR while simultaneously downregulating critical components of the MRN complex (RAD50 and NBS1), proteins essential for recognizing and repairing double-stranded DNA breaks. Notably, endometriosis stromal tissue similarly shows reduced expression of RAD50 and NBS1, and untreated endometriosis MenSCs display increased colony-forming capacity that is abolished by replication stress. These defects in DNA damage recognition and repair machinery suggest that compromised genomic surveillance in endometriosis stromal cells may contribute to the pathology underlying the disease's association with chronic pelvic pain and infertility.

title: SpheroidSync as edge cutting transfer strategy for uniform and robust MCF7 spheroids in 3D culture
summary:
# Summary

The SpheroidSync (SS) method represents an improved edge-cutting transfer strategy that produces uniform, viable MCF7 breast cancer spheroids superior to conventional hanging drop and agarose gel approaches. SS spheroids maintain exceptional viability (95% at day 2, 92% at day 7) and exhibit the characteristic three-layer architecture of tumors, with proliferating outer cells, quiescent middle layers, and a necrotic core defined by nutrient and oxygen gradients. Critically, the SS method successfully enriches cancer stem cells (CSCs) characterized by elevated CD44, ALDH1, and HIF-1α expression alongside reduced CD24, which correlates with the decline in esterase activity as cells transition toward stemness. Since CSCs drive tumor growth, recurrence, and metastasis, the robust SS spheroid system provides a superior in vitro model for investigating CSC biology and testing therapeutic interventions targeting these aggressive cell populations responsible for treatment resistance and disease progression.

title: Transcriptional coregulator ZMIZ1 modulates estrogen responses that are essential for healthy endometrial function
summary:
# Summary

ZMIZ1 is a transcriptional coregulator highly expressed in endometrial stromal cells that plays a critical role in modulating estrogen signaling through interaction with estrogen receptor α (ESR1). The protein regulates multiple transcription factors including AR, SMAD, NOTCH, and TP53, and is essential for normal endometrial function, as ZMIZ1 disruption impairs both cell proliferation and decidual differentiation in cultured endometrial stromal cells. In vivo, genetic ablation of Zmiz1 in mice results in infertility, reduced ovulation, decreased progesterone levels, inability to mount a decidual response, and accelerated uterine fibrosis, accompanied by decreased expression of cell cycle regulators (E2F, CCNA2, FOXM1) and progesterone receptor. Notably, ZMIZ1 expression is reduced in endometrium from patients with endometriosis and is downregulated during decidualization, yet ZMIZ1 is frequently mutated in endometrial and uterine cancers, highlighting its dual role in preventing malignant transformation while supporting normal reproductive function.

title: Microengineered patient-derived endometrium-on-a-chip for the evaluation of endometrial receptivity and personalised translational medicine
summary:
# Summary

This study develops a patient-derived vascularized endometrium-on-a-chip (EoC) platform that recapitulates the dynamic microenvironment of the human endometrium during the window of implantation. The researchers demonstrate that endometrial receptivity depends on coordinated expression of key molecular markers, including integrin αvβ3 and osteopontin, which mediate embryo adhesion through trophoblast-endometrial interactions, as well as progesterone-regulated angiogenic responses involving VEGF-driven vascular formation. The EoC successfully models hormone-responsive changes across the estrous cycle, including increased angiogenic sprout formation and elevated expression of endometrial receptivity markers during the receptive phase. To enable personalized assessment, the team developed an endometrial receptivity scoring system (ERS2) that integrates molecular profiling of integrin αvβ3, osteopontin, and angiogenic features to evaluate individual patient endometrial health and implantation potential. This patient-derived organ-on-chip approach allows real-time monitoring of cell-to-cell interactions and maternal-fetal communication, offering a translational platform to identify endometrial dysfunction in repeated implantation failure and pregnancy loss cases and to optimize therapeutic interventions such as progesterone timing or platelet-rich plasma treatment.

title: Endometrial Injury and Its Rescue by Mesenchymal Stem Cells Is Dependent on Estrous Cycle Phase
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary: Endometrial injury in the diestrus (secretory) phase causes infertility through fibrosis and scarring, mimicking Asherman syndrome in humans, whereas injury in the estrus (proliferative) phase has negligible effects on fertility. Bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells (BM MSCs) effectively treat endometrial injury and restore fertility by recruiting immune cells, particularly CD45+ leukocytes, to sites of damage rather than differentiating into endometrial cells themselves. This repair mechanism operates through immunomodulation, where BM MSCs upregulate expression of chemokines (CCL3), cytokines (IL-1β), and matrix metalloproteinases (MMP3) to facilitate inflammatory recruitment and tissue remodeling. The estrous cycle phase critically determines both injury severity and stem cell therapeutic efficacy, with estrogen and progesterone driving endometrial regeneration capacity and stem cell recruitment, while decidualization in the secretory phase impedes regeneration. These findings demonstrate that BM MSC treatment significantly reduces time to conception following diestrus-phase injury through paracrine immunomodulatory mechanisms rather than direct cell replacement, providing a phase-dependent therapeutic approach for endometrial dysfunction-related infertility.

title: Cellulose Plant-Derived Scaffolds as a Tool for Myometrium Modeling
summary: This study demonstrates that decellularized green onion leaf scaffolds provide an effective platform for three-dimensional myometrial smooth muscle cell (SMC) modeling by offering unidirectional cell alignment that mimics native tissue architecture. Mouse myometrial SMCs cultured on these plant-derived cellulose scaffolds proliferated, maintained viability for up to 2.5 months, and retained expression of key contractile markers including α-smooth muscle actin, smooth muscle myosin heavy chains, calponin, transgelin, connexin-43, and oxytocin receptor. The unidirectional orientation facilitated by the scaffold's natural microarchitecture is critical because proper muscle cell alignment directly supports contractile function, which is essential for normal uterine contractions required during menstruation, labor, and delivery. Aberrant myometrial contractions are associated with significant reproductive pathologies including preterm birth, dysfunctional labor, and infertility, while myometrial dysfunction contributes to conditions such as adenomyosis and uterine fibroids. By preserving the differentiated SMC phenotype and preventing the phenotypic switching toward a synthetic state that occurs in traditional two-dimensional cultures, this three-dimensional cellulose scaffold model offers a promising tool for studying myometrial biology and potentially developing therapeutics for endometrial dysfunctions underlying reproductive failure.

title: Endometrial Signatures of Subfertility in Beef Heifers Reveal Dysregulation of MAPK Signaling and Ciliary Function
summary:
# Summary

RNA-Seq analysis identified 894 differentially expressed genes in the endometrium of subfertile beef heifers, with significant dysregulation of MAPK and Rap1 signaling pathways alongside impaired immune response regulation. Key transcriptional regulators including DUSP2, DUSP10, and MAPK13 were downregulated in subfertile animals, suggesting disrupted signal transduction and cellular homeostasis. Beyond signaling dysfunction, ciliary structure and function were markedly compromised, with dysregulated expression of ciliary genes including DNAH2, DNAI2, DNAAF4, CCDC65, and the master regulator FOXJ1, which showed increased network connectivity in the subfertile group. Additional pathways associated with subfertility included TNF, VEGF, and IL-17 signaling, with altered expression of immune mediators such as IL1B, IL15, and PTGS2. These findings indicate that endometrial subfertility in heifers results from convergent dysfunction in multiple biological systems—specifically defective MAPK signaling, impaired ciliary motility, and dysregulated immune responses—all of which likely compromise the endometrial environment necessary for successful embryo implantation and pregnancy establishment.

title: Therapeutic Applications of 3D Bioprinting in Surgery for Female Reproductive Tract Disorders: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Three-dimensional bioprinting has emerged as a promising therapeutic approach for treating diverse pathologies of the female reproductive system, including endometrial dysfunction, premature ovarian insufficiency, intrauterine adhesions, and congenital anomalies. The technology primarily utilizes Fused Deposition Modeling to fabricate hydrogel-based scaffolds derived from structural proteins of the extracellular matrix, which are crosslinked through UV exposure or calcium chloride treatment. Bioprinted constructs targeting ovarian tissue have demonstrated the capacity to restore hormone production including estrogen, progesterone, and follicle-stimulating hormone, while endometrial patches have shown improved embryo implantation success and tissue integration in murine models. Additionally, uterine and vaginal scaffolds promote wound healing and tissue regeneration while achieving mechanical properties similar to native tissue. Though current literature shows high in vitro cell viability and promising preclinical outcomes, the field remains in early stages with limited clinical translation and ongoing optimization needed to fully replicate native tissue properties and achieve clinical efficacy in human applications.

title: Establishment of immortalized ovarian stromal cell lines using Sendai virus vectors: a platform for studying tumor–stroma interactions and carcinogenesis
summary:
# Summary

This study established immortalized ovarian stromal cell lines using Sendai virus vectors encoding human telomerase reverse transcriptase, BMI1, and SV40 large T antigen, creating a platform to study tumor-stroma interactions in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer. The immortalized stromal cells exhibited characteristic fibroblast morphology with expression of stromal markers (vimentin, MMP1, PAPPA) while lacking epithelial markers (keratin), and maintained proliferative capacity beyond 25 passages. Importantly, stromal cells derived from ovarian endometriomas displayed early chromosomal instability (including X-chromosome loss and 1;9 translocations) and significant upregulation of extracellular matrix remodeling genes (MMP1, PAPPA) and paracrine signaling molecules (CXCL1, GREM1) that promote angiogenesis, matrix remodeling, and immune modulation. These molecular alterations are consistent with cancer-associated fibroblast phenotypes that support ovarian cancer progression through multiple mechanisms including FGF7-FGFR2-HIF1α signaling, versican-hyaluronan-CD44 interactions driving peritoneal dissemination, and WNT5A-mediated cancer stemness. The findings establish endometriosis-derived stromal cells as having oncogenic potential and provide a valuable in vitro model for investigating the molecular mechanisms linking endometriosis to ovarian carcinogenesis.

title: Linking the Warburg effect to endometrial receptivity: metabolic parallels in embryo implantation
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals striking metabolic parallels between the Warburg effect and successful embryo implantation, centering on aerobic glycolysis as a central regulator of endometrial receptivity. Blastocysts and trophoblasts produce high lactate levels through Warburg-like glycolysis, establishing a pro-receptive, acidic microenvironment that facilitates implantation. Glycolysis directly regulates critical endometrial receptivity genes (MRAP2, BCL2L15) and implantation-supporting cytokines (IL-1, LIF, TGF-β), with hormonal control through estrogen and progesterone, which modulate glycolytic enzymes GLUT1 and PFKFB3. The HIF-1α/HIF-2α signaling axis drives this metabolic program, with HIF-2α activating the Oct-4/Fgf-4 pathway essential for embryo viability and regulating MMP-9 transport for endometrial remodeling. Multiple traditional herbal compounds and formulas (Paeoniflorin, Rg1, Rg3, WSYXD, KLP) enhance endometrial receptivity by modulating this glycolytic-inflammatory network, upregulating adhesion molecules and hormone receptors, and improving endometrial repair through antioxidant and angiogenic pathways. These findings suggest that metabolic dysfunction in endometrial glycolysis may underlie receptivity defects in reproductive failure, offering new therapeutic targets for improving implantation outcomes.

title: Induction of menstruation in mice reveals the regulation of menstrual shedding
summary:
# Summary

This study describes the development of the first transgenic mouse model of menstruation (X-Mens), which recapitulates the anatomical and cellular hallmarks of human menstrual shedding through chemogenetic control of endometrial signaling. The researchers found that progesterone elevation induces decidualization—the transformation of endometrial fibroblasts into specialized decidual cells—through increased calcium signaling in epithelial cells and elevated cAMP in stromal fibroblasts, which can be artificially controlled via DREADD agonist treatment. Progesterone withdrawal triggers menstrual shedding, during which the superficial functionalis layer detaches from the underlying basalis layer along a plane defined by distinct fibroblast subtypes and concentric rings of developmentally staged decidual cells expressing maturation markers like Cdh3, IGFBP1, and Wnt signaling components. The basalis layer, comprising fibroblast subtype eFib1 and glandular epithelial cells, is preserved during menstruation, enabling scarless endometrial regeneration—a unique physiological feature disrupted only when basalis damage occurs. This spatially organized fibroblast differentiation paradigm reveals how pre-menstrual endometrial layering determines the extent and location of tissue shedding, providing new mechanistic insights into menstruation and potential therapeutic targets for endometrial disorders.

title: Enhancing female fertility by biomaterial-based regeneration of uterine tubes
summary:
# Summary

Tubal factor infertility, which accounts for approximately 35% of female infertility cases, represents a major clinical challenge since the uterine tubes are essential for oocyte transport, fertilization, and early embryo development. While in vitro fertilization remains the gold standard treatment, tissue engineering and regenerative medicine (TERM) approaches offer promising alternatives through biomaterial-based regeneration strategies. The review evaluates diverse biomaterial scaffolds—including collagen-based matrices, decellularized extracellular matrix, and synthetic polymers (polylactic acid, polyglycolic acid, polycaprolactone)—which support critical cellular functions such as attachment, migration, differentiation, and neovascularization. Specialized scaffolds like gelatin/polycaprolactone electrospun membranes promote endometrial regeneration by upregulating estrogen receptor α and downregulating tumor necrosis factor, while alginate and hyaluronic acid-based hydrogels prevent adhesion formation through anti-inflammatory properties. Advanced approaches including three-dimensional bioprinting, organoid development, and Apelin-13-loaded scaffolds for oviductal recanalization demonstrate the evolving capacity to regenerate damaged tubal tissue caused by inflammation, hydrosalpinx, or ectopic pregnancy. These TERM strategies, combining stem cells, bioactive molecules, and tunable biomaterials, represent a transformative approach to restore female fertility by regenerating functional uterine tube architecture.

title: The menstrual cycle is a vital sign across the lifespan
journal: The Lancet. Obstetrics, gynaecology, & women's health
summary:
# Summary: The Menstrual Cycle as a Vital Health Indicator

The menstrual cycle functions as an underutilized but powerful vital sign for assessing both gynecological and general health throughout a woman's lifespan. Menstrual cycle characteristics can serve as predictive biomarkers for chronic disease development later in life, making regular cycle monitoring a clinically valuable screening tool. By tracking menstrual patterns and abnormalities, healthcare providers can identify early indicators of reproductive dysfunction and systemic health conditions before they progress. This approach highlights the importance of integrating menstrual cycle assessment into routine clinical practice as a fundamental component of preventive health care. Recognition of the menstrual cycle's role as a health indicator has significant implications for understanding endometrial dysfunction and reproductive failure, as cycle irregularities may reflect underlying pathophysiological mechanisms.

title: Pathophysiologic implications and therapeutic potentials of telocytes in multiorgan fibrosis
summary:
# Summary

Telocytes are specialized stromal cells critical for maintaining tissue homeostasis, regeneration, and stem cell niche function across multiple organ systems including the heart, lungs, kidneys, reproductive organs, and gastrointestinal tract. Loss or structural degeneration of telocytes is associated with pathological fibrosis in systemic sclerosis, endometriosis, and other multiorgan fibrotic conditions, where telocyte depletion leads to aberrant fibroblast activation and excessive extracellular matrix remodeling. Mechanistically, healthy telocytes suppress fibroblast proliferation and differentiation through secreted factors that downregulate pro-fibrotic genes including collagen I, ACTA2, and CTGF, while telocytes contribute to stem cell maintenance by delivering paracrine signals such as Wnt ligands and R-Spondin 3. Therapeutically, telocyte transplantation and administration of telocyte-derived extracellular vesicles and conditioned medium effectively mitigate fibrosis progression and reduce pathological matrix deposition. These findings establish telocytes as key mediators of tissue fibrosis and highlight their considerable therapeutic potential for treating fibrotic disorders across multiple organ systems.

title: Integrative bioengineering strategies for endometrial regeneration: From biomaterials and stem cells to organoids and organ-on-a-chip technologies
summary:
# Summary

The endometrium undergoes cyclical regeneration regulated by hormones, but injury to the basal layer—which contains essential stem and progenitor cells—leads to intrauterine adhesions and thin endometrium, both major causes of infertility. Bioengineering approaches using collagen-based scaffolds, particularly type III collagen hydrogels and composite materials incorporating mesenchymal stem cells, effectively promote endometrial regeneration by activating pro-angiogenic pathways (MEK/ERK1/2, VEGF), suppressing pro-inflammatory signaling (NF-κB, TGF-β), and modulating immune responses through macrophage polarization toward anti-inflammatory phenotypes. Mechanistically, collagen engages discoidin domain receptors (DDR1/DDR2) and integrin pathways while suppressing inflammatory mediators, thereby enhancing decidualization markers and restoring endometrial thickness and glandular density. Advanced platforms including endometrial organoids, organ-on-chip systems, and 3D bioprinting technologies, combined with stem cell-derived extracellular vesicles and AI-assisted monitoring, represent emerging transformative approaches for disease modeling and personalized regenerative therapies. These integrated bioengineering strategies collectively restore endometrial function and fertility potential through coordinated tissue regeneration, angiogenesis, and immune modulation.

title: Identification of novel differentiation trajectories and gene network associations with ectopic pregnancy in fallopian tube epithelium
summary: This study identifies a novel OVGP1+ epithelial progenitor population in fallopian tube epithelium that undergoes bidirectional differentiation into both mature secretory and ciliated cells, supporting the tissue's critical role in gamete transport, fertilization, and early embryo development. Single-cell transcriptomic analysis reveals that fallopian tube secretory cells are enriched for lipid metabolism and immune-related gene pathways, which may influence the periconceptual environment. The research identifies multiple genes—including MUC1, SLC7A2, CLDN1, GLS, PEX6, PLXNA4, NR2F1, CLGN, PGGHG, and ANKRD36—associated with ectopic pregnancy susceptibility, implicating fallopian tube epithelial dysfunction as a key pathogenic mechanism. While ciliated epithelial cells show transcriptomic similarities between fallopian tube and endometrium, secretory cell populations remain distinctly different between tissues, suggesting tissue-specific functional specialization. Tubal fluid produced by secretory cells influences embryo metabolism and DNA methylation, establishing the fallopian tube epithelium as a specialized microenvironment essential for reproductive success. These findings elucidate the cellular and molecular basis of fallopian tube dysfunction in reproductive failure and ectopic pregnancy pathogenesis.

title: Nociceptive Nerve‐Derived CGRP Exacerbates Uterine Fibrogenesis in Adenomyosis by Promoting CD140b+ CD146+ Fibroblast Differentiation
summary: Adenomyosis is characterized by excessive myometrial fibrosis and dysmenorrhea that impairs fertility through mechanisms involving nociceptive nerve-derived calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP). CGRP, secreted by overabundant nociceptive nerves enriched in adenomyotic lesions, activates the ERK signaling pathway in CD140b+ CD146+ vascular fibroblasts expressing high levels of the CGRP receptor component RAMP1, promoting their differentiation into pro-fibrotic CHI3L1+ fibroblasts that drive extracellular matrix deposition. Genetic or pharmacological ablation of nociceptive nerves significantly reduced myometrial fibrosis, ectopic lesion infiltration, and associated pain behaviors in adenomyosis models. The FDA-approved CGRP/RAMP1 antagonist rimegepant similarly reduced adenomyosis-associated fibrosis and improved fertility outcomes by blocking this neuro-fibroblast axis. These findings establish a pathogenic role for nociceptive nerve-fibroblast crosstalk in adenomyosis and validate CGRP pathway inhibition as a potential therapeutic strategy for preserving fertility in affected patients.

title: Mechanobiology and the extracellular matrix in pulmonary fibrosis
summary:
# Summary

Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis develops through a mechanobiological process initiated by chronic epithelial damage and involving coordinated dysfunction of epithelial cells, fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and immune cells. The extracellular matrix undergoes pathological reorganization characterized by excessive collagen deposition and increased stiffness, which paradoxically perpetuates fibrosis by reprogramming normal fibroblasts toward a pathological phenotype. Matrix stiffness activates the mechanotransduction pathway involving YAP/TAZ nuclear translocation and myofibroblast differentiation, a process enhanced by TGFβ signaling and mediated through mechanosensors like Rho/ROCK and Piezo channels. Fibroblastic foci, enriched in collagen I/III, fibronectin, and myofibroblasts expressing alpha-smooth muscle actin, represent highly active sites of fibrosis inception and progression whose abundance correlates with mortality. Two approved anti-fibrotic therapies, pirfenidone and nintedanib, slow disease progression, though restoration of physiologically soft matrices can deactivate IPF fibroblasts, suggesting that matrix mechanical properties represent a critical therapeutic target.

title: Endometrial Stromal Cells from Endometriosis Patients Reflect Lesion-Type-Specific Heterogeneity
summary: Endometrial stromal cells (ESCs) from endometriosis patients exhibit distinct functional and molecular signatures that vary by lesion type, reflecting the heterogeneous nature of the disease. ESCs play central roles in endometriosis development through multiple mechanisms, including secretion of pro-inflammatory factors, promotion of angiogenesis, and altered hormonal crosstalk that supports ectopic tissue survival and growth. Notably, ESCs from endometriotic lesions show reduced proliferation but markedly increased migratory capacity, with the most pronounced effect in endometrioma-derived cells, which express elevated integrins (αvβ3) and cytokeratins (C18/C19) that facilitate fibrotic processes and tissue infiltration. In contrast, ESCs from superficial and deep lesions display increased contractility involving altered collagen metabolism and reduced CD90+ fibroblasts, features associated with tissue scarring and pain perception. Gene expression analyses reveal lesion-type-specific alterations in cell division, migration, and extracellular matrix organization pathways, with elevated estrogen receptor β across all lesion types. These findings demonstrate that endometriosis-associated ESC heterogeneity, driven by distinct molecular profiles and functional phenotypes, underlies the variable clinical presentations and pathophysiological mechanisms across different endometriotic lesion types.

title: Distribution and Neurochemical Characterization of Dorsal Root Ganglia (DRG) Neurons Containing Phoenixin (PNX) and Supplying the Porcine Uterine Cervix
summary: Phoenixin (PNX) is a neuropeptide localized in dorsal root ganglia sensory neurons innervating the uterine cervix, where it is predominantly co-expressed with pain-related neurotransmitters including calcitonin gene-related peptide (80.9%), substance P (77.9%), and neuronal nitric oxide synthase (34%). Beyond its role in sensory transmission, PNX plays a critical regulatory function in reproductive physiology by activating GPR173 receptors to promote ovarian follicle development, granulosa cell proliferation, and luteinizing hormone release through upregulation of gonadoliberin receptor mRNA. Clinically relevant to endometrial dysfunction, serum PNX levels are significantly reduced in endometriosis patients, suggesting PNX involvement in disease pathogenesis and pelvic pain exacerbation, while elevated PNX has been observed in polycystic ovary syndrome and precocious puberty. The finding that endometrial cells upregulate SMIM20 (the PNX precursor) in response to low PNX levels indicates a compensatory feedback mechanism. These results establish PNX as a multifunctional neuroendocrine peptide linking sensory innervation, reproductive hormone regulation, and endometrial pathology, with potential therapeutic applications for treating endometriosis and associated epithelial dysfunction.

title: DNA-based cell typing in menstrual effluent identifies cell type variation by sample collection method: toward noninvasive biomarker development for women’s health
summary:
# Summary

Menstrual effluent represents a noninvasive biospecimen for monitoring women's reproductive health, including endometriosis and fertility outcomes, as it contains the shed uterine lining and reflects endometrial biology. DNA methylation-based cell typing methods, particularly HiTIMED deconvolution, successfully identified distinct cell type compositions across three common menstrual sample collection methods: menstrual cups captured the highest proportions of immune cells including neutrophils, monocytes, natural killer cells, and CD4+ T cells, while vaginal swabs yielded predominantly epithelial and stromal cells, and menstrual pads captured a balanced mixture of immune, epithelial, and stromal populations. Collection method significantly influenced methylation class membership and cell type proportions, indicating that sampling approach must be standardized for biomarker development. Menstrual cup samples were particularly enriched for dendritic cells and B cells, reflecting the inflammatory milieu of menstruation in which myeloid immune cells and leukocytes increase to facilitate endometrial tissue degradation and repair. Menstrual pad collection emerged as the most promising approach for future research as it is truly noninvasive, requires no insertion, and captures diverse cell types reflecting both epithelial and immune components of menstrual effluent. These findings establish the methodological foundation for developing menstrual effluent-based biomarkers to investigate inflammatory conditions such as endometriosis and other reproductive disorders.

title: Uterine collagen deposition fluctuates throughout the estrous cycle and provides a scaffold for gland visualization via the SHG-casting method
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that fibrillar collagen deposition in the uterine endometrium fluctuates dynamically throughout the estrous cycle, with significantly elevated levels during the estrus phase when estrogen peaks and the endometrium is maximally receptive. Endometrial fibroblasts produce this fibrillar collagen-rich extracellular matrix, which forms a distinctive scaffold surrounding uterine glands—structures essential for pregnancy that are absent or dysfunctional in conditions like endometriosis and infertility. The researchers developed a novel SHG-casting method using second harmonic generation microscopy to label-free visualize the fibrillar collagen cage as a three-dimensional template for reconstructing uterine gland morphology, revealing variable gland structures including characteristic lobular morphology during estrus and involution in later phases. The fibrillar collagen cage displays approximately 10-fold higher signal intensity than surrounding stroma and significantly increases in thickness and intensity during the estrus phase compared to proestrus and metestrus phases. This collagen-rich extracellular matrix creates mechanical tension between epithelial cells and the stromal environment, which is critical for supporting gland branching morphogenesis and proper endometrial remodeling across reproductive cycles. The findings establish that cyclic changes in endometrial collagen deposition are fundamental to uterine health and suggest that defects in this dynamic extracellular matrix remodeling may contribute to reproductive pathologies including endometriosis and infertility.

title: Reframing the significance of menstruation: evolutionary insights from an organismal-relational perspective
journal: History and Philosophy of the Life Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This article reframes menstruation through an evolutionary and organismal-relational lens, demonstrating that menstrual cycles represent complex physiological systems shaped by both developmental and ecological factors rather than simple reproductive byproducts. The menstrual cycle is intrinsically linked to spontaneous ovulation and uterine decidualization, with inflammatory processes serving essential functions beyond reproduction, including tissue repair and endometrial renewal through the action of decidual cells. Menstrual pain and uterine contractions reflect functional mechanisms for cavity clearance and may indicate fertility status, paralleling the mechanisms involved in childbirth. The article emphasizes that developmental constraints and processes actively drive evolutionary outcomes, arguing that organisms are not passive subjects of natural selection but active agents that construct their environments and shape selective pressures. This organismal-relational perspective challenges conventional evolutionary frameworks by highlighting how internal developmental mechanisms and organism-environment interactions fundamentally influence the evolution and physiology of reproductive traits.

title: Single‐cell transcriptome analyses reveal disturbed decidual microenvironment in women of advanced maternal age
summary: Advanced maternal age (AMA) disrupts the decidual microenvironment through a TGF-β-dependent mechanism centered on the loss of PRR15, a novel decidual stromal cell regulator. PRR15 deficiency in AMA unleashes hyperactive TGF-β/SMAD signaling, which drives fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transition, extracellular matrix overproduction, and decidualization failure while simultaneously suppressing critical negative feedback inhibitors of TGF-β signaling. The altered decidual composition in AMA includes expanded fibroblasts with elevated pro-fibrotic gene expression (COL1A1, COL6A1, SFRP2, FN1) and contracted immune populations (reduced macrophages and NK cells), coupled with impaired decidual stromal cell differentiation marked by downregulation of decidualization markers (IGFBP1, PAEP, PRL) and metabolic suppression. Functionally, decidual stromal cells from AMA women exhibit elevated migration, TGF-β signaling, and collagen production while displaying reduced decidualization capacity and oxidative phosphorylation. TGF-β receptor inhibition completely abrogates myofibroblast differentiation and restores normal fibroblast function, confirming the therapeutic potential of blocking this pathway to restore the decidual microenvironment and improve pregnancy outcomes in women of advanced reproductive age.

title: Generation and characterization of a mouse model of conditional Chd4 knockout in the endometrial epithelium
summary: This study generated and characterized a conditional knockout mouse model with epithelial-specific loss of CHD4, a chromatin remodeling protein and component of the NuRD complex that regulates transcriptional repression, DNA damage response, and cell cycle progression. CHD4 plays critical roles in endometrial homeostasis by maintaining cell identity and epithelial integrity through chromatin remodeling, and mutations or loss of CHD4 function are associated with endometrial pathologies including endometriosis, hyperplasia, and carcinoma, where CHD4 functions as a tumor suppressor. Using the BAC-Sprr2f-Cre transgenic line to drive Cre recombination specifically in the endometrial epithelium, the authors achieved variegated CHD4 loss predominantly in the luminal epithelium. Surprisingly, despite CHD4's known role as a tumor suppressor and its frequent mutations in aggressive uterine serous carcinoma, mice with conditional Chd4 knockout in the endometrial epithelium maintained normal endometrial histology, normal fertility, and normal litter sizes over a six-month breeding trial, with no changes in markers of proliferation, apoptosis, or epithelial integrity. These findings suggest that epithelial-specific CHD4 loss alone is insufficient to drive endometrial dysfunction or tumorigenesis, implying that additional genetic alterations or mutations in cooperating genes such as TP53, PIK3CA, or PTEN may be required for CHD4-associated endometrial carcinoma development.

title: Atosiban-conjugated 3WJ-pRNA nanoparticles delivering GAS1-enhanced extracellular vesicles: targeting the decidua to combat recurrent miscarriage
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that GAS1, a glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored protein highly expressed in decidual stromal cells, serves as a critical regulator of extracellular vesicle (EV) biogenesis through modulation of the RAB39B pathway. GAS1 deficiency is associated with multiple pregnancy pathologies including recurrent miscarriage, recurrent implantation failure, and endometriosis, whereas GAS1-enriched EVs promote embryonic adhesion, trophoblast migration, and angiogenesis—all essential processes for successful pregnancy. The researchers developed atosiban-conjugated three-way junction pRNA nanoparticles to deliver GAS1-enhanced EVs specifically to the decidua, exploiting the oxytocin receptor pathway dysregulated in recurrent miscarriage. This targeted therapeutic approach effectively restores decidual microenvironment organization and reduces fetal resorption rates in a murine recurrent miscarriage model, offering a novel non-invasive treatment strategy for women with pregnancy loss. The strategy leverages both the natural healing properties of cell-derived EVs and rational nanoparticle engineering to overcome endometrial dysfunction at the maternal-fetal interface.

title: Single-cell transcriptomics reveals notch regulation in quiescent LEPR⁺ endometrial mesenchymal stem cells
summary: Single-cell transcriptomics reveals a distinct population of LEPR+ endometrial mesenchymal stem cells (eMSCs) that occupy a quiescent, primitive state at the root of the eMSC differentiation trajectory and exhibit superior clonogenic and self-renewal capacity compared to bulk eMSCs. These quiescent LEPR+ eMSCs are predominantly arrested in the G0 phase of the cell cycle and express high levels of Notch receptors, positioning them as highly communicative cells within the endometrial niche. Notch signaling, particularly through its ligands JAG1 and DLL1, plays a critical role in maintaining both the LEPR+ phenotype and quiescence of these cells by inhibiting differentiation and promoting self-renewal, while the RBP-Jκ nuclear factor is essential for transducing Notch signals. The quiescent state is dynamically regulated, as these eMSCs can resume the cell cycle in response to microenvironmental cues from Notch and Wnt signaling, suggesting that Notch-mediated control of LEPR+ eMSC homeostasis is contextually responsive. These findings establish Notch signaling as a therapeutic target for manipulating LEPR+ eMSC function in endometrial regeneration, with implications for addressing endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure.

title: Proteome Analysis of Daily Urine Samples of Pregnant Rats Unveils Developmental Processes of Fetus as Well as Physiological Changes in Mother Rats
summary:
# Summary

Daily urinary proteome analysis in pregnant rats serves as a non-invasive biomarker reflecting both fetal developmental stages and maternal physiological adaptations throughout pregnancy. Early pregnancy (days 2-7) is characterized by enrichment of cell division processes involving proteins such as Chmp and Vps family members, coinciding with blastocyst formation and early embryonic cell divisions. By day 8-9, organ development becomes prominent, particularly nervous system and vascular morphogenesis, alongside critical developmental events including gastrulation, placentation, and mesodermal differentiation. During mid-to-late pregnancy, blood coagulation and complement activation pathways are progressively upregulated to prevent hemorrhage and defend against pathogens at the fetal-maternal interface, while fibrinolysis activation around day 10 prevents pathologic thrombosis. The late pregnancy phase (days 16-18) shows marked enrichment in lactation-related proteins (Prl family members) regulated by progesterone withdrawal and sustained by prolactin and oxytocin. These findings demonstrate that urinary proteome profiling can effectively monitor pregnancy progression and identify dysregulation of key biological processes that, when impaired through mechanisms like placental dysfunction, maternal nutritional imbalance, or endocrine disorders, may lead to adverse pregnancy outcomes.

title: Do combined oral contraceptives induce formation of tissue factor?
journal: Research and Practice in Thrombosis and Haemostasis
summary: Combined oral contraceptives increase thrombosis risk 2-6 fold through alterations in coagulation profiles, though the specific role of tissue factor remains complex. While estrogen in COCs upregulates tissue factor mRNA in monocytes and increases monocyte TF presentation, this study found no significant increase in extracellular vesicle-bound tissue factor, procoagulant phospholipids, or extracellular vesicle concentration following COC use. COCs do appear to induce a more thrombogenic coagulation profile through activation of monocytes and endothelial cells, which may produce tissue factor-bearing extracellular vesicles, and additionally promote low-grade inflammation. The disconnect between increased monocyte TF expression and unchanged circulating EV-TF levels suggests that COC-induced thrombosis risk operates through multiple mechanisms beyond extracellular vesicle-mediated tissue factor transfer. Second-generation COCs containing lower estrogen doses show reduced thrombotic risk, and smoking further elevates monocyte TF presentation in COC users, indicating that individual susceptibility factors modulate the prothrombotic effects of oral contraceptives.

title: Stochastic Nature of Fascia: From Layered Pedagogical Artifact to Morphogenetic Reality in Clinical Anatomy
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the structural and functional role of hyaluronic acid (HA) within fascia, establishing it as a key molecular regulator of tissue properties and cellular behavior. HA, primarily synthesized by fasciacytes and fibroblasts through HAS2 expression, maintains tissue hydration, viscoelasticity, and gliding capacity—properties that scale with movement demands across different anatomical regions. The article reveals that HA concentrations are highest in mobile tissues such as synovial fluid and deep fascia of the lower leg, supporting their biomechanical functions. A critical finding is that HA promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) through the calcium-HAS2 axis, enabling cell migration and tissue remodeling essential for development, healing, and regenerative processes. The work establishes fascia not as an inert structural scaffold but as an active biomolecular integrator where stochastic cellular behavior and deterministic mechanical forces dynamically regulate HA production to meet physiological demands.

title: Development of polypeptide scaffold for anti-endometrial invasion and ordered myometrial recovery in uterine diverticulum
summary:
# Summary

Uterine diverticulum, a common complication following cesarean section characterized by abnormal uterine bleeding and impaired fertility, results from defective wound healing complicated by local ischemia, infection, and ectopic endometrial tissue invasion. Single-cell RNA sequencing analysis revealed that diverticulum tissues contain aberrant endometrial glands with dysregulated immune infiltration (elevated T cells, granulocytes, and myeloid cells) and altered fibroblast populations, particularly reduced diverticulum-associated fibroblasts that normally express the smooth muscle marker ACTA2. The researchers developed a novel polypeptide scaffold (BSBs@exo@poly) composed of aligned silk fibroin channels with agarose cross-linking that effectively suppressed ectopic endometrial invasion, promoted organized myofiber reconstruction, enhanced angiogenesis, and reduced inflammation in a rat model of uterine injury. Key molecular mechanisms involve the MT-RNR1/MOTS-c pathway, which upregulates type I collagen production through TGFB/SMAD signaling, and fibroblast-endothelial cell communication mediated by collagen and integrin families. This innovative biomaterial scaffold represents a promising therapeutic approach for preventing uterine diverticulum development and restoring normal uterine structure and fertility.

title: Metabolomic analysis of the endometrium of Large White and Meishan pigs reveals differences in biological processes during late gestation
summary:
# Summary

This metabolomic study reveals breed-specific differences in endometrial metabolite profiles during late gestation that correlate with perinatal outcomes in pigs. The endometrium, which is crucial for providing nutrients to the fetus and supporting fetal maturity, shows distinct metabolic signatures in Large White versus Meishan breeds. Large White pigs exhibit reduced glucogenic amino acids (aspartate, glutamate, glutamine), decreased glucose and glycogen availability, and impaired glutathione metabolism leading to oxidative stress, alongside reduced protein metabolism and cellular homeostasis. Conversely, accumulation of hypoxanthine and succinate in the endometrium suggests activation of hypoxic catabolism with downstream inflammatory modulation, while ferroptosis signaling pathways become enriched, potentially contributing to increased cell death. These metabolic dysfunctions in Large White endometrium are associated with elevated perinatal mortality compared to Meishan pigs, suggesting that endometrial metabolic competence—particularly maintenance of amino acid availability, antioxidant capacity, and energy metabolism—is critical for fetal survival and postnatal growth.

title: A comparative review of single-cell atlases: mapping cellular diversity across species and tissues
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary:
# Summary

Single-cell sequencing technologies have revolutionized biomedical research by enabling comprehensive analysis of cellular heterogeneity, gene regulation, and tissue architecture at unprecedented resolution, surpassing the limitations of traditional bulk RNA sequencing approaches. Comprehensive single-cell atlases, including the Human Cell Atlas and Mouse Cell Atlas, serve as foundational frameworks for exploring cellular diversity, gene regulatory networks, and cross-tissue comparisons within and across organisms. These atlases have proven particularly impactful in oncology and infectious disease research, where scRNA-seq has revealed tumor heterogeneity, identified circulating tumor cells, and uncovered critical host-pathogen interactions, exemplified by studies demonstrating the dual roles of type I interferon responses and immune cell subsets like megakaryocytes and monocytes in COVID-19 disease progression. Emerging approaches such as spatial RNA-seq and cross-cancer comparative studies enable mapping of gene expression patterns and identification of conserved cellular states across tissues and tumors, facilitating personalized therapy development and patient stratification. Whole-organ atlases like the Human Lung Cell Atlas provide detailed references of organ structure and cellular composition in both healthy and disease states, positioning single-cell atlases as essential virtual frameworks for translating molecular phenotypic variation into precision medicine strategies.

title: Murine implantation chamber formation precedes natural and artificial decidualization
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that implantation chamber formation is a critical precursor to decidualization in mice, occurring before the uterine stromal cells undergo the mesenchymal-to-epithelial transition characteristic of decidualization. The embryo induces V-shaped implantation chambers through recognition of specific carbohydrate moieties on the uterine epithelium, but non-biological focal stimuli—including Concanavalin A-coated beads, lectins, and oil droplets—can replicate this response, indicating that physical-chemical properties rather than embryonic identity are essential for chamber formation. These implantation chambers trigger sub-epithelial PTGS2 (cyclooxygenase-2) expression and subsequent decidualization, with prostaglandins playing a critical role in this process, as demonstrated by the ability of prostacyclin and beta-adrenergic inhibitors to block decidualization. The study identifies key genes upregulated at implantation sites including Bmp2, Bmp8a, Wnt4, and Ptgs2, and reveals distinct molecular signatures between embryo-induced and artificial (bead/oil-induced) decidualization models. These findings suggest that implantation chamber formation represents a mechanistically distinct and necessary precursor event that licenses the decidualization program through hormone-dependent (progesterone and estrogen) and prostaglandin-dependent signaling pathways.

title: Abnormal zona pellucida and follicular development in ZP1-mutant macaques
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that ZP1 (zona pellucida glycoprotein-1) is essential for proper oocyte development and fertility in primates. Using ZP1-mutant cynomolgus macaques, researchers found that ZP1 mutations lead to structural defects in the zona pellucida including incomplete formation, uneven thickness, and poor organization, ultimately resulting in primary infertility and empty follicle syndrome. ZP1-deficient oocytes exhibited markedly reduced maturation rates, diminished ZP1 and ZP3 protein localization, and severely impaired developmental competence following fertilization. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that ZP1 mutations dysregulate critical mitochondrial pathways including the TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation, suggesting that ZP1 dysfunction compromises oocyte energy metabolism. These findings, supported by primate models that closely mirror human reproductive biology, establish ZP1 mutations as a significant cause of reproductive failure and suggest that ZP protein abnormalities represent an important pathogenic mechanism underlying infertility in women undergoing assisted reproduction.

title: The Impact of Heterotopic Spleen Regeneration on Tumor Growth
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that heterotopic spleen regeneration paradoxically promotes tumor growth by restoring the spleen's function as a reservoir for tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) and suppressing anti-tumor immunity. While splenectomy reduces TAM infiltration and slows tumor progression, heterotopic splenic transplantation restores normal spleen architecture and immune cell populations within 30 days, leading to enhanced macrophage recruitment to tumors and a shift toward anti-inflammatory macrophage phenotypes characterized by Arginase 1 expression. Regenerated splenic tissue stimulates early infiltration of CD4+ T-lymphocytes and B-lymphocytes while actively suppressing CD8+ T-lymphocyte responses, thereby compromising cytotoxic tumor immunity. The net result is increased tumor cell proliferation rates and accelerated tumor growth in animals with regenerated spleens compared to splenectomized controls, highlighting the spleen's critical role in supporting immunosuppressive tumor microenvironments. These findings reveal a mechanistic link between splenic regeneration and immune tolerance to malignancy, with implications for understanding post-splenectomy outcomes and tumor immunobiology.

title: Spatial Phenotype of the Mast Cell Population in Endometritis of Various Severities
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis, a condition strongly associated with infertility, implantation failure, and recurrent miscarriage, features an expanded population of mast cells (MCs) within the endometrial stroma that increases proportionally with disease severity. These MCs accumulate in focal niches and extensively interact with CD8+ cytotoxic lymphocytes, monocytes, and both type 1 and type 2 macrophages through juxtacrine and paracrine mechanisms that become progressively more direct and intense as endometritis worsens. Recruitment and expansion of mast cells is promoted by upregulation of adhesion molecules and chemotactic factors including VCAM1, CCL2, CCR1, and stem cell factor (SCF), while the MC-derived protease tryptase is deposited in the extracellular matrix where it contributes to tissue fibrosis and structural remodeling. Through their capacity to secrete multiple inflammatory mediators, mast cells play a central role in orchestrating both innate and acquired immune responses while simultaneously promoting the fibrotic transformation of endometrial tissue, thereby perpetuating the pro-inflammatory microenvironment characteristic of endometritis and its associated reproductive complications.

title: LPS-Stressed Bovine Endometrial Cells upon Morulae in a Transwell Model of Embryo—Maternal Talk
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how endometritis-induced inflammation impairs embryo development using an in vitro model of lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-stressed bovine endometrial epithelial cells co-cultured with morulae embryos. LPS stimulation mimics bacterial infection and triggered a hostile uterine environment by increasing extracellular vesicle concentration and size while simultaneously reducing galectin-9 levels and elevating leukaemia inhibitory factor secretion. The inflammatory stress significantly suppressed embryo development rates, with LPS-treated cultures showing reduced blastocyst formation by day 9 and complete failure of blastocyst hatching by day 11 compared to control cultures. These findings demonstrate that endometritis disrupts critical bidirectional communication between endometrial cells and embryos, mediated partly through altered extracellular vesicle cargo and inflammatory cytokine imbalance. The results highlight how endometrial inflammation compromises the nutrient-rich uterine environment essential for preimplantation embryonic development, providing mechanistic insight into endometritis-associated early embryonic loss in cattle and potentially other species.

title: Endometrial Collagen in Woolly Monkey (Lagothrix l. poeppiggi) and the Uakari (
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study of primate endometrial collagen composition reveals that Type I and Type III collagen are dynamically regulated throughout the reproductive cycle in coordination with hormonal changes. Type I collagen predominates in the endometrial extracellular matrix and is highest during the early proliferative phase and secretory phase, where it provides structural stability and supports cell adhesion, angiogenesis, and tissue regeneration. Estrogen and progesterone orchestrate cyclical changes in collagen expression and organization: estrogen stimulates fibroblast activity and growth factor production during proliferation, while progesterone during the secretory phase enhances transforming growth factor beta and platelet-derived growth factor production to promote collagen synthesis and fibroblast proliferation. Conversely, declining progesterone levels trigger matrix metalloproteinase production, leading to collagen degradation and endometrial remodeling in preparation for menstruation or the next cycle. These quantitative and organizational variations in collagen types are essential determinants of endometrial remodeling, decidualization, and implantation competence, with alterations in this finely-tuned system potentially contributing to reproductive dysfunction and conditions like endometriosis.

title: From Biological Waste to Therapeutic Resources: A Comprehensive Review of Stem Cell Sources, Characterization, and Biomedical Potentials
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines stem cells derived from biological waste materials as promising therapeutic resources for regenerative medicine and tissue engineering. Stem cells from diverse sources—including adipose tissue, urine, and umbilical cord blood—demonstrate robust multilineage differentiation capacity and can be efficiently isolated through enzymatic or mechanical methods. Mesenchymal stem cells and their derivatives show clinical efficacy in treating various conditions, including osteoarthritis, ischemic heart disease, autoimmune disorders, and blood cancers, with survival and recovery rates reaching 70–90% and 30–40% respectively. Notably, urine-derived stem cells and adipose-derived stem cells secrete bioactive molecules including cytokines, growth factors (VEGF, HGF, TGF-β, IGF-1), and exosomes that mediate immunomodulation, reduce oxidative stress, promote angiogenesis, and enhance tissue repair. Stem cell-derived exosomes display particular therapeutic potential, evidenced by their protective effects in diabetic nephropathy and chronic kidney disease through antioxidant activity and CD4+ T-cell inhibition. These findings establish biological waste materials as accessible, viable sources for advancing stem cell-based therapeutics across multiple disease states.

title: The nuclear and mitochondrial genomes need to tango. How is their dance synchronized during development?
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the coordinated regulation of nuclear and mitochondrial genomes during mammalian development, with particular relevance to reproductive outcomes and endometrial function. The mitochondrial genome, comprising 13 protein-encoding genes of the electron transport chain, 22 transfer RNAs, and 2 ribosomal RNAs, is maternally inherited and strictly controlled through multiple nuclear-encoded factors including transcription factors (TFAM, TFB1M, TFB2M, POLRMT, TEFM, MTERF1) and replication machinery (POLG, POLG2, TWNK, SSBP1, TOP1MT). Mitochondria exhibit cell-type specific energy metabolism patterns—rapidly dividing cells rely on glycolysis while metabolically complex cells depend on oxidative phosphorylation—and generate critical metabolic intermediates like α-ketoglutarate that epigenetically regulate DNA methylation and histone modifications during oogenesis and early development. The maternal oocyte contains over 200,000 mtDNA copies that undergo selective replication and copy number changes critical for developmental competence, with assisted reproductive technologies potentially perturbing this delicate genomic balance. Paternal mitochondria are eliminated through mitophagy-dependent mechanisms involving PARKIN and MUL1, preventing heteroplasmy and the competitive disadvantages of biparental inheritance. These findings underscore how synchronized nuclear-mitochondrial communication is essential for proper developmental progression and reproductive success, with implications for understanding endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure.

title: Decidual stromal cells drive CD16+ macrophages towards an immunoregulatory phenotype via extracellular matrix-adhesion molecule interaction during early pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

During early pregnancy, decidual stromal cells orchestrate a critical immunoregulatory dialogue with CD16+ macrophages through extracellular matrix (ECM) components, a mechanism essential for successful pregnancy establishment. Decidual stromal cells produce three key ECM molecules—collagen IV, osteopontin, and hyaluronic acid—that promote the development and immunoregulatory phenotype of CD16+ decidual macrophages by binding to their corresponding adhesion receptors (CD49a, CD44, LYVE1, and integrin αVβ3). These ECM-mediated interactions drive CD16+ macrophages toward an anti-inflammatory M2-like phenotype characterized by elevated expression of immunomodulatory markers CD209 and IDO while suppressing pro-inflammatory markers CD86 and HLA-DR. In patients with recurrent miscarriage, this protective ECM-CD16+ macrophage regulatory axis is significantly weakened, suggesting that impaired stromal-immune crosstalk contributes to early pregnancy loss. The study confirms that the ECM-adhesion molecule interaction axis represents a fundamental mechanism by which decidualization creates an immunologically permissive microenvironment necessary for embryo implantation and placental development.

title: Evidence for KISS-1 nuclear translocation and PI3K/AKT signaling in the ultrastructurally and morphometrically analyzed human endometriosis
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary: This study demonstrates that dysregulation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway, combined with nuclear translocation of KiSS-1, drives pathological changes in endometriosis. In endometrial tissue from endometriosis patients, both eutopic and ectopic regions show elevated PI3K and AKT expression with altered subcellular localization, while KiSS-1 expression is reduced and abnormally shifts to the nucleus in response to cellular stress. AKT hyperactivation promotes proliferation, invasion, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and resistance to apoptosis, while disrupted endometrial homeostasis leads to progesterone resistance and abnormal estrogen-dependent proliferation. The interaction between PI3K/AKT signaling and nuclear KiSS-1 translocation appears to function as an adaptive response to chronic inflammation and hypoxia, regulating survival and proliferation processes. Ultrastructural and morphometric analysis revealed that ectopic endometrium exhibits cellular stress markers including mitochondrial dysfunction and autophagy, alongside fibrotic tissue remodeling characterized by decreased glandular volume and increased stromal matrix deposition. These findings collectively suggest that aberrant PI3K/AKT-mediated signaling and nuclear KiSS-1 translocation are key mechanisms underlying the severe pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and subfertility associated with endometriosis.

title: IGF2BP3-dependent glutamine/BCAA metabolic rewiring rejuvenates aged human adipose-derived stem cells for enhanced tissue regeneration
summary:
# Summary

Aging impairs the self-renewal and differentiation capacity of human adipose-derived stem cells (hASCs), a decline linked to attenuation of the IGF2BP3-m6A-BCAT1/GLS metabolic axis. The RNA-binding protein IGF2BP3, enriched in the metabolically active ACTA2+TAGLN+ subpopulation of young cells, stabilizes mRNAs encoding BCAT1 and GLS, key regulators of branched-chain amino acid and glutamine metabolism that maintain redox homeostasis and mitochondrial function. Age-related reduction of this axis drives metabolic reprogramming and senescence-associated functional collapse in elderly hASCs. Remarkably, genetic restoration of BCAT1/GLS or supplementation with BCAAs and glutamine significantly rejuvenated aged hASCs, restoring proliferation, differentiation capacity, and wound-healing ability. These findings identify m6A epitranscriptomic modifications as central regulators of stem cell aging and reveal that amino acid metabolic interventions can reverse age-related senescence in mesenchymal stem cells, with potential implications for regenerative medicine applications.

title: Decrypting spatiotemporal code of human endometrial receptivity
summary:
# Summary

This study decrypts the spatiotemporal molecular code governing human endometrial receptivity throughout the menstrual cycle, identifying distinct stromal and epithelial cell populations with coordinated transcriptional programs. Key receptivity-associated genes include the stromal markers NR2F1 and CEBPD alongside epithelial markers SGK1, KLF5, and ELF3, which are tightly coordinated with the opening of the implantation window. The FGFs-FGFR2 signaling pathway mediates critical stroma-epithelium crosstalk that drives epithelial differentiation, while cholesterol metabolism promotes ciliogenesis during the receptive phase. Stromal cells undergo dynamic transformation from proliferative and undifferentiated states (marked by TOP2A/MKI67 and SFRP4/PTCH1 respectively) to differentiated states expressing ALDH1A3 and NR2F1, before progressing to degrading cells enriched in autophagy and apoptosis markers. The temporal dynamics reveal that natural killer cells significantly increase while proliferating stromal cells decrease toward the receptive window, and expression of genes like FOXO1, THBS2, and SERPING1 peaks during mid-to-late secretory phases to establish optimal conditions for embryo implantation.

title: Organoids for disease modeling and treatment: state-of-the-art
summary:
# Summary

Organoid technology represents a revolutionary breakthrough in biomedical research, enabling the generation of three-dimensional, self-organized cellular structures that faithfully recapitulate key aspects of organ development, morphogenesis, and disease progression. Human organoids can be derived from diverse cell sources including human pluripotent stem cells (hESCs and hiPSCs), adult stem cells, tissue-specific progenitor cells, and primary tissues, with the emergence of patient-specific hiPSC-derived organoids particularly enabling disease modeling and precision medicine applications. Three-dimensional organoids have been successfully developed across multiple organ systems—including brain, heart, kidney, liver, intestine, and muscle—where they recapitulate developmental processes and support pharmacological, immunological, and tumorigenesis studies. The generation and maturation of organoids depend critically on extracellular matrix components and engineered microenvironments such as Matrigel and synthetic hydrogels, which provide the necessary structural support for self-organization and cellular specification. These organoid platforms are extensively applied for drug screening, regenerative medicine, tissue engineering, and modeling human embryonic development, positioning organoid technology as a transformative tool for understanding developmental biology and advancing personalized therapeutic interventions.

title: Inflammatory proteomics of uterine fluid is potentially a non-invasive predictor for endometrial receptivity: a pilot study
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study demonstrates that inflammatory proteomics analysis of uterine fluid using the OLINK Target-96 Inflammation panel is a feasible non-invasive approach for assessing endometrial receptivity. Women with a displaced window of implantation (WOI)—a condition associated with implantation failure—exhibited significantly elevated levels of multiple inflammatory proteins including CXCL10, IL-17C, VEGFA, MMP-10, and LIFR compared to those with normal receptive endometrium. Gene set enrichment analysis revealed that the displaced WOI group showed substantial enrichment in adaptive immune response pathways, including B cell-mediated immunity and immunoglobulin-mediated responses, whereas immune-related gene expression was significantly lower in the normal WOI group. Several key receptivity-associated factors, such as LIFR and CCL20, were consistently upregulated at both protein and transcript levels in the displaced WOI group, supporting their role in endometrial dysfunction. These findings suggest that inflammatory protein signatures in uterine fluid could serve as biomarkers for identifying endometrial receptivity defects, offering potential clinical value for improving diagnosis and treatment of implantation failure in assisted reproductive technology cycles.

title: Dynamic Expression of Endometrial Adhesion G Protein-Coupled Receptors During the Menstrual Cycle and Early Mouse Pregnancy: Modulation by Ovarian Stimulation
summary:
# Summary

Adhesion G protein-coupled receptors (ADGRs) are dynamically expressed throughout the menstrual cycle and early pregnancy, with expression regulated by estradiol and progesterone to support critical reproductive processes including endometrial receptivity, decidualization, and embryo implantation. Specific ADGR members play distinct roles in reproduction: ADGRD1 is required for embryo transit through the oviduct, while ADGRG2 is involved in fluid reabsorption and sperm maturation, with reduced ADGRG2 expression characterizing a receptive endometrium in women. Ovarian stimulation used in assisted reproductive technologies induces supraphysiologic hormone levels that disrupt the normal temporal expression pattern of endometrial ADGRs, thereby impairing endometrial receptivity and potentially explaining implantation failures following fresh embryo transfer. Dysregulated decidualization, which may result from altered ADGR signaling, compromises placental formation and function, leading to adverse pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage, fetal growth restriction, preeclampsia, and preterm labor. These findings demonstrate that ADGR expression represents a critical molecular mechanism linking hormonal regulation to endometrial competence for successful pregnancy establishment.

title: Immune Cell Infiltration and Kynurenine Pathway Activation Define Early Injury and Progression in Diabetic Nephropathy
summary: This study identifies immune cell infiltration and kynurenine pathway activation as key mechanisms driving diabetic nephropathy progression across disease stages. CCL4⁺ myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) emerge as the predominant infiltrating immune population in early diabetic nephropathy, characterized by high expression of S100A9 and pro-inflammatory cytokines including CXCL2, CXCL3, CCL3, and IL-1β. The long non-coding RNA MALAT1 maintains MDSC function, with its inhibition reducing MDSC populations, suggesting a therapeutic target. Kynurenine pathway metabolites—including kynurenine, 3-hydroxykynurenine, and quinolinic acid—associate with enhanced MDSC induction and adverse renal outcomes, with kynurenase upregulation occurring during proximal tubule injury in early disease. In late-stage diabetic nephropathy, kidney macrophages undergo reprogramming toward a lipid-associated phenotype marked by elevated CD9 and TREM2 expression, accompanied by endothelial cell changes characterized by GPX3 and SPP1 expression. The identification of circulating CXCL2 and S100A9 as potential early biomarkers suggests these molecules could enable early detection and monitoring of diabetic nephropathy progression.

title: Quantifying inflammatory resolution in human menstruation reveals disease-specific failure modes and enables a non-invasive diagnostic for endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Menstruation is a coordinated inflammatory process involving immune activation, tissue breakdown, and vascular remodeling that normally resolves through a reproducible temporal cascade progressing from cycle day 1 through day 3, with healthy resolution characterized by a transition from EPAS1-dominated inflammatory breakdown to HIF1α-mediated tissue repair. Endometriosis disrupts this normal inflammatory resolution trajectory through persistent aberrant immune responses, altered tissue remodeling, and pathological rewiring of endothelial programs that remain coupled to epithelial and stromal activity rather than decoupling as occurs in healthy menstruation, creating a distinctly different transcriptional signature detectable in menstrual effluence. By analyzing the cellular composition and gene regulatory networks in menstrual effluence—a readily accessible biospecimen containing endometrial tissue, immune populations, and stromal cells—researchers developed a non-invasive Inflammatory Resolution Score that quantifies disease-specific deviations and enables classification of endometriosis with superior diagnostic precision compared to traditional approaches. The endometriosis classifier successfully detects disease status from menstrual samples and shows measurable shifts following surgical intervention, demonstrating both biological validity and potential clinical utility. This resolution biology framework reveals that endometriosis, unlike autoimmune diseases, exhibits constrained network rewiring concentrated in extracellular matrix remodeling and angiogenic pathways, highlighting a specific pathophysiological failure mode distinct from other inflammatory diseases.

title: Obesity Reduces Endometrial Receptivity by Downregulating the Ob-Rb/STAT-3 Signaling Pathway in Women and Female Mice
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies obesity-induced dysfunction of the Ob-Rb/STAT-3 signaling pathway as a central mechanism impairing endometrial receptivity and reproductive outcomes. Obese women and diet-induced obese mice exhibit elevated serum leptin levels coupled with reduced expression of the leptin receptor (Ob-Rb), phosphorylated STAT-3 (pSTAT-3), and endometrial receptivity factors including leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). These molecular changes correlate with clinically relevant fertility reductions, as obese leptin-resistant mice demonstrate pregnancy rates of only 50% compared to 90% in normal-weight controls.
