Mechanistically, leptin resensitization through dietary weight loss restores Ob-Rb/STAT-3 signaling and endometrial receptivity markers while improving pregnancy rates to 80%, whereas selective Ob-Rb inhibition phenocopies the infertile obese state. These findings establish leptin signaling dysfunction as a therapeutic target for obesity-associated reproductive failure and suggest that weight loss interventions may reverse endometrial defects underlying reduced implantation and pregnancy success in obese women.

title: Engineering AQP1-Deficient DF-1 Suspension Cells for High-Yield IBDV Production and Vaccine Scale-Up
summary:
# Summary

This study engineered chicken DF-1 cells by disrupting the AQP1 gene using CRISPR/Cas9 to create a suspension cell line optimized for infectious bursal disease virus (IBDV) vaccine production. AQP1, a transmembrane protein that regulates cell-cell adhesion and membrane dynamics, was targeted because its deletion reduces cellular adhesiveness while maintaining normal cell morphology and viability. The resulting DF-1/AQP1− suspension cells demonstrated significantly enhanced IBDV replication, producing viral titers of 9.0 log TCID50/mL compared to lower titers in wild-type and monolayer cultures. These engineered cells exhibited excellent genetic stability, maintaining the AQP1 mutation over 20 passages, and showed no tumorigenicity in immunocompromised mice, addressing biosafety concerns. This approach provides a scalable and safe platform for large-scale IBDV vaccine manufacturing by leveraging rational modification of adhesion-related genes to convert adherent cells into highly productive suspension cultures.

title: GM-CSF improves the receptivity of thin endometrium by promoting glandular and stromal cell proliferation in mice and humans
summary:
# Summary

Thin endometrium (≤7 mm) is a critical cause of infertility and pregnancy complications including recurrent loss and placental abnormalities. This study demonstrates that granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), which is naturally expressed throughout the female reproductive tract, significantly improves endometrial receptivity by increasing thickness and gland number while promoting proliferation of both glandular and stromal cells in mouse and human models. GM-CSF treatment upregulates key tissue repair and regeneration genes including SEMA5A, IGFBP-3, ABCA1, and LAMA2, while simultaneously suppressing apoptotic signaling, inflammatory responses, and programmed cell death through activation of the Wnt signaling pathway and promotion of angiogenesis and cell adhesion. In clinical outcomes, GM-CSF treatment enhanced implantation rates (24.10% versus 17.39%) and clinical pregnancy rates (34.78% versus 26.32%) compared to control groups. These findings establish GM-CSF as a therapeutic strategy to restore endometrial function and improve reproductive outcomes in patients with thin endometrium-associated infertility.

title: TRPV1: from structure to function—a multidimensional target for therapeutic advances
journal: Journal of Zhejiang University. Science. B
summary: TRPV1 is a multifunctional ion channel predominantly expressed in nociceptive neurons that plays a critical role in both physiological and pathological processes. The channel's unique capacity to respond to temperature changes, chemical ligands, and voltage fluctuations positions it as a key regulator of multiple biological systems. TRPV1 mediates diverse cellular responses essential for maintaining homeostasis and processing nociceptive signals. Its involvement in temperature sensing and homeostatic regulation suggests TRPV1 represents a potential therapeutic target for conditions involving endometrial dysfunction and reproductive failure. The broad functional significance of this ion channel across multiple biological pathways indicates that TRPV1-targeted interventions could address several aspects of reproductive pathology simultaneously.

title: Nitric Oxide Synthase Expression in Endometrium During Physiological Cycle
summary:
# Nitric Oxide Synthase Expression in Endometrium During Physiological Cycle

This study demonstrates that nitric oxide (NO), produced primarily by two nitric oxide synthase isoforms (NOS2 and NOS3), plays critical roles in regulating endometrial function throughout the menstrual cycle and is essential for successful embryo implantation. NOS3 expression peaks during the early secretory phase and supports vascular regulation and endometrial receptivity, while NOS2 expression progressively increases through the cycle, peaking in the late secretory and menstrual phases where it coordinates local immune responses and tissue remodeling. The correlation between NOS2 expression and CD56+ uterine natural killer (uNK) cells suggests that NO-mediated immunomodulation is critical for establishing immune tolerance and facilitating trophoblast invasion and vascular remodeling. Estrogen upregulates NO production, and progesterone may enhance this effect, while inhibition of nitric oxide synthase causes implantation failure—a defect that can be reversed by nitric oxide donors. Dysregulation of either NO or uNK cells is associated with reproductive disorders, and excessive NOS2 expression combined with elevated uNK activity may intensify local inflammation and impair endometrial receptivity. These findings position nitric oxide signaling as a key mechanistic target for understanding endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure.

title: Uncovering immune signatures of the endometrium in heavy menstrual bleeding: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined endometrial immune signatures in women with heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) by analyzing leukocyte populations across the menstrual cycle. The research revealed that dysregulation of endometrial immune cell numbers, particularly a reduction in CD56+ uterine natural killer (uNK) cells during the proliferative phase, is associated with HMB. Macrophages, T cells, and uNK cells normally orchestrate critical endometrial functions including tissue remodeling, angiogenesis, vascular stability, and immune tolerance; impaired expansion of uNK cells in the late secretory phase may compromise these vascular repair mechanisms and contribute to excessive bleeding. While CD4+ and CD8+ T cell counts remained similar between HMB and control groups overall, the HMB group exhibited greater variability in these populations during the secretory phase, suggesting dysregulated immune dynamics. The findings support altered uNK cell dynamics as a potential pathophysiologic mechanism in HMB, linked to abnormal endometrial vascular remodeling and defective hemostasis, highlighting the importance of immune cell coordination in maintaining normal menstrual homeostasis.

title: New models of implantation: towards a whole better than the sum of parts
summary: Recent advances in in vitro modeling have transformed our understanding of implantation by developing sophisticated blastoid and endometrial organoid systems that can now be combined to recapitulate early reproductive events. Blastoids—stem cell-derived structures resembling pre-implantation embryos—achieve implantation efficiencies approaching 90% and can enter diapause through mTOR pathway manipulation, while their developmental potential can be enhanced through careful regulation of signaling pathways including Wnt/β-catenin and growth factor responses. Endometrial models have progressed from simple cultures to complex assembloids incorporating polarized epithelium, stromal cells, and vascular components (human umbilical vein endothelial cells), with optimized extracellular matrices ranging from collagen-based gels to innovative decellularized endometrial hydrogels that better support physiological differentiation. Critical advances include the discovery that menstrual fluid provides an accessible source of endometrial cells exhibiting hormone responsiveness and decidualization capacity, and that minimal medium supplemented with specific factors including 8-bromo-cAMP and medroxyprogesterone acetate can sustain endometrial assembloid function. By integrating these embryo and endometrial models, researchers can now mechanistically investigate reproductive failures caused by both embryo mosaicism and endometrial dysfunction, ultimately addressing infertility and pregnancy loss that affect 10-18% of reproductive-age populations.

title: Thinking outside the mouse: emerging vertebrate models in biomedicine
summary:
# Summary

This review highlights emerging vertebrate models that offer unique advantages for understanding fundamental biological processes relevant to reproductive and regenerative medicine. The spiny mouse (Acomys) emerges as a particularly valuable model, being one of the few rodents that naturally menstruate and possess remarkable regenerative capacities across multiple tissues including skin, heart, kidney, and spinal cord, without scarring through specialized mechanisms involving collagen remodeling and tissue-resident macrophages. Beyond reproduction, these alternative models reveal principles of longevity, cancer resistance, and tissue resilience—naked mole-rats demonstrate stress-resistant cardiac physiology and extended fertility, while bats and other species display unique immune adaptations linked to disease resistance and extended healthspan. These diverse vertebrate systems enable investigation of menstrual biology and tissue regeneration pathways, with direct applications for understanding endometrial dysfunction and reproductive failure. The integration of comparative approaches using natural variants in regeneration, immune function, and reproductive longevity provides unprecedented opportunities to uncover conserved mechanisms that could inform therapeutic strategies for human reproductive and regenerative medicine.

title: Amniotic Mesenchymal Stromal/Stem Cell–Derived Extracellular Vesicles for Equine Chronic Degenerative Endometritis Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Equine chronic degenerative endometritis (CDE) is a progressive condition characterized by endometrial fibrosis and stromal inflammation that compromises reproductive function in older mares. The disease involves myofibroblast-mediated accumulation of extracellular matrix, dysfunction of glandular structures, and altered endometrial secretions that disrupt critical fetal-maternal communication necessary for successful pregnancy. This study investigated amniotic mesenchymal stromal cell-derived extracellular vesicles (AMC-EVs) as a therapeutic approach, demonstrating that these nanoparticles effectively transport bioactive molecules including proteins, lipids, and microRNAs with immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory properties. AMC-EV treatment resulted in pregnancy establishment in 11 of 12 treated mares, with the intervention apparently restoring functional paracrine signaling between embryo and uterine tissues despite minimal histological improvement in fibrosis classification. While two mares showed improved endometrial categorization from IIB to IIA, histological fibrosis itself was not reversed, indicating that AMC-EVs likely restored physiological communication rather than regenerating damaged endometrial architecture. These findings suggest that extracellular vesicle-based therapy may offer clinical utility for subfertile mares with CDE by compensating for compromised endometrial function rather than reversing the underlying pathology.

title: Mesothelial cells promote peritoneal invasion and metastasis of ascites-derived ovarian cancer cells through spheroid formation
summary:
# Summary

Epithelial ovarian cancer cells undergo trans-coelomic dissemination through the peritoneal cavity via ascites, a process critically dependent on their interaction with mesothelial cells. Ovarian cancer cells form aggregated cancer-mesothelial spheroids (ACMSs) with mesothelial cells present in ascites, with over 60% of cancer spheroids containing mesothelial cell components that protect against anoikis and promote invasion. Transforming growth factor-β1 stimulates mesothelial cell transformation, upregulating fascin-1 and promoting invadopodium formation while downregulating the mesothelial marker calretinin, thereby converting mesothelial cells into cancer-associated mesothelial cells with enhanced invasive capability. These ACMSs are highly invasive into both collagen and mesothelial layers through coordinated action involving matrix metalloproteinase-mediated extracellular matrix degradation and invadopodia formation. The findings demonstrate that mesothelial cells actively facilitate peritoneal metastasis by initiating invasion within these spheroid structures, establishing mesothelial cell-cancer cell crosstalk as a fundamental mechanism driving ovarian cancer dissemination and chemotherapy resistance in the abdominal cavity.

title: Ovulatory follicular fluid promotes the clonogenicity and invasion of ectopic and eutopic endometrial cells
summary:
# Summary

Ovulatory follicular fluid (FF) significantly promotes the proliferation, clonogenicity, migration, and invasion of both eutopic and ectopic endometrial cells, with particularly dramatic effects on invasion capacity (9.1-fold in eutopic cells and 4.8-fold in ectopic cells). While eutopic endometrial cells demonstrate greater overall responsiveness to FF across most measures and retain a plastic, stem-like phenotype, ectopic cells exhibit inherently higher clonogenic capacity and undergo epithelial-mesenchymal transition, adopting a fibroblast-like phenotype that may reflect adaptation to the ectopic ovarian microenvironment. The enhanced pathogenic effects of FF are attributed to its enrichment in growth factors including IGF2, HGF, and EGF-like ligands, as well as extracellular matrix proteins such as fibronectin and vitronectin. These findings establish ovulation as a critical driver of endometriotic lesion growth and progression, complementing the retrograde menstruation hypothesis by providing the conditions necessary for ectopic endometrial cell establishment and proliferation. The results suggest that ovulation-suppressive therapies such as combined or progesterone-only oral contraceptives may effectively prevent endometriosis development, and that targeting FF-mediated growth factor signaling pathways represents a promising therapeutic approach.

title: A 3D ovarian cancer metastasis model using a decellularised peritoneal matrix to study therapy response
summary: High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma spreads across the peritoneal cavity through transcoelomic metastasis, driven by peritoneal fluid dynamics and facilitated by the extracellular matrix, which critically shapes tumor phenotype and disease aggressiveness. The desmoplastic ECM promotes chemoresistance through β1 integrin and FAK signaling, while fibronectin secreted by peritoneal mesothelial cells induces platinum resistance via Akt activation. This study presents a decellularized peritoneal matrix scaffold that preserves native ECM structural integrity and composition, enabling realistic three-dimensional modeling of HGSOC growth and metastasis. The model supports assessment of both conventional chemotherapy sensitivity and novel immunotherapy responses, accounting for the complex tumor microenvironment including immune cell infiltration. Comparative analysis demonstrates structural and compositional similarities between porcine and human peritoneal matrices, validating the platform's translational relevance. This decellularized ECM-based approach provides a superior alternative to traditional organoid cultures for studying how matrix remodeling, immune dynamics, and therapy resistance interact in peritoneal carcinomatosis.

title: Hybrid endometrial-derived hydrogel and human endometrial organoids synergize for uterine regeneration in an immunocompetent murine model
summary: This study demonstrates that a hybrid hydrogel combining endometrial extracellular matrix with synthetic PuraMatrix, seeded with human endometrial organoids, effectively promotes endometrial regeneration in a mouse model of Asherman syndrome and endometrial atrophy. The hybrid hydrogel significantly improved multiple indices of endometrial health, including tissue thickness, glandular density, and vascularization, while simultaneously reducing fibrosis and ferroptosis—key pathological features of endometrial dysfunction that impair fertility. The synergistic approach leverages the biochemical signaling capacity of natural endometrial extracellular matrix components with the mechanical stability and biocompatibility of synthetic hydrogels to create an optimal microenvironment for organoid retention and tissue regeneration. Human endometrial organoids, containing epithelial and stromal stem cells, provided biological capacity for regeneration when delivered within this hybrid scaffold. Although the combined therapy substantially restored endometrial structure and function in the immunocompetent mouse model, complete fertility restoration was not achieved, potentially due to persistence of synthetic hydrogel components in the tissue. These findings suggest that hybrid bioengineered constructs combining natural and synthetic materials with cell-based therapies represent a promising regenerative medicine approach for endometrial disorders causing reproductive failure.

title: Senescence-Linked Fibrosis in the Aging Human Ovary Revealed by p16-Based Histological Profiling and Spatial Transcriptomics
summary: This spatial transcriptomics study reveals that cellular senescence, marked by p16INK4a expression, drives fibrotic remodeling in the aging postmenopausal ovary. p16-positive senescent cells accumulate primarily in stromal regions and cluster with macrophages and myofibroblasts, with expression increasing with age. Within these senescent regions, classical senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) factors including SERPINE1, complement components (CFD, C3), and extracellular matrix remodeling genes (TGM2, LAMB1, FBLN1, PCOLCE) are upregulated, while growth-promoting genes (IGFBP3, CCN5, TGFBR3) and metabolic regulators are downregulated. Pathway analysis demonstrates activation of pro-fibrotic and inflammatory programs including TNFα/NF-κB, IL-6/JAK-STAT3, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and extracellular matrix organization, accompanied by suppression of proliferative programs and translation. These findings establish senescence-mediated SASP signaling and immune crosstalk as key drivers of age-related ovarian fibrosis and dysfunction, with implications for understanding reproductive failure in aging and endometrial pathology.

title: Research note: Isolation and characterization of telocytes from the silky fowl embryonic skin
summary: This study successfully isolated and characterized telocytes from silky fowl embryonic skin, confirming their identity through positive immunofluorescence staining for the markers CD34 and vimentin. The isolated telocytes demonstrated stem-like characteristics and formed robust homocellular networks via desmosomes and gap junctions, enabling intercellular signaling and creating interlocking structures through telopodes. In native skin niches, telocytes colocalize with mast cells, macrophages, and stem cells, suggesting functional involvement in tissue regeneration and immune regulation. The vimentin expression on telocytes is notable as this marker coordinates fibroblast proliferation and keratinocyte differentiation through TGF-β-Slug signaling during wound healing. These findings support the hypothesis that telocytes play a significant role in tissue-regenerative potential and establish a stable in vitro culture system for further investigation of their biological functions in developmental and regenerative processes.

title: Single-cell mapping of human endometrium and decidua reveals epithelial and stromal contributions to fertility
summary: This single-cell transcriptomic study reveals the cellular and molecular basis of endometrial receptivity through coordinated epithelial and stromal remodeling during the menstrual cycle. Estradiol and progesterone, produced by the dominant follicle and corpus luteum respectively, bind their cognate receptors (ESR1 and PGR) to orchestrate sequential cellular changes that prepare the endometrium for implantation during the mid-secretory phase window of implantation. The authors identified a 556-gene Glandular Epithelium Receptivity Module (GERM) signature enriched in secretory glandular epithelial cells, which is significantly reduced in women with impaired fertility, establishing the critical role of glandular epithelium in supporting receptivity. Complementing epithelial changes, stromal populations shift during pregnancy to include decidualized cells marked by IGFBP1, PRL, and FOXO1 expression, alongside senescent stromal cells and increased immune infiltration (myeloid, NK, and B cells). Notably, ovarian stimulation induces dyssynchrony between endometrial glands and stroma, providing a mechanistic explanation for reduced fertility in assisted reproduction cycles and supporting the clinical utility of the Endometrial Receptivity Array for optimizing embryo transfer timing.

title: Personalized Models of Biological Barriers and Their Diseases: Recent Progress with Organs‐On‐Chips
summary: This article reviews organs-on-chips technology for modeling biological barriers and their dysfunction in disease states. The work emphasizes that barrier function depends on integrated physical and biochemical factors, including tight junctions mediating paracellular transport, transmembrane proteins controlling transcellular transport, tissue geometry, mechanical stimulation, extracellular matrix composition, and fluid shear stress. The review extensively documents how impaired barrier functions associate with neurological, gastrointestinal, metabolic, and infectious diseases, using cardiovascular disease as a detailed case study. In atherosclerosis models, macrophage infiltration, foam cell formation, and lipid accumulation drive pathological vascular remodeling, while patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells reveal disease mechanisms such as increased calcification and inflammatory sensitivity in genetic conditions like Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome. The personalized organs-on-chips approach enables mechanistic studies of endothelial dysfunction, thrombotic complications, and vascular aging, offering potential for individualized disease modeling and therapeutic screening.

title: Evidence of compensatory elastin remodeling in non-prolapsed uterosacral ligaments and its divergence in histopathological subtypes of pelvic organ prolapse
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that pelvic organ prolapse involves dysregulation of compensatory elastin remodeling mechanisms that normally occur in non-prolapsed uterosacral ligaments. Elastin and its assembly factors (fibulin-5, LOXL1) are critical for pelvic support, and healthy ligaments show increased elastin content with advancing vaginal parity as a reparative response. However, prolapsed tissues reveal distinct histopathological subtypes with divergent pathologies: the POP-A subtype exhibits smooth muscle loss and adipose infiltration with reduced elastin, the POP-I subtype shows increased inflammation, and the POP-V subtype displays vascular remodeling with elevated myofibroblasts and neointimal hyperplasia. These findings reveal that pelvic organ prolapse results from failure of this inherent reparative elastin-remodeling capacity, suggesting that understanding these subtype-specific mechanisms may inform targeted therapeutic interventions for different presentations of pelvic floor dysfunction.

title: Evaluating deconvolution methods using real bulk RNA-expression data for robust prognostic insights across cancer types
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated computational deconvolution methods for estimating cell type proportions from bulk RNA-seq data using real cancer transcriptomes rather than simulated pseudobulk data. ReCIDE and BayesPrism emerged as the top-performing methods, with ReCIDE achieving the highest cross-cohort reproducibility (Jaccard index 0.41–0.73) and overall ranking (average rank 1.29), while BayesPrism excelled in F1-score consistency and single-cell RNA-seq alignment. The analysis revealed that matrix cancer-associated fibroblasts and classical monocytes are robust prognostic indicators across multiple cancer types, providing a combined signature significant in five TCGA cohorts. In contrast, older methods like MuSiC, CIBERSORT, and DWLS exhibited substantial limitations, including frequent zero proportion assignments and poor reproducibility in identifying cancer-prognostic cell types. The study underscores that strong performance on pseudobulk data does not guarantee accuracy on real bulk RNA-seq, establishing ReCIDE and BayesPrism as superior approaches for translating single-cell discoveries into clinically actionable cell type signatures.

title: First person – Muhammad Dimas Reza Rahmana
summary: Endometriosis, affecting approximately one in ten women worldwide, causes debilitating chronic pelvic pain through mechanisms initiated by retrograde menstrual blood flow, which allows endometrial fragments to attach to the peritoneum and establish peritoneal lesions. The research identifies the endometrial–peritoneal interface as a critical structure in early disease formation, composed of endometrial stromal cells expressing CD10 and cytokeratin that facilitate cell migration during lesion development. By isolating both endometrial and peritoneal cells from patient biopsies during laparoscopic surgery, the researchers established an in vitro model that recapitulates the cellular interactions necessary for peritoneal endometriosis formation. The endometrial–peritoneal interface represents the key zone where endometrial and peritoneal cell populations interact to promote disease progression. This modeling approach provides a platform to further dissect the pathogenic mechanisms by incorporating additional cell types such as immune cells and menstrual hormones to better replicate the dynamic in vivo conditions that drive endometriosis development.

title: Compound design of a patient-derived 3D cell culture system modelling early peritoneal endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study presents a patient-derived 3D culture system that successfully models early peritoneal endometriosis by combining endometrial epithelial organoids and stromal cells into endometrial assembloids, which are then cultured on a biomimetic peritoneal layer. The peritoneal layer was constructed using human peritoneal mesothelial cells and fibroblasts embedded in a collagen I and Matrigel matrix at a 50:50 ratio, with mesothelial cells producing tissue plasminogen activator and expressing appropriate markers (zonula occludens 1, WT1, mesothelin) that recapitulate normal peritoneal physiology. When endometrial assembloids were transplanted onto this peritoneal model, they adhered to the apical surface and formed an endometrial-peritoneal interface, with endometrial stromal cells infiltrating into the underlying peritoneal compartment, faithfully simulating early endometriotic lesion formation. This compound 3D system thus provides a physiologically relevant platform for studying the cellular and molecular mechanisms of endometriosis initiation, which could advance understanding of how this chronic inflammatory condition that causes debilitating pelvic pain and subfertility develops.

title: In Vitro 3D Model of Human Endometrial Stromal and Trophoblast Cells: Techniques for an Optimized Formation and Cryopreservation of Spheroids
summary:
# Summary

This study optimized techniques for generating three-dimensional spheroids from human endometrial stromal cells (tHESC, St-T1b, D-tHESC) and trophoblast cells (JEG3, HTR8/SVneo) using the hanging drop method to model embryo-maternal interactions and implantation. Methyl cellulose significantly enhanced cell aggregation and spheroid formation while maintaining structural integrity, with spheroid formation beginning within 8 hours and achieving morphological stability by 48 hours across all cell lines. Decidualization of stromal cells—induced by 8-Br-cAMP and MPA and confirmed by prolactin secretion—was successfully incorporated into the 3D spheroid model to represent the receptive endometrial environment. These physiologically relevant 3D spheroid models successfully maintained functional viability for up to 72 hours post-thaw following cryopreservation in DMSO-based medium, enabling their application in drug screening and reproductive biology studies. The protocol achieved 100% spheroid formation efficiency and provides a standardized approach for studying trophoblast invasion, embryo-maternal communication, and the role of endometrial decidualization in reproductive success.

title: Loss of Parkinson Disease Protein 7 (PARK7) upregulates ROS and cell migration and is associated with recurrent pregnancy loss
summary: Loss of the DJ-1 protein, encoded by the PARK7 gene, impairs endometrial decidualization and increases susceptibility to recurrent pregnancy loss by dysregulating reactive oxygen species (ROS) and cytoskeletal dynamics. DJ-1 normally functions as a multifaceted redox-sensitive chaperone and antioxidant regulator that maintains optimal ROS levels and controls actin cytoskeleton reorganization through its interaction with the downstream effector Palladin. When DJ-1 is depleted in endometrial stromal cells, elevated ROS levels and increased actin polymerization drive abnormal cell migration toward aneuploid embryos, preventing proper embryo discrimination and implantation. The disruption of the DJ-1-Palladin axis during the critical implantation window leads to defective decidualization and compromises the endometrium's ability to support early pregnancy. Therapeutic interventions targeting components of this pathway—such as restoring Palladin expression or boosting antioxidant enzymes like glutathione peroxidase 3—can reverse these pathological effects and restore normal endometrial function, suggesting potential avenues for treating recurrent pregnancy loss.

title: High resolution multi-scale profiling of embryonic germ cell-like cell derivation reveals pluripotent state transitions in humans
summary: This study employs multi-scale profiling to characterize the developmental transitions of human embryonic germ cell-like cells (hEGCLCs) derived from human pluripotent stem cells through an intermediate incipient mesoderm-like cell stage. Human PGC-like cells (hPGCLCs), identified by expression of markers including BLIMP1, TFAP2C, NANOS3, and SOX17, undergo conversion to hEGCLCs through exposure to factors such as GSK3 inhibitors, bFGF, hLIF, and retinoic acid, accompanied by reversible epigenetic reprogramming involving DNA demethylation patterns. During this transition, hPGCLCs downregulate germline-specific markers while upregulating pluripotency genes (SOX2, OCT4, NANOG, SSEA-4), resulting in hEGCLCs that are transcriptionally and functionally equivalent to naive induced pluripotent stem cells. The derived hEGCLCs retain full pluripotent capacity, capable of differentiating into all three germ layers and reverting back to hPGCLCs with efficiency comparable to the parental hiPSCs. These findings demonstrate that human germ cells exist in a latent pluripotent state analogous to mouse models, with reversible epigenetic and transcriptional mechanisms controlling the balance between germline identity and pluripotency during early human development.

title: Association of polymorphic loci of the matrix metalloproteinase genes with the development of fetal growth retardation: A case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study identifies the MMP-2 rs243865 polymorphism as a significant risk factor for fetal growth retardation (FGR), with the T allele conferring increased susceptibility. The polymorphic locus is located in regulatory regions of amnion and placenta tissues, including enhancer regions and DNase-1 hypersensitivity sites, where it alters myogenic factor transcription affinity and regulates epigenetic modifications critical for fetal development. Matrix metalloproteinases broadly play essential roles in pregnancy by mediating cytotrophoblast migration and invasion, spiral artery remodeling, syncytiotrophoblast formation, and angiogenesis; disruption of MMP expression is associated with multiple pregnancy complications including FGR, preeclampsia, and placental insufficiency. MMP-2 specifically degrades extracellular matrix components and regulates uterine vascularization, such that decreased MMP-2 levels reduce spiral artery diameter and dilation, leading to progressive uteroplacental ischemia and impaired fetal growth. The rs243865 variant also influences transcriptional activity of the RP11-212I21.2 gene in arteries and LPCAT2 gene polyadenylation, which affects embryonic development and estrogen-regulated cellular processes, suggesting pleiotropic effects on reproductive competence through multiple molecular pathways.

title: A Cxcl15 Cre Recombinase Mouse Model Useful to Study Gland Development in the Uterus
summary: This study presents a novel Cxcl15-iCre transgenic mouse model specifically targeting the glandular epithelium of the developing and adult uterus. Cxcl15 is highly and specifically expressed in uterine glandular epithelium throughout development and co-expresses with the established gland marker Foxa2, with expression also observed in vaginal and cervical epithelium but absent from oviducts and ovaries. Using this model, the researchers demonstrated that Foxa2 is essential for glandular epithelium differentiation and uterine gland genesis, as Cxcl15-iCre;Foxa2 conditional knockout mice are infertile due to impaired production of leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) by glandular cells, a factor critical for embryo implantation. Notably, Lif supplementation rescues implantation and pregnancy in Foxa2-deficient mice, establishing a mechanistic link between glandular Foxa2, LIF production, and reproductive success. The Cxcl15-iCre model itself demonstrates normal fertility and viability, making it a valuable transgenic tool for studying endometrial gland development and function in pregnancy establishment, as well as gland-associated pathologies including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and endometrial adenocarcinoma.

title: Leveraging menstrual effluent (ME) to investigate vitamin D and human decidualization, a potential link with fertility
journal: The Journal of clinical endocrinology and metabolism
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that vitamin D deficiency is associated with female infertility and reproductive disorders including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome, with a potential mechanistic link through impaired endometrial decidualization. Using menstrual effluent-derived endometrial stromal cells, researchers found that calcitriol (active vitamin D) significantly enhanced decidualization, as measured by increased IGFBP1 and prolactin production, while simultaneously reducing AKT and PRAS40 phosphorylation—suggesting that vitamin D promotes decidualization through suppression of AKT signaling pathways. Calcitriol upregulated vitamin D receptor (VDR) mRNA expression during decidualization, and peripheral vitamin D levels correlated with menstrual effluent vitamin D biomarkers, establishing menstrual effluent as a practical biosampling tool. In vivo cholecalciferol supplementation enhanced decidualization capacity in endometrial tissue, while calcitriol itself did not affect cell proliferation, indicating that vitamin D specifically enhances the differentiation process critical for embryo implantation. These findings provide a mechanistic explanation for vitamin D's association with uterine receptivity and implantation success, supporting potential therapeutic benefits of vitamin D supplementation for fertility outcomes.

title: Back to the Future—A 50-Year Dive into Embryo Implantation Research: Cell Biological Paradox, Epithelial Cell Polarity, and EMT
summary:
# Summary

Embryo implantation presents a fundamental cell biological paradox: the trophoblast of the blastocyst must establish contact with and invade the uterine epithelium, yet both tissues retain epithelial characteristics rather than undergoing complete epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT). The trophoblast develops adhesive and invasive properties autonomously, acquiring apical membrane competence through polarity changes that appear to involve only partial EMT programs, while the uterine luminal epithelium acts as a selective barrier that becomes permissive specifically during the implantation window defined by endometrial receptivity. Species-specific differences in trophoblast invasion—ranging from non-invasive attachment in pigs to deep stromal invasion in humans—correlate with changes in adhesion molecule expression and trophoblast differentiation state. The endometrium plays a critical regulatory role beyond blastocyst quality, with the luminal epithelium functioning as a signal transducer between embryonic and maternal systems while mediating decidualization and controlling implantation success. Implantation failure remains a major cause of assisted reproductive failure, highlighting the need to better understand the complex cellular and molecular interactions governing epithelial polarity and partial EMT rather than focusing solely on embryo assessment.

title: Loss of Function of the Zxdb Gene Leads to a Decrease in the Decidualization Rate and Number of Pups Born in Mice by Affecting the Expression of the Cell Adhesion Molecules
summary: This study identifies the Zxdb gene as a critical regulator of endometrial decidualization and reproductive success in mice. Loss of Zxdb function in maternal uterine tissue significantly impairs decidualization rates and reduces litter size, demonstrating that maternal Zxdb expression is essential for successful embryo implantation. The mechanism underlying these reproductive defects involves dysregulation of cell adhesion molecules, particularly an abnormal CDH1/CDH2 ratio, which disrupts the epithelial-mesenchymal transition required for proper endometrial remodeling and blastocyst implantation. Zxdb expression increases progressively in uterine tissue during pregnancy and regulates the expression of adhesion molecules including E-cadherin, N-cadherin, and BMP2, all critical for endometrial receptivity. These findings suggest that Zxdb dysfunction may contribute to reproductive failures including recurrent implantation failure and recurrent spontaneous abortion in humans, with potential implications for genetic counseling and understanding endometrial dysfunction in infertility.

title: Quantitative Flow Cytometry—Medical Applications with a Focus on Blood Platelets
summary:
# Summary

Quantitative flow cytometry (QFCM) employs fluorochrome-labeled monoclonal antibodies to measure cell-surface antigen expression with high precision, serving as a valuable diagnostic and monitoring tool for platelet disorders and other hematological conditions. The technique quantifies antigens such as GPIIb/IIIa, GPIb, and P-Selectin on platelet surfaces, enabling detection of inherited platelet disorders including Glanzmann thrombasthenia and Bernard-Soulier syndrome, where specific glycoprotein complexes are quantitatively deficient. QFCM reflects biological functions through antigen clustering and activation-dependent changes; for example, thrombin receptor agonist peptide (TRAP) activation induces increased GPIIb/IIIa and P-Selectin externalization while decreasing GPIb surface expression. The methodology's robustness depends on proper calibration using standardized beads and indirect immunofluorescence protocols that minimize background noise, particularly important when analyzing sensitive cells like platelets. Beyond blood cells, QFCM can characterize extra-cellular vesicles including exosomes and ectosomes involved in intercellular communication. This quantitative approach proves especially valuable for monitoring patients receiving targeted therapies and testing neonates or thrombocytopenic individuals where sample volumes are limited.

title: The Role of Granulocyte Colony-Stimulating Factor in Endometrial Preparation for Embryo Implantation in In Vitro Fertilization
summary:
# Summary

Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) plays a critical role in endometrial preparation for embryo implantation by orchestrating multiple biological processes essential for successful pregnancy. Expressed in the endometrial epithelium and stroma at elevated levels during the mid-luteal secretory phase, G-CSF binds to its receptor (G-CSFR) on stromal fibroblasts, decidualized cells, and endothelial cells, activating the JAK2-STAT3 signaling pathway. STAT3 activation promotes decidualization, enhances endometrial stromal cell survival and differentiation, and activates critical transcriptional regulators including HOXA10 while integrating progesterone signaling. Additionally, G-CSF facilitates immune tolerance by augmenting regulatory T-cell activity and promoting macrophage polarization toward an M2-like phenotype, while simultaneously enhancing endothelial survival and microvasculature stability. Clinical studies demonstrate that intrauterine G-CSF administration increases endometrial thickness and improves implantation and pregnancy rates in patients with thin endometrium or recurrent implantation failure, establishing G-CSF as a potential therapeutic target for endometrial dysfunction in in vitro fertilization.

title: Temporal trajectory of plasma miRNA during the peri-implantation stage: comparing implantation success and failure in single frozen–thawed blastocyst transfers
summary: This study identifies plasma microRNA (miRNA) expression patterns as dynamic biomarkers distinguishing successful from failed embryo implantation during the peri-implantation period. Differentially expressed (DDE) miRNAs correlate with fluctuating estradiol and progesterone levels and exhibit distinct temporal patterns, with successful implantation characterized by sustained M-pattern miRNAs while implantation failures show pattern transitions toward T-pattern miRNAs. Functional analysis reveals that these miRNAs regulate critical implantation pathways, particularly Wnt/mTOR signaling involved in decidualization and embryo implantation, with hsa-miR-214-3p specifically targeting CTNNB1 to modulate these processes. The coordinated interplay of ovarian hormones, cytokines, chemokines, and multiple endometrial cell types (luminal and glandular epithelial cells, stromal cells, and immune cells) during implantation is regulated through miRNA-mediated mechanisms that reflect endometrial receptivity. Support vector machine modeling using early DDE miRNAs from peripheral blood successfully predicts implantation outcomes before embryo transfer. These findings establish plasma miRNA expression signatures as novel diagnostic tools for assessing endometrial receptivity and identifying implantation failure risk.

title: Proteomic Profiling of Exosomes Derived from Endometrial Stem Cells and Adipose-Derived Stem Cells
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial stem cells (EnSCs) isolated from menstrual blood are multipotent mesenchymal stem cells expressing characteristic markers (CD29, CD73, CD90, CD105, CD146, SUSD2) that exhibit higher proliferation rates and osteogenic differentiation potential compared to adipose-derived stem cells (ASCs). Proteomic profiling of EnSC-derived exosomes identified 1,218 total proteins, with 236 proteins more abundant in EnSC exosomes than ASC exosomes, including key regulatory proteins such as stromal-derived factor 1 (SDF-1), insulin-like growth factor-binding proteins (IGFBP5 and IGFBP7), and TAP binding protein (TAPBP). EnSC-derived exosomes demonstrate therapeutic potential for endometriosis by reducing inflammation, suppressing endometrial cell migration and angiogenesis, and inducing apoptosis, while their enriched proteomic profile suggests mechanisms for immune modulation through enhanced antigen presentation and cytotoxic T cell activation. These findings highlight EnSC-derived exosomes as promising cell-free therapeutics for treating endometrial dysfunction and reproductive failure associated with endometriosis.

title: Endometrial Assembloid Model Reveals Endometrial Gland Development Regulation by Estradiol‐Driven WNT7B Suppression
summary: This study identifies estradiol-driven suppression of WNT7B as a critical mechanism promoting endometrial gland development using an innovative endometrial assembloid model. Estradiol downregulates WNT7B expression, which normally inhibits gland formation, thereby facilitating tubular gland development and epithelial proliferation and differentiation during the proliferative phase of the menstrual cycle. Stromal-epithelial cross-talk further regulates this process, with TGFβ1 signaling from endometrial stromal cells modulating WNT7B expression through vitamin D receptor interactions. Endometrial glands are essential for secretory functions and cyclical remodeling required for embryo implantation, and dysregulation of this estradiol-WNT7B axis has been implicated in reproductive disorders including endometriosis, infertility, and endometrial hyperplasia. The three-dimensional endometrial organoid model, incorporating multiple cell types and extracellular matrix scaffolds, effectively recapitulates in vivo endometrial conditions and reveals these molecular mechanisms with greater physiological relevance than traditional two-dimensional cultures. These findings establish the estradiol-WNT7B signaling pathway and stromal-derived TGFβ1-VDR crosstalk as therapeutic targets for improving treatment of gland-related reproductive disorders.

title: Two layers of fascia envelop the pudendal nerve canal: a cadaver study
summary:
# Summary

This cadaver study elucidates the anatomical architecture of the pudendal nerve canal, revealing a complex fascial anatomy critical for clinical interventions in the pelvic region. The pudendal nerve is enclosed within two distinct fascial layers derived from the sacrotuberous ligament, which connects the sacrum to the ischial tuberosity and contains the pudendal canal within its structure. The membrane extending from the sacrotuberous ligament is notably thicker than the proper fascia of the obturator internus muscle, and these fascial planes form anatomically distinct compartments that separate the pudendal nerve pathway from the adjacent obturator internus nerve, which courses between the obturator internus muscle and its own proper fascia. The pudendal nerve does not traverse the fascia of the obturator internus muscle, maintaining a separate compartmentalization. These findings have significant implications for surgical planning and tumor resection procedures involving the pudendal canal, as surgeons must carefully consider the precise anatomical relationships between the fascial layers and nerve structures to minimize iatrogenic injury during operative interventions.

title: Embryo secretome regulation of the endometrial epithelial transcriptome: development of a model to test for embryo viability
summary: This study develops an endometrial epithelial organoid model to investigate how embryo secretions regulate endometrial gene expression and implantation success. The researchers exposed three-dimensional organoids derived from endometrial epithelial cells to culture supernatants from live-birth embryos, which modulated the expression of 32 genes—24 upregulated and 8 downregulated—enriched for pathways related to cell motility and cytoskeletal dynamics. The organoids demonstrated appropriate hormone responsiveness, undergoing decidualization in response to estradiol and progesterone treatment, with expression of secretory phase markers including SPP1 and maintained epithelial integrity markers. Successful implantation requires synchronization between a high-quality embryo and receptive endometrium during the window of implantation; while about one-third of implantation failures result from embryo aneuploidy, the remaining two-thirds are attributed to altered endometrial receptivity. This organoid platform provides a robust experimental system to test embryo viability and investigate mechanisms of implantation failure by identifying how viable embryos specifically communicate with and remodel the endometrial epithelium.

title: Multi‐Omics Data Reveal Estrogen‐Driven Dysregulation and Stromal‐Epithelial Signaling Alterations in Endometrial Polyps
summary: Endometrial polyps are common uterine lesions associated with abnormal bleeding and infertility, driven by a complex interplay of estrogen-mediated dysregulation and aberrant cellular communication. Multi-omics analysis reveals that polyps exhibit enhanced estrogen signaling leading to excessive epithelial proliferation, alongside reduced T cell cytotoxicity and altered immune surveillance. Stromal cells in polyps show upregulation of WNT, IGF, and VEGF signaling pathways, creating dysregulated stromal-epithelial crosstalk that promotes glandular overgrowth and a pro-inflammatory microenvironment. Genomic instability including copy number variations and transcriptional heterogeneity within epithelial subpopulations contribute to polyp formation and carry potential implications for malignant transformation, which occurs in 0–13% of cases. In vitro organoid models confirm that epithelial transcriptional alterations directly drive polyp formation, emphasizing the role of intrinsic epithelial factors alongside extrinsic stromal signals. Hysteroscopic polypectomy remains the primary therapeutic approach and effectively improves pregnancy outcomes, highlighting the clinical significance of removing these lesions in managing reproductive failure.

title: Single-cell transcriptomic landscape of the mid-secretory eutopic endometrium reveals receptivity defects in adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Single-cell transcriptomic analysis of mid-secretory endometrium reveals that adenomyosis causes impaired endometrial receptivity through disrupted epithelial-stromal communication. In adenomyosis, expanded epithelial stem cell populations (marked by LGR5 and SOX9) exhibit excessive proliferation and strengthened tight junctions, while simultaneously displaying hyperactive PTN signaling and reduced DKK1 and IGF2 expression that further impairs stromal decidualization. The critical DIO2+ decidual cell population with senescence-associated secretory phenotype features is markedly reduced in adenomyosis, coinciding with downregulation of inflammatory mediators and key decidualization markers. These defects prevent the normal epithelial-to-secretory transition and compromise stromal decidualization, mechanisms mediated by the progesterone-responsive Indian Hedgehog and FOXO1 signaling axis that normally promotes endometrial receptivity. Together, these multi-level alterations in epithelial proliferation, decidual cell function, and intercellular signaling pathways provide mechanistic insight into how adenomyosis causes infertility, dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia in reproductive-age women.

title: Role of immune cells in clear cell ovarian cancer: a two-sample bidirectional Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This bidirectional Mendelian randomization study identifies multiple immune cell phenotypes as significant contributors to clear cell ovarian cancer (CCOC) susceptibility through distinct immunological mechanisms. CD8+ and CD4+ T cells show complex associations with CCOC, with elevated CD4+ infiltration correlating with reduced survival, while specific T cell subsets including double-negative (DN) T cells and CD8dim T cells emerge as risk factors. B cell abnormalities also play a central role, with increased IgD−CD38dim and IgD+CD38− B cell populations positively associated with CCOC development and poor prognosis. The study reveals that regulatory T cells promote immune tolerance through adenosine production via CD38 expression, which subsequently inhibits cytotoxic T cells and natural killer cells, thereby facilitating tumor immune evasion. Additionally, CD25 gene polymorphisms (rs7072793 T>C) in Treg cells enhance this suppressive pathway, allowing malignant cells to escape anti-tumor immunity. These findings establish immune cell composition and function as critical determinants of CCOC pathogenesis, with implications for developing immunotherapy strategies targeting these immunosuppressive mechanisms.

title: Is it possible to use the serum levels of alpha 1-antitrypsin as a serum biomarker to distinguish endometriosis and endometriosis-associated epithelial ovarian cancers?
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: This study investigates serum alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) isoforms as a potential biomarker to distinguish endometriosis from endometriosis-associated epithelial ovarian cancer (EAEOC), particularly ovarian clear cell carcinoma. Endometriosis is strongly associated with ovarian clear cell carcinoma, which frequently occurs in younger women and presents with inherent chemoresistance driven by genetic changes, epigenetic modifications, glycosylation alterations, and tumor microenvironment factors. The researchers found that patients with ovarian clear cell carcinoma had significantly elevated serum AAT isoform levels compared to benign tumors and endometriosis cases, with levels dramatically decreasing after debulking surgery, suggesting correlation with tumor burden. AAT functions as an acute-phase protein that inhibits proteolytic enzymes including elastase, trypsin, chymotrypsin, and thrombin, and its altered glycosylation patterns (involving agalactosylation, sialylation, and fucosylation) in cancer patients reflect the chronic inflammatory state associated with cancer development and progression. The elevated serum AAT isoforms may serve as a complementary biomarker to CA125 for monitoring therapeutic response and detecting recurrence in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer, though the precise mechanisms linking glycosylation changes to malignant transformation require further investigation.

title: Highly Tunable and Cell‐Remodelable Thiol‐ene Alginate‐Peptide Crosslinked Hydrogels to Recreate Cellular and Organoid Microenvironments for Biofabrication
summary: This study describes the development of tunable thiol-ene alginate-peptide crosslinked hydrogels as a versatile platform for recreating cellular microenvironments in three-dimensional culture systems. The hydrogels are constructed by crosslinking norbornene-functionalized alginate with di-thiolated peptide sequences that are cleavable by matrix metalloproteinases, and further decorated with RGD cell-adhesion peptides, enabling dynamic remodeling that mimics natural extracellular matrix properties. The platform demonstrates superior biocompatibility and high cell viability across multiple organoid systems, including human endometrial organoids that maintain spherical morphology, express key markers like E-cadherin and Ki67, and enable epithelial-stromal cross-talk critical for endometrial tissue modeling. Additionally, the hydrogels support diverse differentiation programs in multiple organoid types, including thyroid follicles producing thyroglobulin and T4, intestinal organoids adopting proliferative phenotypes, and renal organoids showing reduced fibrosis compared to static alternatives. Importantly, this defined and tunable alginate system offers an ethical, cost-effective alternative to poorly characterized animal-derived matrices like Matrigel, providing researchers with unprecedented control over hydrogel properties for biofabrication and disease modeling applications.

title: Conjoining cell reprogramming and mass spectrometry to identify the proteomic variations in the reprogrammed bladder cancer cells: finding cues of normalisation
summary: This study employs Sendai virus-mediated delivery of Yamanaka factors (Oct-4, Sox-2, Klf-4, and c-Myc) to reprogram bladder cancer cells and identify proteomic signatures associated with cellular normalization. The reprogrammed cancer cells successfully express pluripotency-associated markers including OCT-4, SOX-2, NANOG, c-MYC, KLF-4, and LIN28, while demonstrating altered phenotypic behaviors including increased colony formation and cell migration. Proteomics analysis via LC-MS/MS revealed that reprogramming normalized 297 dysregulated proteins in the cancer cells, indicating a shift toward a more normal cellular state. Notably, the reprogrammed cells displayed enhanced sensitivity to anticancer therapies such as paclitaxel, which reduced cell migration in these cells compared to parental cancer cells. These findings suggest that controlled partial reprogramming can serve as a therapeutic strategy by reversing cancer-associated dysregulation while identifying candidate biomarkers and protein interactions that could optimize future interventions for bladder cancer treatment.

title: Disruption of primary ciliary prostaglandin E2 signaling by transforming growth factor-β1 impairs endometrial receptivity
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that transforming growth factor-β1 (TGF-β1) disrupts primary ciliary prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) signaling, thereby impairing endometrial receptivity and decidualization. Primary cilia in endometrial stromal cells serve as critical signaling hubs where EP4 receptors mediate PGE2-stimulated decidualization, a process essential for uterine receptivity and implantation. TGF-β1, elevated in the uterine and peritoneal environment of endometriosis patients, suppresses ciliogenesis by downregulating key transcription factors like COUPT-TF II and kinesin family member 3B, resulting in shortened cilia and reduced decidualization capacity. In women with endometriosis, this TGF-β1-mediated ciliary dysfunction correlates with impaired decidualization and diminished expression of critical decidual markers including FOXO1 and IGFBP1. Notably, blocking TGF-β receptor signaling restores ciliogenesis and decidualization, suggesting a therapeutic approach to improve endometrial function. Cilia length and ciliation frequency emerge as quantitative biomarkers for predicting endometrial receptivity and implantation potential in reproductive failure.

title: Increased C-Reactive Protein Concentrations During Menstruation May Be Important for the Pathophysiology of Endometriosis and Possibly for Adhesion Formation—A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review proposes that elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) during menstruation plays a critical role in endometriosis pathophysiology and adhesion formation. The mechanism begins with retrograde menstruation, which triggers acute pelvic inflammation and causes mesothelial cell retraction, thereby exposing the peritoneal basal membrane and facilitating implantation of endometrial cells. The inflammatory cascade involves macrophages and T cells producing interleukin-6, which stimulates hepatic CRP secretion—a response that is intensified during menstruation and the early follicular phase when peritoneal fluid contains blood and elevated hormonal concentrations. Blood itself is highly irritant, directly promoting mesothelial retraction, pain, and adhesion formation through activation of fibroblasts and fibrin deposition. The authors suggest that minimizing peritoneal trauma through gentle surgical technique, continuous irrigation, and bloodless procedures can prevent adhesions by preserving mesothelial repair, while considering menstruation suppression as a potential preventive strategy for endometriosis in non-conception-seeking women.

title: Efficient derivation of stable sheep embryonic stem cells opens a new avenue for agricultural and biomedical application
summary:
# Summary

This study describes the development of TePR-sESCs, sheep embryonic stem cells derived using a novel culture system combining mTeSR PLUS base medium with the WNT/TNK inhibitor IWR-1. The TePR-sESCs exhibit stable morphology and maintain pluripotency markers including POU5F1 and NANOG expression, with transcriptomic analysis revealing their resemblance to early embryonic stages (8-cell/morula), consistent with sheep genome activation timing. These cells demonstrate robust trilineage differentiation capacity and support efficient genome editing through PiggyBac transposition and CRISPR/Cas9, as evidenced by successful mCherry integration and MSTN knockouts. The derivation of stable sheep ESCs with embryonic-like pluripotency opens significant applications in livestock trait engineering and biomedical modeling, particularly leveraging sheep's physiological similarity to humans for disease modeling including endometriosis, cardiovascular disease, and osteoarthritis.

title: Rare variant analyses provide insights into the genetic architecture of endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This rare variant analysis study identifies SOGA1 as a significantly associated gene for endometriosis, with additional suggestive loci including MYBPC2 and RAB19, alongside previously reported genes such as GSK3B, PPP2CA, ESR2, SAMHD1, CYLD, and AURKA. SOGA1 encodes a microtubule-associated protein that regulates multiple biological processes implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis, including autophagy, glucose homeostasis, chromosome segregation, and cell migration. The gene is highly expressed in endothelial, epithelial, smooth muscle, mesenchymal, and stromal cells, cell types significantly enriched in endometriomas. Functional enrichment analyses reveal that SOGA1 and its interacting genes converge on pathways involving autophagy, glucose metabolism, Wnt signaling, and actin cytoskeleton regulation—processes recognized as central to the development of endometrial lesions, fibrosis, angiogenesis, and cellular migration. Together, these findings illuminate the genetic architecture of endometriosis at the rare variant level and suggest that dysregulation of glucose homeostasis and autophagy represents a key mechanistic pathway linking genetic susceptibility to disease manifestation.

title: Endometrial immune cell profile at the time of frozen embryo transfer as prognostic indicator of live birth
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometrial immune cell composition at the time of frozen embryo transfer (FET) serves as a prognostic indicator of implantation success and live birth outcomes. Elevated γδ T cells (γδ TCR+) in the endometrium were significantly associated with live birth, while increased neutrophils (CD66b+ cells) were associated with implantation failure, with an optimal cutoff value of >39.60% identifying high-risk patients. The uterine immune microenvironment involves multiple cell populations—particularly decidual natural killer cells, T regulatory cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells—that support essential implantation processes including maternal-fetal tolerance, spiral artery remodeling, angiogenesis, and trophoblast invasion. Notably, higher levels of CD57+ NK cells and optimal IL-15/Fn-14 ratios correlated with improved pregnancy outcomes, suggesting that specific immune cell signatures can predict reproductive success. This first application of immune cell profiling at FET using receiver operating characteristic analysis establishes the endometrial immune landscape as a measurable biomarker for identifying patients at risk of implantation failure.

title: Changes in Maternal Leucine-Rich α-2 Glycoprotein Levels During Pregnancy: Comparison Between Preterm and Term Births
summary: This study examined leucine-rich α-2 glycoprotein (LRG), an acute-phase protein produced by macrophages and hepatocytes, as a potential biomarker for preterm birth and intrauterine infection. Maternal serum LRG levels remained stable throughout pregnancy in both preterm and term birth groups but increased significantly during the postpartum period, with numerically higher levels observed in the preterm birth group, particularly among cases with chorioamnionitis. LRG expression is regulated by transforming growth factor-beta signaling pathways and is associated with peripartum inflammation, suggesting its role in response to infection-related complications that trigger preterm labor through uterine contractions, cervical ripening, and fetal membrane weakening. Elevated cord blood and umbilical vein LRG levels correlate with fetal infection and chorioamnionitis, with LRG expression localized in fetal hepatocytes, indicating fetal systemic response to infection. While maternal serum LRG levels did not differ significantly between preterm and term births during pregnancy, postpartum LRG elevation may serve as a marker of peripartum inflammation and chorioamnionitis severity. These findings suggest LRG warrants investigation as a potential biomarker for intrauterine infection-related preterm birth, though further prospective studies are needed to establish its clinical utility.

title: Single-cell dissection of regulated cell death dynamics in ovarian aging
summary:
# Summary

This single-cell study reveals that multiple regulated cell death pathways drive ovarian aging through distinct mechanisms in specific cell populations. Parthanatos, mediated by PARP1 activation, emerges as the dominant aging-associated pathway, particularly in granulosa cells, where it correlates with declining hormone synthesis and elevated DNA damage markers (8-OHdG, γH2AX). Complementary cell-type-specific death programs include apoptosis in granulosa cells and endothelial cells, ferroptosis in theca and stromal cells, and lysosome-dependent cell death in immune cells, collectively contributing to diminished ovarian reserve and fertility loss. Age-related increases in oxidative stress and inflammatory signaling (NOD-like receptor and NF-κB pathways) drive these cell death cascades while suppressing DNA repair and meiotic cell cycle machinery. The study identifies therapeutic potential for quercetin, melatonin, and spermidine in protecting ovarian function by regulating autophagy and ferroptosis, suggesting that targeting regulated cell death pathways may mitigate age-related reproductive decline and associated systemic diseases including cardiovascular disease and cognitive decline.

title: Progesterone modulates protein cargo in extracellular vesicles released from bovine uterine epithelial cells
summary:
# Summary

Progesterone regulates the protein cargo composition of extracellular vesicles (EVs) released from bovine uterine epithelial cells, altering the expression of 45 proteins while maintaining consistent EV release levels. These progesterone-responsive EVs express characteristic exosomal markers (CD9 and CD81) and carry proteins critical for embryo–maternal communication, including adhesion molecules like galectin-3 and galectin-3-binding protein, along with the actin-binding protein gelsolin. The EVs are successfully internalized by bovine embryos and deliver their protein cargo into developing embryos, thereby facilitating implantation, embryo adhesion, and proper metabolic development. This work demonstrates that progesterone-mediated modulation of uterine EV composition represents a crucial mechanism for establishing embryo–maternal crosstalk, as a suboptimal uterine microenvironment lacking appropriate EV signaling is associated with high embryonic mortality before implantation. The findings suggest that uterine-derived EVs serve as key mediators of progesterone's role in establishing endometrial receptivity and supporting early embryo development, complementing the nutrient-rich histotroph in preparing the uterine environment for successful pregnancy establishment.

title: Role and mechanism of mesenchymal stem cells in endometrial receptivity remodeling
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) enhance endometrial receptivity, a critical prerequisite for embryo implantation, through multiple coordinated mechanisms involving direct differentiation, angiogenic factor secretion, and immunomodulation. MSCs promote angiogenesis and improve endometrial perfusion by secreting VEGF, bFGF, and HGF, which increase microvessel density and upregulate receptivity-associated molecules while counteracting the hypoxic conditions that characterize pathological states like endometriosis and chronic endometritis. Through immunomodulatory pathways, MSCs induce regulatory T cell expansion and polarize macrophages toward anti-inflammatory M2 phenotypes while suppressing aberrant uterine natural killer cell cytotoxicity, thereby establishing a tolerogenic microenvironment conducive to implantation. Clinical trials with umbilical cord-derived MSCs demonstrate significant improvements in endometrial thickness and clinical pregnancy rates in women with intrauterine adhesions, validating the therapeutic potential of MSC-based interventions. These findings establish MSCs as a promising regenerative approach for restoring endometrial function in reproductive failure associated with impaired receptivity and aberrant immunological environments.

title: Revealing the immune landscape of menstrual blood: unlocking insights into activation, exhaustion, and mitochondrial mass for reproductive health
summary: Menstrual blood contains a diverse array of immune cells including monocytes, macrophages, natural killer cells, and T cells that reflect the tissue-resident immune environment of the endometrium, which undergoes cyclical remodeling, implantation preparation, and pathogen defense. The study revealed that menstrual mononuclear cells display a distinct immune landscape compared to peripheral blood cells, characterized by enrichment of tissue-resident NK cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells, with NK cell phenotypes shifting from CD56dimCD16bright in blood to CD56brightCD16dim in menstrual fluid. Immune cell metabolism—particularly mitochondrial mass—plays a critical role in determining cell fate and function, with notably higher mitochondrial content in CD4 and CD8 T cells from menstrual fluid compared to blood, while monocytes showed reduced mitochondrial content. Endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome each exhibit distinct menstrual immune signatures: endometriosis is characterized by elevated CD38 expression in CD4 T cells and regulatory T cells along with increased mitochondrial mass in plasmablasts, whereas PCOS displays reduced mitochondrial content in regulatory T cells and central memory CD4 T cells. Both disorders are associated with chronic low-grade dysregulated inflammation, and PD-1 expression was elevated on effector memory CD8 T cells in menstrual fluid, indicating potential immune exhaustion. These findings establish menstrual blood as a valuable biofluid for understanding the immunometabolic basis of reproductive pathologies and identifying potential therapeutic targets.

title: Redefining the contribution of retrograde menstruation to endometriosis: single-cell analysis of endometriotic lesions suggests a process more complex than simple autografting
summary:
# Summary

This study redefines endometriosis pathogenesis beyond the simple retrograde menstruation theory through single-cell analysis of endometriotic lesions, revealing that host-derived cells constitute approximately 65% of lesion composition compared to only 35% from transplanted endometrial cells. Single-cell profiling identified diverse immune cell populations including B cells, macrophages, T cells, natural killer cells, and neutrophils, alongside fibroblasts and endothelial cells, indicating a complex tissue with substantial host contribution. Macrophages and neutrophils regulate critical pathological processes including cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and vasculature remodeling through secreted factors, while upregulated genes such as Sf1, Kras, Igf1, and Mmp2 are broadly expressed across immune and stromal cell types to drive endometriosis pathophysiology. Notably, the allograft lesions showed minimal MHC class II expression and absent T cell activation markers or rejection-related cytokines, suggesting immune tolerance of transplanted tissue. The integrated roles of cytokine-mediated immune regulation, particularly elevated chemokines (Ccl2, Ccl4-8, Cxcl12, Cxcl16) and interleukin-1β, establish that endometriosis development involves coordinated host immune infiltration and inflammatory signaling rather than simple autografting of menstrual endometrium.

title: Reproductive tissue–derived stromal cells rescue fertility by coupling follicular activation with endometrial remodeling
summary: Mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) derived from reproductive tissues, particularly endometrial stromal cells (hEndSC) and menstrual stromal cells (hMenSC), restore fertility in chemotherapy-induced premature ovarian failure by coupling follicular activation with endometrial remodeling. In a busulfan/cyclophosphamide-induced premature ovarian insufficiency model, MSC therapy restored fertility in 41–75% of mice, with hEndSC and hMenSC achieving the highest pregnancy rates (75%) and anti-Müllerian hormone recovery. At the ovarian level, MSCs upregulate genes essential for follicle development including Amh, Gdf3, Gja1, and Zp1, while activating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway to promote follicular activation. Simultaneously in the uterus, MSCs enhance endometrial remodeling by upregulating extracellular matrix components (Col1a1, Col3a1, Ctgf) and proliferation markers (Pcna, Ccnd1, Ki67), restoring tissue architecture compromised by chemotherapy. These reproductive tissue-derived MSCs exert their regenerative effects through paracrine signaling and immune modulation, providing a dual mechanism that addresses both ovarian dysfunction and uterine damage in chemotherapy-treated infertility.

title: Engineering the Human Endometrial–Embryo Interface: Breakthroughs in 3D Uterine Models
summary:
# Summary

Successful embryo implantation and pregnancy depend critically on coordinated endometrial remodeling and the synchronized development of endometrial structures, particularly the luminal epithelium, glands, and stromal cells. Estrogen and progesterone govern this cyclical remodeling by promoting endometrial proliferation and differentiation respectively, while endometrial glands produce essential secretions that facilitate stromal cell decidualization and provide nutrients for fetal development. Dysfunction in stromal cell decidualization is associated with endometriosis, recurrent pregnancy loss, implantation failure, and preeclampsia, highlighting the critical role of endometrial gland function in fertility. Recent advances in three-dimensional endometrial models, including epithelial organoids and assembloid co-cultures with stromal cells, now effectively recapitulate the native endometrium's cellular composition and hormone responsiveness, enabling investigation of implantation failure pathologies and endometriosis-related inflammation. Improved biomaterial scaffolds such as decellularized endometrial extracellular matrix, polyethylene glycol-based systems, and photo-cross-linked hydrogels have enhanced long-term culture capabilities and physiological relevance. These engineered endometrial–embryo interface models represent promising platforms for studying reproductive dysfunction and developing therapeutic interventions for endometriosis and implantation failure.

title: Cycle-Dependent Expression of Immune, Morphogenetic, Apoptotic, and Steroid-Related Markers in the Endometrium of Infertile Women: A Pilot Study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study examined the cycle-dependent expression of key immunological, morphogenetic, apoptotic, and steroid-related markers in the endometrium of infertile women to characterize endometrial dysfunction underlying reproductive failure. The researchers identified dynamic, menstrual cycle-dependent expression patterns for several critical regulators: G-CSF showed mid-cycle elevation and peaks on cycle day 24, bone morphogenetic proteins (BMP-2/4) demonstrated the highest overall positivity with peak expression on cycle day 46, heat shock protein 70 (HSP-70) exhibited pronounced cycle-dependent variability, and pentraxin-3 (PTX-3) expression peaked during mid-cycle days. These markers coordinate hormonal, immune, apoptotic, and stress-response pathways essential for endometrial receptivity and successful implantation; altered temporal coordination of their expression was associated with impaired endometrial function. The findings suggest that dysregulation of these factors—including progesterone resistance, aberrant G-CSF signaling, and dysregulated BMP-4 —contributes to implantation failure, recurrent pregnancy loss, and endometriosis, highlighting how disruption of the delicate hormonal and immune balance impairs endometrial maturation and receptivity in infertile patients.

title: Miscarriage and the Microbiome: Host Genetics, Immunity, and the Reproductive Tract Ecosystem
summary: While sporadic pregnancy loss is predominantly caused by embryonic aneuploidy resulting from maternal age-associated meiotic errors, recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) increasingly involves maternal factors including immune dysregulation and microbiome alterations. The reproductive tract microbiome, particularly Lactobacillus-dominant vaginal and endometrial communities, plays a critical role in implantation, placental development, and immune tolerance, with dysbiosis associated with increased inflammation and reduced endometrial receptivity. Successful pregnancy requires precise maternal-fetal immunological tolerance maintained by decidual immune cells: natural killer cells facilitate trophoblast invasion and spiral artery remodeling, decidual macrophages promote tissue remodeling and anti-inflammatory responses, and regulatory T cells suppress excessive inflammation and effector T cells. Women with RPL exhibit characteristic immune dysfunction including enhanced NK cell cytotoxicity, elevated pro-inflammatory effector T-cell populations, reduced regulatory T cell frequencies, and elevated Th1/Th17-to-Treg ratios, creating a pro-inflammatory environment that jeopardizes implantation. Emerging evidence reveals that microbial metabolites regulate immune tolerance and endometrial gene expression through epigenetic modifications, with host genetic background and immune competence critically modulating the microbiome's influence on miscarriage risk. This integrated model reframes miscarriage as resulting from disrupted host-microbe crosstalk rather than isolated pathology, highlighting the interconnection between genetics, immunity, and the reproductive ecosystem.

title: Primary Endometrial Lymphomas: A Systematic Review
summary: Primary endometrial lymphomas are exceedingly rare malignancies arising in the endometrium, characterized by diverse immunophenotypes reflecting their origin from dysregulated immune cells normally present in the endometrial microenvironment. The normal endometrium maintains immune homeostasis through specialized populations including CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes, uterine natural killer cells essential for pregnancy establishment, macrophages supporting tissue repair and angiogenesis, and dendritic cells mediating antigen presentation.
