Primary endometrial marginal zone lymphomas, the most common subtype, express B-cell markers (CD19, CD20, CD22, CD79a) while lacking CD5, CD10, and CD23, typically affecting postmenopausal women. Other rare subtypes include diffuse large B-cell lymphomas responsive to R-CHOP chemotherapy, follicular lymphomas harboring characteristic t(14;18)/IGH-BCL2 translocations, extranodal NK/T-cell lymphomas associated with Epstein-Barr virus, and peripheral T-cell lymphomas, each with distinct immunophenotypic profiles. These malignancies commonly present with abnormal vaginal bleeding and may arise in association with endometrial polyps, highlighting the importance of recognizing these entities to facilitate timely diagnosis and appropriate treatment.

title: Potential Role of Mast Cells in Intervertebral Disc Ageing, Herniation Resolution, and Degeneration: Evidence and Lessons Learned from Studies of Mast Cells in Other Connective Tissues
summary: This review examines the emerging role of mast cells in intervertebral disc (IVD) pathology, drawing parallels from their involvement in other connective tissues. Mast cells, bone marrow-derived immune cells typically associated with allergies and asthma, are found elevated in degenerative IVDs and contain preformed mediators including histamine, tryptase, chymase, and proteinases (MMP-3, HTRA1) that can promote inflammatory and degradative processes. Evidence from ligaments, tendons, and skin wound healing demonstrates that mast cells exacerbate tissue damage through dysregulated inflammation and aberrant healing, roles that appear conserved in IVD degeneration where notochordal cells die and the nucleus pulposus transforms from gelatinous to fibrocartilaginous tissue with aging. Notably, mast cell stabilizers such as ketotifen have shown therapeutic promise in preventing pathological scarring and contractures in other tissues, suggesting similar interventions may benefit IVD degeneration-related conditions like low back pain and sciatica. The review also notes that mast cells may participate in additional connective tissue disorders including endometriosis and multiple sclerosis, indicating a broader pathogenic role. These findings support investigating mast cell stabilization as a potential treatment strategy for IVD-related clinical conditions.

title: Female Reproductive Tract Organ-on-Chips: Modeling Barrier Function and Drug Transport
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the female reproductive tract as a dynamic multilayered barrier system critical for drug transport and reproductive health. The vagina, cervix, endometrium, and placenta employ coordinated mechanisms including tight junction proteins (claudins, occludins, ZO-1), cervicovaginal mucus composed of mucin 5B, and a protective Lactobacillus-dominated microbiota that maintains acidic pH and inhibits pathogen colonization. Dysbiosis disrupts this barrier through inflammation, oxidative stress, and altered pH, potentially impairing drug delivery and contributing to conditions like endometriosis, which causes pelvic pain and infertility through dysregulated inflammatory responses that disrupt epithelial tight junctions. Hormonal regulation through estradiol and progesterone modulates epithelial stratification, mucus properties, and transporter expression across the menstrual cycle, with progesterone enhancing drug penetration during the secretory phase. The placental syncytiotrophoblast expresses ATP-dependent efflux transporters (P-glycoprotein, BCRP, MRPs) that restrict fetal xenobiotic exposure, while passive diffusion dominates transport of small lipophilic compounds. Understanding these barrier functions through organ-on-chip models provides crucial insights for optimizing therapeutic drug delivery and treating reproductive disorders involving barrier dysfunction.

title: Ultradeformable Vesicles for Wound Healing: Ethosomes, Transferosomes, and Transethosomes in Topical Drug Delivery
summary:
# Summary

Wound healing is a dynamic biological process involving skin's epidermal and dermal layers, where keratinocytes and fibroblasts coordinate with growth factors and cytokines across four overlapping phases: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling. The hemostasis phase initiates through platelet activation and aggregation, leading to fibrin clot formation enriched with fibronectin and vitronectin, while hemostatic mediators like endothelin and prostaglandins promote vasoconstriction. The inflammatory phase recruits immune cells (neutrophils, macrophages, lymphocytes) via chemokines like CCL2, with neutrophils providing antimicrobial defense through reactive oxygen species and macrophages secreting growth factors including TGF-α, TGF-β, and VEGF to resolve inflammation through pro-resolving lipid mediators. The proliferative phase involves granulation tissue formation, angiogenesis driven by endothelial cells and pericytes through VEGF and FGF signaling, and re-epithelialization coordinated by TGF-β signaling. Finally, the remodeling phase involves collagen deposition and extracellular matrix turnover regulated by myofibroblasts and matrix metalloproteinases, with dysregulation of these mechanisms contributing to chronic wounds characterized by elevated IL-1β and TNF-α.

title: Beyond traditional models: microfluidic technologies for engineering the human placenta in vitro
summary:
# Summary

The human placenta is a highly specialized organ that functions as the critical interface between maternal and fetal circulations, performing essential roles in maternal-fetal exchange, endocrine activity, and immune tolerance. The placenta accomplishes these functions through specialized cellular components including syncytiotrophoblasts and cytotrophoblasts within chorionic villi, extravillous trophoblasts that remodel maternal spiral arteries, and the decidua that anchors the placenta while establishing an immune-privileged environment. Beyond nutrient and gas exchange, the placenta synthesizes key hormones such as human chorionic gonadotropin, human placental lactogen, estrogen, and progesterone, while producing growth factors like VEGF that promote angiogenesis and transferring maternal IgG antibodies for fetal immune protection. Microfluidic placenta-on-chip platforms now enable advanced study of placental barrier function, drug transport, and endocrine signaling, complementing traditional models including trophoblast cell lines, tissue explants, and precision-cut tissue slices to investigate molecular mechanisms regulating trophoblast invasion and differentiation. Research using these models has revealed that dysregulation of placental development contributes to severe pregnancy complications such as preeclampsia and fetal growth restriction, with identified biomarkers including altered matrix metalloproteinase levels and placenta-derived extracellular vesicles that may predict pregnancy disorders.

title: Luminal epithelium remodeling underlies endometrial regeneration during menstruation and pregnancy
summary:
# Luminal Epithelium Remodeling in Endometrial Regeneration

This study reveals that luminal epithelium undergoes dramatic remodeling as a unified mechanism protecting the endometrium during menstruation and pregnancy. The luminal epithelium serves critical roles in decidualization, embryo attachment, and preventing fibrosis by mediating interactions between epithelial and stromal compartments. Using lineage tracing in Cxcl15-expressing glandular progenitors, the authors demonstrate that luminal epithelial cells are primarily self-renewing during homeostasis but can be regenerated by glandular progenitors following chemical ablation. During menstruation and pregnancy, the luminal epithelium undergoes extensive proliferation and morphological expansion into distinctive "smile" structures that actively resurface denuded stromal tissue concurrent with decidual remodeling and tissue disruption. These findings challenge the prevailing model that glandular progenitors are the primary drivers of endometrial re-epithelialization and instead identify luminal epithelial expansion as the dominant mechanism by which the endometrium achieves resilience and maintains fertility through major reproductive transitions.

title: Endometrial epithelial cells with high ALDH activity control uterine development and regeneration
summary:
# Summary

Aldehyde dehydrogenase 1A1 (ALDH1A1)-expressing cells represent hormone-sensitive endometrial stem cells critical for uterine development, regeneration, and homeostasis. ALDH1A1+ cells localize dynamically to glandular crypts and luminal epithelium in response to estradiol and progesterone, with expression peaking during the diestrus phase and expanding during postnatal development, estrus cycling, and postpartum repair. These high-ALDH-activity (ALDH HI) cells display enhanced clonogenic capacity and express elevated levels of stem cell markers including Lgr5, CDH2, and SUSD2, while showing reduced proliferation signatures and cell cycle genes. ALDH1A1 catalyzes retinoic acid metabolism, a pathway reciprocally regulated with TGFβ signaling; inhibition of TGFβ signaling upregulates retinoic acid signaling in endometrial organoids. Notably, ALDH1A1 is enriched in the basalis endometrium and ectopic endometriotic lesions, and stem cells detected in menstrual effluent may contribute to endometriosis establishment by seeding the peritoneal cavity during retrograde menstruation. Ablation of ALDH1A1+ cells impairs endometrial gland development and FOXA2 expression, demonstrating their essential role in tissue architecture and regenerative function.

title: Human receptive endometrial assembloid for deciphering the implantation window
summary: This study developed a specialized human window-of-implantation (WOI) endometrial assembloid that accurately mimics the receptive endometrium and exhibits hormone responsiveness characteristic of natural endometrial receptivity. The assembloids were generated through self-assembly of endometrial cells induced by signaling molecules including Noggin, EGF, FGF2, WNT-3A, and R-Spondin1, and successfully reproduced key structural features such as pinopodes and cilia while secreting glycogen into the lumen. Estrogen and progesterone treatment upregulated their respective receptor genes and target genes (EGR1, OLFM4, PGR, PAEP), with progesterone signaling centrally driving the WOI phenotype through elevation of the receptivity marker FOXO1. The WOI assembloids displayed characteristics of epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and upregulated critical pathways including mitochondrial energy metabolism, cell adhesion, peptide metabolism, and lipid metabolism, while expressing established receptivity markers IGFBP1, MAOA, and DPP4. This platform provides a physiologically relevant model for deciphering implantation window mechanisms and could facilitate investigation of endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure.

title: Bridging the gap: meta-epidemiological analysis on the clinical translation of stem cell-based therapies in women’s reproductive diseases
summary:
# Summary

This meta-epidemiological analysis evaluates the clinical translation of stem cell-based therapies for women's reproductive diseases, revealing promising mechanistic foundations but significant translational gaps. Stem cells, primarily mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) derived from bone marrow, umbilical cord, and adipose tissue, exert therapeutic effects through self-renewal, multi-lineage differentiation, and paracrine signaling, treating conditions including premature ovarian failure, intrauterine adhesions, endometriosis, and uterine factor infertility. The clinical trial landscape is heavily concentrated in early-phase studies (76.3% Phase 1/2), predominantly single-center trials conducted in China and the USA, with notable geographic variation: Chinese trials focus primarily on ovarian failure and intrauterine adhesions, while American trials concentrate on ovarian cancer. Critical limitations impede clinical translation, including design heterogeneity across trials, inadequate safety endpoint reporting in only 36.8% of randomized controlled trials, short-term follow-up periods creating concerns about long-term tumorigenicity risks, and substantial underrepresentation of high-potential conditions such as endometriosis and thin endometrium. Emerging approaches incorporating exosomes and biomaterial scaffolds show promise, yet the field requires standardized trial protocols, extended safety monitoring, and expansion of early-phase therapies toward Phase 3 trials to achieve meaningful clinical translation.

title: PTN/IGF-2 signaling modulates endometrial decidualization and immune cell trafficking to facilitate pregnancy maintenance
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies pleiotrophin (PTN) as a central dysregulated gene in recurrent implantation failure and recurrent pregnancy loss, operating through the PTN/IGF-2 signaling axis in endometrial stromal cells. PTN is normally expressed in endometrial stromal cells and promotes decidualization by upregulating key markers including IGFBP1, PRL, IGF-2, WNT4, and LIF, while simultaneously establishing a tolerogenic immune microenvironment. In patients with RIF and RPL, PTN expression is significantly downregulated, and this dysregulation contributes to immune dysfunction characterized by altered NK cell activity, reduced M2 macrophages and myeloid-derived suppressor cells, elevated Th1/Th2 ratios, and decreased Treg/Th17 ratios. In vivo knockdown of uterine PTN impaired embryo implantation and increased resorption rates, defects that were partially rescued by IGF-2 supplementation, demonstrating functional importance of this pathway. PTN serves as a highly predictive diagnostic biomarker for both RIF and RPL, with superior diagnostic value compared to other candidate genes, suggesting its potential clinical utility in identifying patients with endometrial dysfunction.

title: Expression and Function of Meflin in Human Endometrial Decidualization
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates Meflin, a glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored protein, as a critical regulator of endometrial decidualization, the progesterone-dependent transformation of endometrial stromal fibroblasts essential for successful implantation and pregnancy. Meflin expression is significantly elevated during the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle and is further upregulated during in vitro decidualization, where it functions by suppressing alpha-smooth muscle actin (αSMA) expression and reducing cellular contractility. Mechanistically, Meflin overexpression attenuates the nuclear translocation of FOXO1, a key transcription factor required for decidualization marker IGFBP1 expression, thereby dampening the full decidualization response despite promoting morphological changes characteristic of decidualized cells. These findings suggest that Meflin plays a paradoxical role in decidualization by promoting the structural remodeling necessary for endometrial receptivity while restraining complete functional differentiation. Since impaired decidualization contributes to infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, and placental dysfunction, dysregulation of Meflin and its downstream signaling could represent a novel mechanism underlying reproductive failure, with implications for understanding endometrial dysfunction in conditions such as endometriosis.

title: Fabrication and characterization of decellularized human endometrial hydrogel as a scaffold for in vitro embryo implantation: An experimental study
summary: This study demonstrates that decellularized human endometrial tissue can be successfully fabricated into a biocompatible hydrogel scaffold that supports embryo implantation in vitro. The decellularized tissue preserves critical extracellular matrix components including collagen, laminin, and fibronectin while removing cellular material, as confirmed by complete DNA reduction and maintained fibrillary stromal structure. When seeded with endometrial mesenchymal stem cells expressing high levels of CD90 and CD44 markers, the recellularized hydrogel promoted cell adhesion, proliferation, and angiogenesis, creating a microenvironment that mimics native endometrial tissue. Mouse blastocysts successfully adhered to, invaded, and penetrated the recellularized hydrogel with enhanced expansion when cultured alongside endometrial mesenchymal cells. These findings establish a novel three-dimensional implantation model combining human endometrial components with embryos, offering potential therapeutic applications for endometrial dysfunction-related infertility conditions such as Asherman's syndrome and endometriosis. This tissue engineering approach represents a significant advancement toward regenerating functional endometrial tissue for reproductive failure management.

title: Engineering human endometrial model systems in reproductive health and disease
summary:
# Summary

The human endometrium is a dynamic tissue essential for fertilization, embryo development, and menstrual cycle regulation, with its remodeling controlled by cyclical fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone. The endometrium comprises multiple cell types including epithelial cells, stromal cells, stem/progenitor cells, and immune cells that undergo coordinated changes during the menstrual cycle and pregnancy, including stromal decidualization that recruits immune cells and promotes angiogenesis. Multiple endometrium-related diseases—including endometriosis, adenomyosis, endometrial cancer, and Asherman syndrome—disrupt normal endometrial function and reproductive health. Recent bioengineering advances such as organs-on-chips, stem cell organoids, hydrogels, and bioprinting technologies enable creation of sophisticated endometrial model systems that can replicate normal pathophysiology and disease states. These engineered models, particularly endometrial epithelial organoids cultured in Matrigel with growth factors like R-spondin1, EGF, and FGF10, facilitate investigation of implantation failures and other reproductive disorders. Such bioengineered platforms represent powerful tools for understanding endometrial dysfunction and developing therapeutic interventions for reproductive disease.

title: Dynamic and Ongoing De Novo L1 Retrotransposition Contributes to Genome Plasticity and Intrapatient Heterogeneity in Ovarian Cancer
summary: High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma is characterized by dysregulation of L1 retrotransposons, which are normally silenced in healthy cells but become highly expressed and active in cancer. De novo L1 retrotransposition occurs dynamically throughout HGSC evolution, beginning early in precursor serous tubal intraepithelial carcinomas and continuing at late disease stages, where it contributes significantly to genomic instability and genome plasticity. L1 activity drives substantial intrapatient tumor heterogeneity, with L1 insertion profiles diverging considerably more than single-nucleotide variants and creating distinct molecular subtypes: tumors with high L1 burden exhibit increased proliferative capacity, while those with low or absent L1 insertions show enrichment of immune response and cell death pathways. The source of somatic retrotransposition in these tumors originates from specific competent L1 elements such as RC-L1_22q12.1 and RC-L1_Xp22.2, and the L1-ORF1p and L1-ORF2p proteins are essential for the copy-and-paste retrotransposition mechanism. Patient-specific differences in L1 burden acquisition appear driven by molecular properties of the progenitor tumor, potentially creating exploitable molecular vulnerabilities through retrotransposition-associated DNA damage and replication stress that could inform therapeutic targeting in HGSC.

title: YWHAZ loss is associated with endometrial dysfunction in proliferative-phase endometriosis
summary: This study identifies YWHAZ (14-3-3 zeta) as a significantly downregulated gene in the eutopic endometrium of women with endometriosis during the proliferative phase, contributing to endometrial dysfunction and infertility. YWHAZ expression is markedly reduced in both epithelial and stromal compartments of endometriotic tissue and is dependent on progesterone receptor (PR) and STAT3 signaling, suggesting YWHAZ acts downstream in the progesterone-signaling pathway. The loss of YWHAZ is functionally linked to progesterone resistance and STAT3 dysregulation—two hallmark pathological features of endometriosis that impair endometrial receptivity and decidualization. During normal pregnancy in mice, YWHAZ expression increases substantially in the decidualized endometrium, indicating its critical role in implantation and uterine function. These findings establish YWHAZ as a previously unrecognized molecular hub connecting progesterone resistance, STAT3-mediated inflammation, and defective endometrial function in endometriosis-associated infertility, potentially opening new therapeutic avenues targeting this pathway.

title: Free apical surface hetero-cellular CDH1 homophilic binding is a major mediator of blastocyst–endometrium interaction in humans
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that E-cadherin (CDH1) expressed on the free apical surface of endometrial epithelial cells mediates blastocyst-endometrium attachment through homophilic binding with CDH1 on the polar trophectoderm. Embryonic signals, particularly human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG), upregulate apical CDH1 expression during the window of implantation, and CDH1 knockdown significantly reduces both adhesion molecule expression and embryo attachment in vitro. Clinical evidence shows that endometrial CDH1 expression is higher in women who achieved live births compared to those with repeated implantation failure, and apical CDH1 localization positively correlates with successful IVF outcomes. The adhesion molecule CTNND1, which stabilizes CDH1 at the membrane, works cooperatively with CDH1 in mediating endometrial receptivity. These findings identify free apical surface CDH1 homophilic binding as a major mechanism of embryo-endometrium interaction and suggest CDH1 expression could serve as a biomarker for predicting implantation success and diagnosing endometrial dysfunction in reproductive failure.

title: Nps‐Expressing Neurons Receive Extensive Input From Auditory Brainstem Nuclei
summary: This study identifies neuropeptide S (NPS)-expressing neurons in the rostral-lateral parabrachial nucleus as integrators of auditory information with homeostatic and emotional processing. These neurons receive extensive input from multiple auditory brainstem structures including the inferior colliculus, cochlear nucleus, superior olivary complex, and ventral nucleus of the lateral lemniscus, as well as from limbic and cortical regions including the insular cortex, auditory cortex, amygdala, and hypothalamus. The NPS system projects prominently to the paraventricular nucleus of the thalamus, bed nucleus of the stria terminalis, and hypothalamus—brain regions critical for circadian function and threat responses. NPS and its receptor NPSR1 regulate multiple physiological processes including locomotion, anxiety, arousal, and food intake, with NPSR1 variants implicated in anxiety disorders, asthma, and endometriosis. The parabrachial nucleus positioning suggests it serves a crucial role in integrating sensory inputs with descending modulatory signals to coordinate behavioral and autonomic responses to environmental challenges. This anatomical organization reveals how auditory-based threat detection can be coordinated with systemic homeostatic adjustments through the NPS neuromodulatory system.

title: Abnormal elevated PANX1 expression hampers endometrial decidualization by upregulating extracellular ATP concentration in patients with recurrent implantation failure
summary: This study identifies abnormally elevated PANX1 (Pannexin1) expression in the endometrium of recurrent implantation failure (RIF) patients as a critical mechanism impairing endometrial decidualization. PANX1, an ATP-permeable channel, functions to release extracellular ATP (eATP), and overexpression of PANX1 sustains pathologically high eATP concentrations that inhibit the decidualization process—the transformation of endometrial stromal cells into secretory cells essential for embryo implantation. In human endometrial stromal cells, PANX1 overexpression reduced expression of decidualization markers prolactin (PRL) and IGFBP-1 while preventing morphological differentiation into polygonal decidual cells, whereas PANX1 knockdown similarly impaired decidualization, indicating a requirement for appropriate PANX1 levels. The research demonstrates that while transient eATP elevation initially promotes decidualization initiation, sustained high eATP concentrations from excessive PANX1 activity prevent the completion of this critical endometrial transformation. These findings establish abnormal PANX1-mediated eATP regulation as a previously unrecognized contributor to RIF pathogenesis and suggest potential therapeutic targeting of this pathway to improve implantation outcomes in assisted reproductive technologies.

title: Milk fat globule EGF and factor V/VIII domain containing (MFGE8) as a novel player in equine endometrial fibrosis
summary: Endometrial fibrosis represents a significant cause of age-related reproductive failure in mares, driven primarily by transforming growth factor beta 1 (TGF-β1)-mediated myofibroblast differentiation and excessive extracellular matrix deposition. TGF-β1 upregulates key profibrotic effectors including connective tissue growth factor (CCN2), transgelin (TAGLN), and multiple growth factors (PDGF, TGFA, AREG, IGFBP3), while simultaneously suppressing protective interferon and complement signaling pathways. In contrast, milk fat globule EGF and factor V/VIII domain containing (MFGE8) exerts potent antifibrotic effects through multiple mechanisms, including promotion of apoptotic cell clearance and collagen uptake by macrophages, reduction of profibrotic leukemia inhibitory factor expression, and upregulation of protective phosphatase and tensin homolog signaling. Histological analysis reveals elevated expression of CCN2 (73%), TAGLN (86%), and MFGE8 (68%) in fibrotic endometrial glands, with localization to epithelial cells, stromal compartments, blood vessels, and periglandular regions. Single-cell transcriptomic analysis confirms that TGF-β1 exposure activates collagen biosynthesis and fibrillogenesis pathways while suppressing immune-regulatory signaling. These findings establish MFGE8 as a novel therapeutic target capable of counteracting TGF-β1-driven endometrial fibrosis and associated fertility decline.

title: Innate Immunity and Microbial Recognition in Reproduction: From Barrier Defense to Maternal–Fetal Tolerance
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how innate immune pattern-recognition receptors—including Toll-like receptors, NOD-like receptors, RIG-I-like receptors, and cGAS-STING pathways—regulate reproductive function from gamete maturation through pregnancy. These receptors are broadly expressed across reproductive tissues by leukocytes, epithelial cells, stromal cells, and trophoblasts, where they detect microbial and danger signals and initiate inflammatory responses through NF-κB and MAPK signaling. While physiological inflammation is essential for tissue remodeling and normal reproduction, excessive or unresolved innate activation contributes to infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, preeclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and preterm birth. Sex hormones (estradiol, progesterone, and androgens) and commensal communities (particularly Lactobacillus-dominant vaginal microbiota) modulate barrier integrity and immune tone through effects on epithelial function, antimicrobial peptide production, and chemokine gradients. The review highlights that microbial ligands can directly impair sperm function and that women generally mount stronger innate responses than men, underscoring how the balance between protective immunity and immune tolerance is critical for reproductive success.

title: Development of a three-dimensional culture model of endometrium to study embryo-uterine interactions
journal: The Journal of Reproduction and Development
summary:
# Summary

Three-dimensional culture systems have emerged as powerful tools for investigating uterine physiology and embryo-endometrial interactions, offering significant advantages over traditional two-dimensional approaches. Gel-based cultures utilizing natural extracellular matrices such as collagen and Matrigel enable endometrial epithelial and stromal cells to organize into physiologically relevant structures, including gland-like formations and polarized organoids that recapitulate key aspects of in vivo tissue architecture. Spheroid cultures are particularly valuable for modeling implantation biology and endometriosis, as they recapitulate cell polarity, paracrine signaling, and tissue remodeling, with progesterone promoting embryo attachment while estradiol prevents it. Type-I interferon and progesterone regulate critical genes including FGF13 and thioredoxin and promote matrix metalloproteinase production, processes essential for interferon-induced matrix remodeling in engineered hetero-spheroids. Stromal cells cultured in three-dimensional matrices undergo hormone-induced decidualization marked by increased prolactin and IGFBP-1 expression, demonstrating the model's capacity to recapitulate endometrial receptivity. Advanced platforms including endometrial organoids and microfluidic endometrium-on-a-chip systems further enhance physiological relevance, enabling long-term culture and hormonal responsiveness studies critical for understanding reproductive failure and endometriosis.

title: HDAC3 Regulates Transcriptional Networks Governing Decidualization
summary: HDAC3 is a critical epigenetic regulator of decidualization and uterine receptivity, functioning as both a transcriptional repressor and coactivator through its interaction with NCoR1/2 complexes. Loss of HDAC3 in the endometrium causes female infertility by impairing decidualization, cytoskeletal remodeling, metabolic signaling, and oxidative stress responses, ultimately leading to implantation failure. HDAC3 ablation dysregulates multiple signaling pathways essential for decidualization, including RHOA, AMPK-NOTCH1-HEY1, and oxidative stress-induced senescence cascades, while suppressing key decidualization markers such as Wnt4 and Bmp2. Mechanistically, loss of HDAC3 promotes aberrant transcriptional activation of collagen genes (Col1a1 and Col1a2), disrupting extracellular matrix remodeling and stromal cell transformation. Notably, HDAC3 expression is markedly reduced in the eutopic endometrium of infertile women with endometriosis, establishing a link between aberrant HDAC3 regulation and endometriosis-associated infertility. These findings highlight HDAC3 as a central regulator orchestrating the epigenetic networks necessary for endometrial remodeling and successful pregnancy establishment.
